data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Add first-class dependency tracking (blocks/blockedBy + typed edges like discovered-from) and use it to power `worklog ready` (unblocked work detection).

Implementation:
- Persist dependency edges between items (direction + type).
- CLI/API: `worklog dep add|rm|list` with edge types and output formats.
- `worklog ready` returns items with no unresolved blocking deps (optionally filtered by status/assignee/tags).

Acceptance criteria:
- Can add/remove/list dependencies and see them on items.
- `worklog ready` excludes items blocked by open dependencies and includes unblocked items.
- Supports at least `blocks` and `discovered-from` edge types.
data.title: Feature: Dependency tracking + ready

data.description:
Add `worklog close` to mirror `bd close`, supporting single and multiple ids and a stored close reason.

Implementation:
- CLI: `worklog close <id...> [--reason "..."]`.
- Store close reason on the item (field) or as an immutable comment/event.
- Set status to `completed` and update timestamps consistently.

Acceptance criteria:
- Closing one or many ids marks each item `completed`.
- `--reason` is persisted and visible via `wl show`.
- Command reports per-id success/failure and returns non-zero if any close fails.
data.title: Feature: worklog close (single + multi)

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Add template support to mirror `bd create --from-template` workflows.

Implementation:
- Define built-in templates (at least: bug, feature, epic) with default fields/tags.
- CLI: `worklog template list` and `worklog template show <name>`.
- Extend `worklog create` with `--template <name>` to prefill fields.

Acceptance criteria:
- `worklog template list` shows available templates.
- `worklog template show <name>` renders the resolved template.
- `worklog create --template <name>` creates an item with the template defaults applied.
data.title: Feature: Templates (list/show + create --template)

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Add `worklog onboard` to generate repo-local instructions/config for consistent agent + contributor setup (optionally Copilot instructions).

Implementation:
- Generate a standard docs/config bundle (paths to be defined) describing how to use worklog in this repo.
- Optionally emit GitHub Copilot/agent instruction files when requested.
- Avoid overwriting existing files unless `--force` is provided.

Acceptance criteria:
- Running `worklog onboard` produces a repeatable set of repo-local onboarding artifacts.
- Re-running is idempotent (no changes) unless `--force`.
- Output clearly states what was created/updated and where.
data.title: Feature: worklog onboard

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Align priorities with workflow expectations by supporting numeric P0‚ÄìP4 (or a compatibility layer mapping them to existing enums).

Implementation:
- Allow creating/updating items with priority `P0..P4` via CLI flags and API.
- Define a canonical internal representation and a mapping to existing `critical/high/medium/low` if needed.
- Ensure list/show output can display the numeric form consistently.

Acceptance criteria:
- User can set priority using `P0..P4` and retrieve it via `wl show`/`wl list`.
- Backward compatible: existing priority values still work.
- Sorting/filtering by priority works for both representations.
data.title: Feature: Priority compatibility (P0-P4)

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Add ‚Äúplan/insights/diff‚Äù style commands (or API endpoints) analogous to bv robot outputs: critical path, cycles, and ‚Äúwhat changed since ref/date‚Äù.

Implementation:
- Define CLI surface (e.g. `worklog insights critical-path|cycles|diff`).
- Implement graph analysis for critical path/cycle detection using dependency graph.
- Implement diff by git ref and/or timestamp (created/updated/closed).

Acceptance criteria:
- Can output critical path and detected cycles for a given scope.
- Can list items changed since a given date and/or git ref.
- Supports `--json` output for automation.
data.title: Feature: plan/insights/diff style commands

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Add branch-per-item support: `worklog branch <id>` creates/switches to a branch named with the id (optionally records branch on item).

Implementation:
- Integrate with git to create/switch branches safely (handle existing branch).
- Use a deterministic branch naming convention (e.g. `wl/<id>-<slug>`).
- Optionally persist branch name on the work item.

Acceptance criteria:
- `worklog branch <id>` switches to an existing branch or creates one if missing.
- Branch name is predictable and collision-safe.
- If configured, the item records the branch name and it shows up in `wl show`.
data.title: Feature: Branch-per-item (worklog branch <id>)

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Add ‚Äúlanding the plane‚Äù automation: `worklog land` runs configurable quality gates, ensures worklog/issue data sync, and verifies git push success.

Implementation:
- Define a configurable pipeline (config file + defaults).
- Run gates (tests/lint/build/etc), validate clean working tree, push current branch.
- Ensure worklog item status/metadata is consistent before/after (e.g. requires linked item id).

Acceptance criteria:
- `worklog land` runs gates in order and fails fast with actionable output.
- Refuses to proceed on dirty worktree unless `--force` (or equivalent).
- Confirms remote push succeeded and reports what ran.
data.title: Feature: worklog land (quality gates + sync)

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

Improve automation ergonomics: add `--sort/--limit/--offset`, `--fields`, NDJSON/table output; plus batch operations like `worklog bulk update --where ...`.

Implementation:
- Extend list APIs to support sorting/pagination.
- Add output formatters: table, JSON, NDJSON; add `--fields` projection.
- Add `worklog bulk update` with filter expression and `--dry-run`.

Acceptance criteria:
- `wl list` supports `--sort`, `--limit`, `--offset` and returns stable results.
- Output can be selected as table/JSON/NDJSON and field-projected.
- `worklog bulk update --where ... --dry-run` reports affected items; without dry-run updates them.
data.title: Feature: Automation ergonomics (sort/limit/fields/bulk)

data.description: Feature: Full-text search (SQLite FTS) ‚Äî WL-0MKRPG61W1NKGY78 Brief summary - Add an FTS5-backed full-text search index and a `worklog search` CLI so contributors can run fast, ranked queries (title, description, comments, tags) with immediate index visibility after writes. Problem statement - Developers and maintainers need fast, relevant full-text search over work items (title, description, comments, tags) so they can find and triage issues reliably at scale. Current listing and filtering are limited and do not support relevance ranking, snippets, or fast text queries. Users - Repository contributors and maintainers who search work items to triage, plan, and debug. Example user stories: - As a contributor, I want to run `worklog search "database corruption"` and see the most relevant items (with snippets) so I can find prior discussions quickly. - As a release engineer, I want to filter search results by `--status open --tags cli` and export JSON for automation so I can programmatically build reports. - As a maintainer, I want the search index to reflect writes immediately so newly created/updated items are discoverable in the next command. Chosen approach (capture fidelity) - SQLite FTS5 is the target engine (user confirmed). Index all text fields plus tags (title, description, comments, tags, other text fields). CLI defaults: human-friendly output with snippets and filters; `--json` for machine consumption. Success criteria - Search returns ranked, relevant results for common queries (top-10 relevance) with snippet highlights matching query terms. - Index updates synchronously on write: create/update/delete operations are visible to subsequent searches within 1 second. - CLI usability: `worklog search ` supports `--status`, `--parent`, `--tags`, `--json`, `--limit` and returns human-friendly output by default; `--json` returns structured results. - Performance: median query latency <100ms on datasets up to ~5,000 items in CI/dev environment; include a small benchmark job in CI. - Tests & CI: unit tests for indexing/querying, integration fixtures covering index correctness and consistency across create/update/delete, and a perf benchmark. Constraints - Requires SQLite with FTS5 enabled; the CLI must detect and fail fast with a clear message if FTS5 is unavailable. - Keep index consistent with the canonical store (SQLite DB or `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` import flow). Prefer DB-backed storage and transactional updates. - Implement synchronous updates with minimal write latency (FTS virtual table + triggers or application-managed transactions that update FTS in the same transaction). Existing state & traceability - Work item: WL-0MKRPG61W1NKGY78 (Feature: Full-text search). - Parent epic: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ ‚Äî Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands. - Related infra: WL-0MKRRZ2DN0898F / WL-0MKRSO1KD1NWWYBP ‚Äî Persist Worklog DB across executions; these inform DB choice/lifecycle. - Source data: `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` ‚Äî use for initial backfill and test fixtures. - Docs: update `CLI.md` with `worklog search` usage and examples. Desired change (high level) - Add FTS5 virtual table `worklog_fts` that indexes title, description, comment bodies, tags and other text fields; include per-document metadata (work item id, status, parentId) to support filtering and ranking. - Keep index in sync synchronously on write via triggers or application-managed transactions; provide `--rebuild-index` admin flag to backfill or rebuild. - Implement `worklog search` supporting: phrase queries, prefix search, bm25 ranking, snippet extraction, filters `--status/--parent/--tags`, `--limit`, and `--json` output. - Provide migration/bootstrap: create FTS table on first run and backfill from `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` or current DB. Example SQL (developer handoff) ``` -- Create a simple FTS5 table tying back to the canonical items table CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE IF NOT EXISTS worklog_fts USING fts5( title, description, comments, tags, itemId UNINDEXED, status UNINDEXED, parentId UNINDEXED, tokenize = 'porter' ); -- Example ranked query with snippet SELECT itemId, bm25(worklog_fts) AS rank, snippet(worklog_fts, '', '', '...', -1, 64) AS snippet FROM worklog_fts WHERE worklog_fts MATCH '"database corruption" OR database*' ORDER BY rank LIMIT 10; ``` Related work (links/ids) - WL-0MKRPG61W1NKGY78 ‚Äî this item. - WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ ‚Äî parent epic. - WL-0MKRRZ2DN0898F / WL-0MKRSO1KD1NWWYBP ‚Äî DB persistence work (relevant). - `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` ‚Äî backfill and fixtures. - `CLI.md` ‚Äî update after implementation. Risks & mitigations - FTS5 unavailable in some SQLite builds ‚Äî Mitigation: detect early, emit a clear error message and document runtime requirements; create a follow-up fallback task if adoption is required. - Noisy/irrelevant results ‚Äî Mitigation: tune tokenization, add field weighting, provide examples in docs, and expose `--limit` and filter flags for deterministic results. - Index corruption or schema drift during upgrades ‚Äî Mitigation: provide `--rebuild-index`, export/backups before migration, and include migration scripts in the PR. - Write latency on very large datasets ‚Äî Mitigation: measure with CI benchmark; keep transactional updates fast; evaluate async updates as follow-up if necessary. Polish & handoff - Update `CLI.md` with usage examples and the SQL snippet above. Include a short dev guide in the PR describing bootstrap steps, `--rebuild-index`, and how to run the perf benchmark. - Final one-line headline for work item body: "FTS5-backed full-text search + `worklog search` CLI: fast, ranked queries over title, description, comments and tags with synchronous indexing." Suggested next steps (conservative) 1) Merge this intake draft into the work item description (after your approval). 2) Create a small spike branch: add FTS5 table + backfill script + prototype `worklog search --json` and run local perf tests. 3) If FTS5 is absent in some environments, open a follow-up work item to add an application-level fallback.
data.title: Full-text search

data.description:
# Worklog doctor intake brief (WL-0MKRPG64S04PL1A6)

## Problem statement
Worklog lacks a reliable, non-destructive diagnostics command to evaluate the integrity and consistency of worklog data during regular use. We need `worklog doctor` to report integrity issues, apply safe workflow-alignment fixes, and interactively handle non-safe fixes without tying results to build/CI failure semantics.

## Users
- Maintainers and SREs who need to validate worklog data health during routine operations.
- Developers and agents who need clear diagnostics and guided remediation when issues are found.

### Example user stories
- As a maintainer, I want `worklog doctor` to summarize integrity problems and guide fixes so the worklog remains consistent during daily use.
- As a developer, I want `worklog doctor --fix` to apply safe workflow-alignment fixes automatically and prompt me on non-safe changes.

## Success criteria
- `worklog doctor` reports integrity findings for graph integrity and status/stage validation.
- Human output is an informative summary; `--json` outputs a single array of detailed findings.
- `--fix` applies safe fixes automatically and prompts interactively for non-safe fixes.
- Safe fixes are limited to workflow-alignment changes; non-safe fixes are never applied without user confirmation.
- The command is usable during regular operations and does not imply CI/build failure behavior.

## Suggested next step
Proceed to implementation planning for `worklog doctor` with `--fix` support, aligning graph integrity and status/stage validation checks to the documented rules and data stores.

## Constraints
- No `--fail-on` or build/CI failure semantics.
- No severity labels on findings.
- Fixing is interactive for non-safe changes.
- Operate on the canonical worklog datastore (DB) and existing JSONL exports.

## Existing state
- Work items and dependency edges are persisted in `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` and the SQLite DB.
- Status/stage rules and known validation gaps are documented in `docs/validation/status-stage-inventory.md`.

## Desired change
- Implement `worklog doctor [--json] [--fix]`.
- Detect graph integrity issues (cycles, missing parents, dangling dependency edges, orphaned non-epic items, malformed/duplicate ids if format rules exist).
- Detect status/stage values that violate config-driven rules.
- Produce an informative human summary by default; `--json` emits a single array of detailed findings.
- When `--fix` is enabled:
  - Apply safe fixes automatically (workflow-alignment changes).
  - Prompt interactively for non-safe fixes (anything not easily reversible).
  - Report any declined non-safe fixes as remaining findings.

## Related work
- Doctor: detect missing dep references (WL-0ML4PH4EQ1XODFM0) ‚Äî child item for dangling dependency checks.
- Doctor: validate status/stage values from config (WL-0MLE6WJUY0C5RRVX) ‚Äî child item for status/stage validation.
- Add wl dep command (WL-0ML2VPUOT1IMU5US) ‚Äî dependency edges and semantics.
data.title: wl doctor

data.description:
Parent: WL-0MKRJK13H1VCHLPZ - Epic: Add bd-equivalent workflow commands

If running as a shared service, add auth + audit trail (actor on writes) to support handoffs and accountability.

Implementation:
- Add authentication/authorization model for API writes (token/session based).
- Record audit events for mutations (who/when/what) with immutable storage.
- Expose audit trail via API and optionally CLI (read-only).

Acceptance criteria:
- All write operations record actor identity and timestamp in an audit log.
- Unauthorized requests are rejected with clear errors.
- Audit log can be queried for an item and shows a complete history of changes.
data.title: Feature: Auth + audit trail (shared service)

When outputting the tree view of an issue make id grey. Also remove the brackets around the ID and instead put it after a hyphen.. So instead of 

`Feature: worklog onboard (WL-0MKRPG5L91BQBXK2)`

We will have:

data.description:
The goal is for us to be able to keep issues in sync across multiple instances of worklog. We currently have the ability to export and import, but there is currently no version control features.

When we do a git pull the latest jsonl file will be retrieved but it currently will not be imported into the database. Furthermore, there may be convlicts that need to be resolved.

Create a sync command that will pull the latest file from git (only the jsonl file), merge the content - including resolving conflicts (use the updatedAt to indicate the most recent data that should take precendence). Export the data and push the latest back to git.

Generate the best possible algorithm to make this happen, do not feel constrained by the detail of my request here, the goal is to ensure that when the sync command is run the local database has all the latest remote updates and the current local updates.
data.title: Issue syncing

data.description: Change all commands to output human readable content by default, while machine readable JSON content is returned if the --json flag is present.
data.title: Add a --json flag

data.description:
Problem
- Worklog currently uses a Map-backed in-memory database (src/database.ts) and relies on importing/exporting .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl per CLI invocation (src/cli.ts) and at API server startup (src/index.ts).
- There is no persistent database process/state that survives between separate CLI runs, and the ‚Äúsource of truth‚Äù/refresh behavior is ad-hoc.
- We want a real persistent DB (on disk) so the database state exists independently of a single process, and a clear refresh rule: if the local JSONL file is newer than what the DB has, reload/refresh the DB from JSONL.
Goal
- Replace the ‚Äúin-memory Map only‚Äù approach with a disk-backed database that persists between command executions.
- Ensure the DB automatically refreshes from .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl when that file is newer than the DB‚Äôs current state.
Proposed behavior
- On CLI start and API server start:
  - Open/connect to the persistent DB stored on disk (location configurable; default under .worklog/).
  - Determine staleness:
    - Compare JSONL mtime vs DB ‚Äúlast imported from JSONL‚Äù timestamp (or DB file mtime if that‚Äôs the chosen proxy).
  - If JSONL is newer:
    - Re-import from JSONL into the DB (rebuild items/comments).
    - Update DB metadata to record the import time + source file path + JSONL mtime/hash (so future comparisons are reliable).
- On writes (create/update/delete/comment ops):
  - Persist to DB immediately.
  - Decide and document the new ‚Äúsource of truth‚Äù model:
    - Option A: DB is source of truth; JSONL is an export artifact (write JSONL on demand or on a schedule).
    - Option B: Keep writing JSONL for Git workflows, but DB remains authoritative for runtime and only refreshes from JSONL when JSONL is newer (e.g., after a git pull).
Scope / tasks
- Add a persistent DB implementation (likely SQLite) and a small DB metadata table, e.g.:
  - meta(key TEXT PRIMARY KEY, value TEXT) with keys like lastJsonlImportMtimeMs, lastJsonlImportAt, schemaVersion.
- Refactor database layer:
  - Introduce an interface (e.g. WorklogStore) implemented by the new persistent DB.
  - Keep the current Map DB as a fallback/dev option if desired, but default to persistent.
- Update CLI (src/cli.ts):
  - Replace loadData()/saveData() logic with ‚Äúopen DB; maybe refresh from JSONL; operate on DB; optionally export JSONL‚Äù.
  - Ensure multi-project prefix behavior still works.
- Update API server startup (src/index.ts):
  - Same refresh logic on boot.
- Refresh logic details:
  - Use fs.statSync(jsonlPath).mtimeMs (or async equivalent).
  - Compare against stored DB metadata value; if JSONL missing, do nothing.
  - If DB is empty/uninitialized and JSONL exists, import.
- Add migration/versioning:
  - DB schema version stored in metadata; handle upgrades safely.
- Tests / acceptance checks
  - Running worklog create then a separate worklog list shows the created item without relying on in-process state.
  - If .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl is modified externally (simulate git pull), the next CLI/API startup refreshes DB and reflects the updated data.
  - If DB has newer data than JSONL, it does not overwrite DB (unless explicitly commanded); behavior is documented.
Acceptance criteria
- DB persists across separate CLI executions (verified by creating an item, exiting, re-running list/show).
- On startup (CLI + API), if .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl is newer than DB state, DB is refreshed from JSONL automatically.
- No data loss when switching from current JSONL-only workflow: existing .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl is imported into the new DB on first run.
- Clear documented ‚Äúsource of truth‚Äù and when JSONL is written/updated.
Notes / implementation preference
- SQLite is a good default (single file on disk, easy distribution, supports concurrency better than ad-hoc JSON).
- Keep .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl for Git-friendly sharing, but treat it as an import/export boundary rather than the primary store.
data.title: Persist Worklog DB across executions; refresh from JSONL when newer

data.description: Currently the worklog-data.jsonl file defaults to being in the root of the project, it should default to the .worklog folder
data.title: Move the default location for the jsonl file

data.description:
Sync is not working. Here's how I have been testing:

- Clone repo into two separate directories
- Add an issue to the first directory with the title "First"
- Add an issue to the second directory with the title "Second"
- Run sync in the first directory
- Run sync in the second directory

What I expect is for the "First" and "Second" issues to be in both versions of the application. However, each version only has the issue created in that directory.
data.title: Sync not working

data.description:
Users should be able to download a release artifact, extract it, place it on their PATH, and run worklog without installing Node or running npm install. We‚Äôll implement a per-platform ‚Äúfolder distribution‚Äù that bundles:
- a small worklog launcher (or worklog.exe)
- a Node.js runtime
- the compiled Worklog CLI (dist/)
- runtime dependencies, including the native better-sqlite3 addon for that platform
This is explicitly not a single-file executable; it‚Äôs a self-contained directory that can be put on PATH.
Goals
- worklog runs on a clean machine with no Node/npm installed.
- Distributions are produced for at least: linux-x64, darwin-arm64, darwin-x64, win-x64 (expand as needed).
- Release artifact layout is stable and documented.
- CLI behavior is unchanged (same commands/options/output).
Non-goals
- Single-file executable.
- Replacing better-sqlite3.
- Building from source on user machines.
Implementation plan
1) Choose packaging approach (recommended)
- Bundle Node runtime + app into a folder:
  - node (or node.exe)
  - worklog launcher script/binary that executes the bundled node:
    - linux/macos: ./node ./dist/cli.js "$@"
    - windows: worklog.cmd or worklog.exe shim calling node.exe dist\cli.js %*
  - dist/ output from tsc
  - node_modules/ containing production deps (incl. better-sqlite3 compiled binary)
2) Add packaging scripts
- Add npm run build (already exists) + new scripts such as:
  - npm run dist:prep to create a clean staging directory
  - npm run dist:bundle to copy dist/, package.json, and install production deps into staging
  - npm run dist:node:<platform> to download/extract the correct Node runtime into staging (or use a build action to fetch it)
  - npm run dist:archive to produce .tar.gz / .zip per platform
- Ensure we install prod-only deps into staging (no devDependencies).
3) Handle native module compatibility (better-sqlite3)
- Build artifacts must be produced on each target OS/arch (or use CI runners per platform) so better-sqlite3‚Äôs .node binary matches the target.
- Verify that the bundled node version matches the ABI expected by the built better-sqlite3 binary (i.e., build/install using the same Node major version you bundle).
4) Entrypoint and pathing
- Ensure the CLI entry works when executed from anywhere:
  - Use process.execPath / import.meta.url-relative paths so it finds dist/ and bundled resources regardless of current working directory.
- Confirm that .worklog/ data paths remain in the current project directory (unchanged).
5) Versioning and update UX
- Add worklog --version (already) and optionally a worklog version --json output for tooling (optional).
- Document upgrade procedure: replace the folder on PATH with a newer version.
6) CI build + GitHub releases
- Add GitHub Actions workflow:
  - Matrix: ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest (and macos for both x64/arm64 as appropriate)
  - Steps: checkout, install, build, stage folder, run smoke tests, archive, upload artifacts
  - On tag push: create GitHub Release and attach artifacts
7) Smoke tests (required)
- On each platform artifact in CI:
  - Extract artifact
  - Run ./worklog --help
  - Run ./worklog --version
  - Optionally run a minimal command that doesn‚Äôt require repo init (e.g., worklog status should error cleanly) to confirm runtime works.
8) Documentation
- Update README.md with:
  - Download links / artifact names
  - Install instructions per OS (PATH update examples)
  - Notes about per-platform downloads
  - Troubleshooting: macOS Gatekeeper/quarantine, Linux executable bit, Windows SmartScreen
Acceptance criteria
- A user can:
  - download the correct artifact for their OS/arch
  - extract it
  - add the extracted directory to PATH
  - run worklog --help successfully with no Node/npm installed
- CI produces and uploads artifacts for the supported platforms on every release tag.
- Artifacts include better-sqlite3 correctly (no missing native module errors).
Notes / risks
- macOS: may require signing/notarization for best UX; at minimum document Gatekeeper prompts.
- Windows: consider providing both worklog.cmd shim and (optional) an .exe shim.
- Size: bundling Node increases artifact size; this is expected for ‚Äúno Node required‚Äù.
data.title: Ship Standalone ‚ÄúFolder Binary‚Äù Distribution (No npm install) for Worklog CLI

data.description: When syncing we need more detail on conflict resolution. It should record what the conflicting fields were and highlight which was chosen. Green for chosen, red for lost.
data.title: More detail on conflict resolution

data.description:
Provide a status command that will do the following:

1. Ensure that the Worklog system has been initialized on the local system. This means that `init` has been run. When `init` is run it should write a gitignored semaphore to .worklog that indicates initialization is complete. This should indicate the version number that did the initialization
2. Provide a summary output about the number of issues and comments in the database
data.title: status command

data.description: Any action taken via the CLI or API that changes the data should trigger an export of the data to JSONL. There are performance issues with doing this, design a performant approach and allow this feature to be runed off with a setting in config.yaml
data.title: Export the data after every action that changes something

data.description: At present we have to run using ` npm run cli --`, this is fine for dev/test but we need the CLI to be installable. The CLI command should be `worklog` with an alias of `wl`
data.title: Add an install

data.description: If we run init a second time it should not force the user to enter details, like the project name and prefix, a second time. Instead it should output the current settings and ask if the user wants to change them.
data.title: init should be idempotent

data.description:
As work is undertaken we will need to record comments against work items. Each work item will have 0..n comments, each comment will have a single work item parent.

Comments will need the following fields:

- author - the name of the author of the comment (freeform string)
- comment - the text of the comment, in markdown format
- createdAt - the time the comment was created
- references - an array of references in the form of work item IDs, relative filepaths from the root of the current project, or URLs
data.title: Add comments

data.description:
The version of config.yaml that is in the public repo should be renamed to config.defaults.yaml and should be used to get values if there is no value in the local config.yaml file. 

Documentation should instruct users to override values found in config.defaults.yaml by adding them to the local config.yaml.

config.yaml should not be in the public repo
data.title: Add .worklog/.config.defaults.yaml

data.description:
We want the Worklog CLI (dist/cli.js, implemented in src/cli.ts) to support a pluggable command architecture where new commands can be added by dropping a compiled ESM plugin file (.js / .mjs) into a known directory, with no Worklog rebuild required. On the next CLI invocation, the new command should appear automatically.
This issue also migrates all existing built-in commands out of src/cli.ts into external command modules (shipped with the package), using the same plugin interface, and fully documents the feature.
Goals
- Pluggable commands discovered at runtime (from disk) and registered into commander.
- All existing commands ported to external command modules (no giant monolith src/cli.ts).
- Clear docs: how to write, build, install, and troubleshoot plugins.
Non-goals
- Loading TypeScript plugins directly.
- Hot-reloading commands within a single long-running CLI session (CLI restart is sufficient).
Proposed design
- Define a plugin contract:
  - Each plugin is an ESM module exporting default as register(ctx): void (or named register).
  - register receives:
    - program: Command (commander instance)
    - ctx shared utilities (output helpers, config/db accessors, version, paths)
- Command module shape (example):
  - export default function register({ program, ctx }) { program.command('foo')... }
- Built-in commands:
  - Move each top-level command (and subcommand groups like comment) into its own module under src/commands/.
  - src/cli.ts becomes a thin bootstrap:
    - create program, global options, shared ctx
    - register built-in commands by importing ./commands/*.js
    - load external plugins from plugin dir and register them
- External plugin discovery:
  - Default plugin directory: .worklog/plugins/ (within the current repo/workdir).
  - Optional override: WORKLOG_PLUGIN_DIR env var and/or config key (documented).
  - Load order:
    - Built-ins first
    - Then external plugins in deterministic order (e.g., lexicographic by filename)
  - Only load files matching *.mjs / *.js (ignore .d.ts, maps, etc.)
  - Use dynamic import() with pathToFileURL() to load ESM from absolute paths.
- Name collisions:
  - If a plugin registers a command name that already exists, fail fast with a clear error (or optionally ‚Äúplugin wins‚Äù via a flag; pick one and document).
- Observability:
  - Add worklog plugins command to list discovered plugins, load status, and any errors (also useful for debugging CI installs).
  - Add --verbose (or reuse an existing pattern) to print plugin load diagnostics.
Documentation requirements
- Add a new docs section (README or dedicated doc) covering:
  - Plugin directory, supported file extensions, load timing (next invocation)
  - Plugin API contract and examples
  - How to author a plugin in a separate repo/package:
    - compile to ESM ("type":"module", output .mjs or .js)
    - place built artifact into .worklog/plugins/
  - Security model (‚Äúplugins execute arbitrary code; only use trusted plugins‚Äù)
  - Troubleshooting: module resolution, ESM/CJS pitfalls, stack traces, worklog plugins
Migration scope (port all existing commands)
- Break out everything currently defined in src/cli.ts:
  - init, status, create, list, show, update, delete, export, import, next, sync
  - comment command group and its subcommands
- Preserve behavior:
  - Options, defaults, JSON output mode, error exit codes, config/db behaviors, sync behavior
  - Help text (--help) remains coherent and complete
Implementation tasks
- Create src/commands/ modules:
  - One module per command (or per command group), exporting a register(...) function
- Create shared CLI context/util module:
  - output helpers, requireInitialized, db factory, config access, dataPath, constants
- Add plugin loader:
  - Resolve plugin directory, discover files, dynamic import, call register
  - Collect and surface load errors
- Add plugins command for introspection
- Update build output wiring so built-in command modules compile to dist/commands/*
- Update README/docs and add at least one end-to-end plugin example
Testing / verification
- Unit tests for:
  - plugin discovery filtering and deterministic ordering
  - plugin load error handling
  - collision handling
- Integration-ish tests (vitest spawning node) for:
  - dropping a plugin file into a temp plugin dir and verifying --help includes the new command
  - worklog plugins reporting
- Ensure npm pack / global install style still works (bin points to dist/cli.js)
Acceptance criteria
- Dropping a compiled ESM plugin file into .worklog/plugins/ makes its command appear on next worklog --help run, without rebuilding Worklog.
- All existing commands are implemented as external command modules (no large command definitions left in src/cli.ts besides bootstrap + shared wiring).
- Documentation added and accurate; includes a minimal plugin example and troubleshooting.
- Tests cover plugin discovery and basic loading behavior.
Notes / risks
- ESM-only environment: plugins must be ESM; document clearly.
- Running arbitrary local code: document security implications; consider an opt-out config/env to disable plugin loading if needed.
data.title: Add Pluggable CLI Command System (JS plugins), migrate built-in commands, and document

data.description:
We need a `next` command that will evaluate the state of the database and suggest the next item that should be worked on. Initially this will be a simplistic algorithm as follows:

- Find in progress items.
- If there are none currently in progress find the item that is highest priority and oldest (created first).
- If there are in progress items walk down the tree of the highest priority / oldest item until you find all the leaf nodes that are not in progress
- Select the leaf node that is highest priority and oldest

When the result is presented, if we are not using --json then the user should offered the opportunity to copy the ID into the clipboard.

It should be possible to filter the results by --assignee.

It should be possible to provide a search term that will fuzzy match against title, description and comments (any item without the search term in one of these places will not be considered).

Note that later iterations will use more complex algorithms for identifying the next item.

data.description:
The current comment command structure is

```
# Create a comment on a work item
npm run cli -- comment-create WI-1 -a "John Doe" -c "This is a comment with **markdown**" -r "WI-2,src/api.ts,https://example.com"

# List comments for a work item
npm run cli -- comment-list WI-1

# Show a specific comment
npm run cli -- comment-show WI-C1

# Update a comment
npm run cli -- comment-update WI-C1 -c "Updated comment text"

# Delete a comment
npm run cli -- comment-delete WI-C1
```

Chang this to use sub commands as shown below:

```
# Create a comment on a work item
npm run cli -- comment create WI-1 -a "John Doe" -c "This is a comment with **markdown**" -r "WI-2,src/api.ts,https://example.com"

# List comments for a work item
npm run cli -- comment list WI-1

# Show a specific comment
npm run cli -- comment show WI-C1

# Update a comment
npm run cli -- comment update WI-C1 -c "Updated comment text"

# Delete a comment
npm run cli -- comment delete WI-C1
```
data.title: Improve comment command structure

data.description:
Users should be able to download a release artifact, extract it, place it on their PATH, and run worklog without installing Node or running npm install. We‚Äôll implement a per-platform ‚Äúfolder distribution‚Äù that bundles:
- a small worklog launcher (or worklog.exe)
- a Node.js runtime
- the compiled Worklog CLI (dist/)
- runtime dependencies, including the native better-sqlite3 addon for that platform
This is explicitly not a single-file executable; it‚Äôs a self-contained directory that can be put on PATH.
Goals
- worklog runs on a clean machine with no Node/npm installed.
- Distributions are produced for at least: linux-x64, darwin-arm64, darwin-x64, win-x64 (expand as needed).
- Release artifact layout is stable and documented.
- CLI behavior is unchanged (same commands/options/output).
Non-goals
- Single-file executable.
- Replacing better-sqlite3.
- Building from source on user machines.
Implementation plan
1) Choose packaging approach (recommended)
- Bundle Node runtime + app into a folder:
  - node (or node.exe)
  - worklog launcher script/binary that executes the bundled node:
    - linux/macos: ./node ./dist/cli.js "$@"
    - windows: worklog.cmd or worklog.exe shim calling node.exe dist\cli.js %*
  - dist/ output from tsc
  - node_modules/ containing production deps (incl. better-sqlite3 compiled binary)
2) Add packaging scripts
- Add npm run build (already exists) + new scripts such as:
  - npm run dist:prep to create a clean staging directory
  - npm run dist:bundle to copy dist/, package.json, and install production deps into staging
  - npm run dist:node:<platform> to download/extract the correct Node runtime into staging (or use a build action to fetch it)
  - npm run dist:archive to produce .tar.gz / .zip per platform
- Ensure we install prod-only deps into staging (no devDependencies).
3) Handle native module compatibility (better-sqlite3)
- Build artifacts must be produced on each target OS/arch (or use CI runners per platform) so better-sqlite3‚Äôs .node binary matches the target.
- Verify that the bundled node version matches the ABI expected by the built better-sqlite3 binary (i.e., build/install using the same Node major version you bundle).
4) Entrypoint and pathing
- Ensure the CLI entry works when executed from anywhere:
  - Use process.execPath / import.meta.url-relative paths so it finds dist/ and bundled resources regardless of current working directory.
- Confirm that .worklog/ data paths remain in the current project directory (unchanged).
5) Versioning and update UX
- Add worklog --version (already) and optionally a worklog version --json output for tooling (optional).
- Document upgrade procedure: replace the folder on PATH with a newer version.
6) CI build + GitHub releases
- Add GitHub Actions workflow:
  - Matrix: ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest (and macos for both x64/arm64 as appropriate)
  - Steps: checkout, install, build, stage folder, run smoke tests, archive, upload artifacts
  - On tag push: create GitHub Release and attach artifacts
7) Smoke tests (required)
- On each platform artifact in CI:
  - Extract artifact
  - Run ./worklog --help
  - Run ./worklog --version
  - Optionally run a minimal command that doesn‚Äôt require repo init (e.g., worklog status should error cleanly) to confirm runtime works.
8) Documentation
- Update README.md with:
  - Download links / artifact names
  - Install instructions per OS (PATH update examples)
  - Notes about per-platform downloads
  - Troubleshooting: macOS Gatekeeper/quarantine, Linux executable bit, Windows SmartScreen
Acceptance criteria
- A user can:
  - download the correct artifact for their OS/arch
  - extract it
  - add the extracted directory to PATH
  - run worklog --help successfully with no Node/npm installed
- CI produces and uploads artifacts for the supported platforms on every release tag.
- Artifacts include better-sqlite3 correctly (no missing native module errors).
Notes / risks
- macOS: may require signing/notarization for best UX; at minimum document Gatekeeper prompts.
- Windows: consider providing both worklog.cmd shim and (optional) an .exe shim.
- Size: bundling Node increases artifact size; this is expected for ‚Äúno Node required‚Äù.
data.title: Ship Standalone ‚ÄúFolder Binary‚Äù Distribution (No npm install) for Worklog CLI

data.description:
We want the Worklog CLI (dist/cli.js, implemented in src/cli.ts) to support a pluggable command architecture where new commands can be added by dropping a compiled ESM plugin file (.js / .mjs) into a known directory, with no Worklog rebuild required. On the next CLI invocation, the new command should appear automatically.
This issue also migrates all existing built-in commands out of src/cli.ts into external command modules (shipped with the package), using the same plugin interface, and fully documents the feature.
Goals
- Pluggable commands discovered at runtime (from disk) and registered into commander.
- All existing commands ported to external command modules (no giant monolith src/cli.ts).
- Clear docs: how to write, build, install, and troubleshoot plugins.
Non-goals
- Loading TypeScript plugins directly.
- Hot-reloading commands within a single long-running CLI session (CLI restart is sufficient).
Proposed design
- Define a plugin contract:
  - Each plugin is an ESM module exporting default as register(ctx): void (or named register).
  - register receives:
    - program: Command (commander instance)
    - ctx shared utilities (output helpers, config/db accessors, version, paths)
- Command module shape (example):
  - export default function register({ program, ctx }) { program.command('foo')... }
- Built-in commands:
  - Move each top-level command (and subcommand groups like comment) into its own module under src/commands/.
  - src/cli.ts becomes a thin bootstrap:
    - create program, global options, shared ctx
    - register built-in commands by importing ./commands/*.js
    - load external plugins from plugin dir and register them
- External plugin discovery:
  - Default plugin directory: .worklog/plugins/ (within the current repo/workdir).
  - Optional override: WORKLOG_PLUGIN_DIR env var and/or config key (documented).
  - Load order:
    - Built-ins first
    - Then external plugins in deterministic order (e.g., lexicographic by filename)
  - Only load files matching *.mjs / *.js (ignore .d.ts, maps, etc.)
  - Use dynamic import() with pathToFileURL() to load ESM from absolute paths.
- Name collisions:
  - If a plugin registers a command name that already exists, fail fast with a clear error (or optionally ‚Äúplugin wins‚Äù via a flag; pick one and document).
- Observability:
  - Add worklog plugins command to list discovered plugins, load status, and any errors (also useful for debugging CI installs).
  - Add --verbose (or reuse an existing pattern) to print plugin load diagnostics.
Documentation requirements
- Add a new docs section (README or dedicated doc) covering:
  - Plugin directory, supported file extensions, load timing (next invocation)
  - Plugin API contract and examples
  - How to author a plugin in a separate repo/package:
    - compile to ESM ("type":"module", output .mjs or .js)
    - place built artifact into .worklog/plugins/
  - Security model (‚Äúplugins execute arbitrary code; only use trusted plugins‚Äù)
  - Troubleshooting: module resolution, ESM/CJS pitfalls, stack traces, worklog plugins
Migration scope (port all existing commands)
- Break out everything currently defined in src/cli.ts:
  - init, status, create, list, show, update, delete, export, import, next, sync
  - comment command group and its subcommands
- Preserve behavior:
  - Options, defaults, JSON output mode, error exit codes, config/db behaviors, sync behavior
  - Help text (--help) remains coherent and complete
Implementation tasks
- Create src/commands/ modules:
  - One module per command (or per command group), exporting a register(...) function
- Create shared CLI context/util module:
  - output helpers, requireInitialized, db factory, config access, dataPath, constants
- Add plugin loader:
  - Resolve plugin directory, discover files, dynamic import, call register
  - Collect and surface load errors
- Add plugins command for introspection
- Update build output wiring so built-in command modules compile to dist/commands/*
- Update README/docs and add at least one end-to-end plugin example
Testing / verification
- Unit tests for:
  - plugin discovery filtering and deterministic ordering
  - plugin load error handling
  - collision handling
- Integration-ish tests (vitest spawning node) for:
  - dropping a plugin file into a temp plugin dir and verifying --help includes the new command
  - worklog plugins reporting
- Ensure npm pack / global install style still works (bin points to dist/cli.js)
Acceptance criteria
- Dropping a compiled ESM plugin file into .worklog/plugins/ makes its command appear on next worklog --help run, without rebuilding Worklog.
- All existing commands are implemented as external command modules (no large command definitions left in src/cli.ts besides bootstrap + shared wiring).
- Documentation added and accurate; includes a minimal plugin example and troubleshooting.
- Tests cover plugin discovery and basic loading behavior.
Notes / risks
- ESM-only environment: plugins must be ESM; document clearly.
- Running arbitrary local code: document security implications; consider an opt-out config/env to disable plugin loading if needed.
data.title: Add Pluggable CLI Command System (JS plugins), migrate built-in commands, and document

data.description:
The current comment command structure is

```
# Create a comment on a work item
npm run cli -- comment-create WI-1 -a "John Doe" -c "This is a comment with **markdown**" -r "WI-2,src/api.ts,https://example.com"

# List comments for a work item
npm run cli -- comment-list WI-1

# Show a specific comment
npm run cli -- comment-show WI-C1

# Update a comment
npm run cli -- comment-update WI-C1 -c "Updated comment text"

# Delete a comment
npm run cli -- comment-delete WI-C1
```

Chang this to use sub commands as shown below:

```
# Create a comment on a work item
npm run cli -- comment create WI-1 -a "John Doe" -c "This is a comment with **markdown**" -r "WI-2,src/api.ts,https://example.com"

# List comments for a work item
npm run cli -- comment list WI-1

# Show a specific comment
npm run cli -- comment show WI-C1

# Update a comment
npm run cli -- comment update WI-C1 -c "Updated comment text"

# Delete a comment
npm run cli -- comment delete WI-C1
```
data.title: Improve comment command structure

data.description:
The goal is for us to be able to keep issues in sync across multiple instances of worklog. We currently have the ability to export and import, but there is currently no version control features.

When we do a git pull the latest jsonl file will be retrieved but it currently will not be imported into the database. Furthermore, there may be convlicts that need to be resolved.

Create a sync command that will pull the latest file from git (only the jsonl file), merge the content - including resolving conflicts (use the updatedAt to indicate the most recent data that should take precendence). Export the data and push the latest back to git.

Generate the best possible algorithm to make this happen, do not feel constrained by the detail of my request here, the goal is to ensure that when the sync command is run the local database has all the latest remote updates and the current local updates.
data.title: Issue syncing

data.description:
We have a problem with ids. If two different machines create a single issue the naive id counter increment will result in conflicting IDs. This will break the sync mechanism.

We want to ensure that will never happen. This means we need a guaranteed world unique ID. At the same time the IDs need to be easy to remember. Can you update

data.description:
Problem
- Worklog currently uses a Map-backed in-memory database (src/database.ts) and relies on importing/exporting .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl per CLI invocation (src/cli.ts) and at API server startup (src/index.ts).
- There is no persistent database process/state that survives between separate CLI runs, and the ‚Äúsource of truth‚Äù/refresh behavior is ad-hoc.
- We want a real persistent DB (on disk) so the database state exists independently of a single process, and a clear refresh rule: if the local JSONL file is newer than what the DB has, reload/refresh the DB from JSONL.
Goal
- Replace the ‚Äúin-memory Map only‚Äù approach with a disk-backed database that persists between command executions.
- Ensure the DB automatically refreshes from .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl when that file is newer than the DB‚Äôs current state.
Proposed behavior
- On CLI start and API server start:
  - Open/connect to the persistent DB stored on disk (location configurable; default under .worklog/).
  - Determine staleness:
    - Compare JSONL mtime vs DB ‚Äúlast imported from JSONL‚Äù timestamp (or DB file mtime if that‚Äôs the chosen proxy).
  - If JSONL is newer:
    - Re-import from JSONL into the DB (rebuild items/comments).
    - Update DB metadata to record the import time + source file path + JSONL mtime/hash (so future comparisons are reliable).
- On writes (create/update/delete/comment ops):
  - Persist to DB immediately.
  - Decide and document the new ‚Äúsource of truth‚Äù model:
    - Option A: DB is source of truth; JSONL is an export artifact (write JSONL on demand or on a schedule).
    - Option B: Keep writing JSONL for Git workflows, but DB remains authoritative for runtime and only refreshes from JSONL when JSONL is newer (e.g., after a git pull).
Scope / tasks
- Add a persistent DB implementation (likely SQLite) and a small DB metadata table, e.g.:
  - meta(key TEXT PRIMARY KEY, value TEXT) with keys like lastJsonlImportMtimeMs, lastJsonlImportAt, schemaVersion.
- Refactor database layer:
  - Introduce an interface (e.g. WorklogStore) implemented by the new persistent DB.
  - Keep the current Map DB as a fallback/dev option if desired, but default to persistent.
- Update CLI (src/cli.ts):
  - Replace loadData()/saveData() logic with ‚Äúopen DB; maybe refresh from JSONL; operate on DB; optionally export JSONL‚Äù.
  - Ensure multi-project prefix behavior still works.
- Update API server startup (src/index.ts):
  - Same refresh logic on boot.
- Refresh logic details:
  - Use fs.statSync(jsonlPath).mtimeMs (or async equivalent).
  - Compare against stored DB metadata value; if JSONL missing, do nothing.
  - If DB is empty/uninitialized and JSONL exists, import.
- Add migration/versioning:
  - DB schema version stored in metadata; handle upgrades safely.
- Tests / acceptance checks
  - Running worklog create then a separate worklog list shows the created item without relying on in-process state.
  - If .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl is modified externally (simulate git pull), the next CLI/API startup refreshes DB and reflects the updated data.
  - If DB has newer data than JSONL, it does not overwrite DB (unless explicitly commanded); behavior is documented.
Acceptance criteria
- DB persists across separate CLI executions (verified by creating an item, exiting, re-running list/show).
- On startup (CLI + API), if .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl is newer than DB state, DB is refreshed from JSONL automatically.
- No data loss when switching from current JSONL-only workflow: existing .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl is imported into the new DB on first run.
- Clear documented ‚Äúsource of truth‚Äù and when JSONL is written/updated.
Notes / implementation preference
- SQLite is a good default (single file on disk, easy distribution, supports concurrency better than ad-hoc JSON).
- Keep .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl for Git-friendly sharing, but treat it as an import/export boundary rather than the primary store.
data.title: Persist Worklog DB across executions; refresh from JSONL when newer

data.description:
Summary:
Clicking a work item in the tree view of the TUI does not update the selected work item shown in the detail pane. The tree selection changes visually, but the detail pane continues to show the previously selected item.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Start the application and open the TUI work item view.
2. In the left-hand tree view, click a work item that differs from the currently selected item.
3. Observe the highlighted selection in the tree and the content shown in the right-hand detail pane.

Expected behavior:
- The detail pane updates to show the details for the clicked work item.
- The detail pane is focused/active for keyboard interactions related to the newly selected item.

Actual behavior:
- The tree view highlights the clicked item, but the detail pane continues to display the previously selected item's details.
- Users must perform an additional action (e.g., keyboard navigation or click in the detail pane) to refresh the detail pane to the correct item.

Suggested investigation / implementation notes:
- Verify the tree selection change event is propagated to the detail pane controller.
- Check whether the detail pane subscribes to tree selection changes or is reading from stale state.
- Look for race conditions when switching focus between panes, or missing UI redraw calls.
- Add a regression test that simulates a tree selection change and asserts the detail pane shows matching work item details.

Acceptance criteria:
- Clicking a work item in the TUI tree view updates the detail pane to show the clicked item's details immediately.
- A test (manual or automated) demonstrates the fix.
- Add a wl comment in this item with the commit hash when a PR is created.

data.description:
Summary
- Update the `wl next` selection logic to treat `updatedAt` (modification time) as a configurable signal (recency boost or penalty) and move from simple priority+createdAt tie-breaking to a multi-factor scoring function.

Why
- Current algorithm uses priority and creation time only (see `src/database.ts::selectHighestPriorityOldest`) which makes creation time the dominant tie-breaker. That causes recently-updated items to not be surfaced appropriately and encourages gaming by creating older stubs.
- Modification time (`updatedAt`) is a valuable signal: it can indicate recent discussion (should often be de-prioritized briefly) or recent activity requiring follow-up (should be surfaced). Making it configurable avoids hard assumptions.

Files of interest
- src/commands/next.ts
- src/database.ts

Proposed changes
1. Add a modular scoring function `computeScore(item, now, options)` that combines:
   - priority (primary)
   - due date proximity (if present)
   - blocked status (large negative if blocked)
   - assignee match (boost if assigned to current user)
   - effort (smaller items encouraged)
   - age (createdAt; small boost to older items to avoid starvation)
   - updatedAt recency: configurable as either a penalty for very recent updates or a boost for recent updates (policy option)
2. Replace `selectHighestPriorityOldest` and related selection points with `selectByScore(items, opts)` using the scoring function. Keep a stable tie-breaker (createdAt then id).
3. Add CLI flags to `wl next`:
   - `--recency-policy <prefer|avoid|ignore>` (default: avoid) to choose how updatedAt affects score
   - optional weight flags (advanced) or use config file to tune weights
4. Add unit tests covering:
   - prefer older items when priorities equal
   - penalize items edited in the last X hours (avoid)
   - prefer recently-updated items when policy=prefer
   - behavior with blocked/critical items remains unchanged
5. Document the behavior change in CLI.md and RELEASE notes.

Acceptance criteria
- `wl next` uses the scoring function and yields deterministic, explainable selection reasons
- New CLI flag `--recency-policy` works and is documented
- Tests added for core scoring behaviors

Notes
- This is non-destructive; default behavior should match existing behavior closely (use small age boost and a modest recent-update penalty by default).
- I will implement the change and include tests if you want; please confirm whether default `recency-policy` should be `avoid` (recommended) or `prefer`.
data.title: Improve 'wl next' selection algorithm to include modification time and scoring

Clicking outside the dialog cancels; ESC cancels. Provide suggestion for shortcut before implementing.

data.description:
Summary

Provide a new UI for updating a work-item's stage (and related fields) inspired by the existing close UI. The initial scope is to allow changing the stage of an item, but the component must be designed to grow to support other quick edits (status, comments, assignees, tags) so the keyboard shortcut and UX are generic. Suggest the primary keyboard shortcut be `U` (for Update) and follow existing patterns used by the close UI.

User stories

- As a Producer or Agent, I want to quickly change an item's stage (eg. idea -> in_progress -> review) without opening a full edit page so I can triage and advance work more efficiently.
- As a keyboard-focused user, I want a single, discoverable shortcut (`U`) to open a compact, accessible modal/popover to make quick changes.
- As a developer, I want a reusable component that can later include status changes, adding comments, and other small edits without a large refactor.

Expected behavior

- Pressing `U` when a work-item is focused opens the Update UI (modal or popover) similar in layout and affordances to the close UI.
- The UI shows the current stage, a list/dropdown of available stages (respecting permissions), and a Confirm/Cancel action.
- Choosing a new stage and confirming updates the work-item and closes the UI. The change should be optimistic or show a short saving state and surface errors.
- The UI should be accessible (keyboard navigable, ARIA roles) and mobile-friendly.

Suggested implementation approach

- Reuse the close UI component patterns: same modal/popover wrapper, header, action layout, and keyboard handling where appropriate.
- Implement a generic `QuickEdit` or `UpdatePanel` component with a small API that supports multiple field types (stage, status, inline comment). Initially only implement the `stage` field.
- Wire the `U` keyboard shortcut to open the component when a work-item row/card is focused. Keep the shortcut registration centralized so future quick-actions can share shortcuts.
- Ensure server API call is the same as used by the full edit flow (reuse existing update endpoint) and that the frontend updates local store/cache appropriately.
- Add unit and integration tests for the component and keyboard shortcut behavior.

Acceptance criteria

1. New work item (feature) exists in Worklog for this task.
2. Pressing `U` opens an Update UI for the focused item.
3. The UI allows selecting a new stage and confirms the change via the existing update API.
4. The UI handles success and error states and is keyboard accessible.
5. Code is implemented as a reusable component that can be extended to other quick edits.
6. Tests cover the main flows and the keyboard shortcut.

Notes / Risks / Dependencies

- Depends on existing close UI patterns; developer should review `Close` UI implementation for consistency.
- Permission checks: only allow stage changes for users with the required permissions; surface a message if not allowed.
- Decide whether `U` conflicts with other shortcuts; coordinate with global shortcut registry.

Suggested priority: medium
data.title: Add 'Update' UI to change work-item stage (shortcut: U)

data.description:
Summary:
Replace the current Tab-based focus advancement in the affected component with Alt+Right Arrow. This work item updates the intended keyboard shortcut and documents expected behaviour and acceptance criteria.

Context:
The component currently advances focus with the Tab key. We want to change the shortcut to Alt+Right Arrow to avoid interfering with native Tab behaviour (sequential focus navigation) and to provide a more explicit, single-key modifier shortcut for this specific action.

User Stories:
- As a keyboard user, I can press Alt+Right Arrow to move the component's internal focus to the next interactive element without changing global Tab order.
- As a user relying on standard Tab navigation, pressing Tab should retain default browser/system behaviour and not trigger the component-specific focus advance.

Expected behaviour:
- Pressing Alt+Right Arrow moves focus to the next logical interactive element within the component.
- Pressing Alt+Left Arrow (if applicable) should move focus to the previous element (if this pattern exists today; if not, consider whether to implement a symmetric shortcut).
- Tab and Shift+Tab continue to perform standard sequential focus navigation and do not trigger the component-specific action.
- Shortcut should be documented in the component's accessibility notes and any visible hints/tooltips where applicable.

Steps to reproduce (before change):
1. Open the component UI that currently uses Tab to advance internal focus.
2. Press Tab and observe the component-specific focus change.

Suggested implementation approach:
- Update the component's keyboard handler to listen for Alt+Right Arrow (e.g. check for `event.altKey && event.key === 'ArrowRight'`) and call the existing focus-advance logic.
- Ensure the handler prevents default only when the Alt+Right combination is used; do not prevent default on plain Tab/Shift+Tab.
- Add unit and keyboard-integration tests to verify behaviour (Alt+Right advances focus; Tab does not trigger the component-specific advance).
- Update documentation and release notes to call out the new shortcut.

Acceptance criteria:
- The work item demonstrates a code change (or spec change) where Alt+Right Arrow is used to advance focus.
- Tests validating the new behaviour are added or updated.
- Documentation (component docs or accessibility notes) is updated to reference Alt+Right Arrow.
- No regression in standard Tab/Shift+Tab focus behaviour.

Notes:
- If other shortcuts use Alt+Right Arrow in the app, evaluate conflicts and adjust accordingly.
- Consider whether Alt+Left Arrow should be implemented for backward navigation and whether it should be part of this ticket or a follow-up.
data.title: Use Alt+Right Arrow to advance focus (replace Tab)

data.description:
Summary
Add a new TUI shortcut key N that opens a dialog showing the text "Evaluating next work item..." while the wl next command runs in the background. When the command returns, display its result in the same dialog. The user can close the dialog (close button, Esc, or click-off) or click a View button that selects the work item in the tree view; if the item is not currently present, prompt with the existing dialog that offers switching to ALL items.

User stories
- As a TUI user, I want to run wl next without leaving the UI so I can quickly identify the next item.
- As a keyboard-first user, I want a simple shortcut to evaluate and jump to the next work item.

Expected behavior
- Pressing N opens a modal dialog with "Evaluating next work item...".
- wl next runs in a background process without blocking UI rendering.
- When the command completes, the dialog updates to show the result (include ID/title and key info from wl next).
- Dialog actions:
  - Close button, Esc, or click-off closes dialog.
  - View selects the returned work item in the tree.
  - If the item is not visible in the current filter, prompt to switch to ALL items and then select it.

Suggested implementation approach
- Reuse existing modal/overlay patterns from close/preview dialogs.
- Use a background process to run wl next --json and parse its result.
- Update dialog content on completion; handle errors with a clear message.
- Ensure focus is restored to the tree when dialog closes.

Acceptance criteria
1. N opens the evaluation dialog with initial text.
2. wl next runs asynchronously and updates the dialog on completion.
3. View selects the item in the tree; if not visible, user is prompted to switch to ALL and then selection updates.
4. Dialog can be closed via close button, Esc, or click-off.
5. Errors from wl next are surfaced in the dialog.
data.title: Add N shortcut to evaluate next item in TUI

2. Expanded state includes ancestors of in-progress items without collapsing user-expanded nodes.
3. Behavior applies to refresh events (e.g., R shortcut and programmatic refresh).
data.title: Expand in-progress nodes on refresh

data.description:
Summary
Add command support to Opencode prompts (press O). If the user starts input with '/', it signals command mode. As they type, autocomplete shows the most likely command they are trying to use. When they press Enter, the command is completed and a trailing space is inserted so they can continue typing arguments.

User stories
- As an Opencode user, I want to type '/' to trigger command mode and get autocomplete suggestions so I can discover and use commands quickly.
- As a keyboard-first user, I want Enter to accept the suggested command and continue typing arguments without extra steps.

Expected behavior
- In Opencode prompt mode, typing a leading '/' enters command mode.
- Autocomplete shows the top matching command as the user types.
- Pressing Enter accepts the suggested command and inserts a space after it, keeping the cursor in the input.
- If there is no match, Enter submits as normal (or leaves input unchanged per existing behavior).

Suggested implementation approach
- Hook into the Opencode prompt input handling to detect leading '/'.
- Maintain a list of available commands (existing slash commands) and compute the best match by prefix.
- Render inline ghost text or suggestion UI consistent with existing prompt styling.
- Ensure normal text input works when '/' is not the first character.

Acceptance criteria
1. Typing '/' at the start of the prompt activates command autocomplete.
2. Autocomplete updates as the user types.
3. Enter accepts the suggested command and inserts a trailing space.
4. Existing prompt behavior is unchanged when not in command mode.
data.title: OpenCode

data.description:
Summary
Add a search feature to the TUI: press a key to enter a search term, run wl list <search-term> to filter items in the tree, and show active search term(s) in the footer labeled Filter:. Update footer text to remove Press ? for help and show -Closed (x) when closed items are hidden (and nothing when they are not hidden).

User stories
- As a TUI user, I want to hit a key and type a search term so I can filter the tree quickly.
- As a user, I want the active filter shown in the footer so I remember what I‚Äôm viewing.

Expected behavior
- A new keybinding opens an input to capture a search term.
- The TUI runs wl list <search-term> and uses the results to filter the tree view.
- Footer displays Filter: <term> when active.
- Footer no longer includes Press ? for help.
- When closed items are hidden, footer shows -Closed (x); when not hidden, it shows nothing about closed items.

Suggested implementation approach
- Reuse existing modal/input patterns for capturing the search term.
- Use the wl list command with the term to fetch matching items.
- Ensure filters reset/clear appropriately.

Acceptance criteria
1. Keybinding opens search input.
2. Tree view filters to results from wl list <term>.
3. Footer shows Filter: with the active term(s).
4. Footer updates closed-items label as described.
data.title: Add TUI search filter using wl list

data.description:
Summary: User reports interactive shell produces garbled output and missing interactivity. Investigate opencode raw logs and key-forwarding code that bridges terminal input to the child process.

Steps to perform:
1) Locate worklog/opencode raw log (opencode-raw.log) and read recent entries.
2) Inspect code that resolves worklog directory and writes/reads the raw log.
3) Search for terminal/pty usage in the codebase (node-pty, stdin.write, pty.spawn) and review key-forwarding implementation.
4) Identify likely API mismatches (e.g., using child.stdin.write against node-pty which uses .write()) and suggest fixes.
5) Report findings and recommended code changes.

Expected outcome: A diagnosis of why input is not forwarded correctly and a short list of targeted code changes to fix the issue.

Acceptance criteria: Work item created; logs read; key-forwarding code located; clear recommendations with file references.
data.title: Investigate interactive shell log and pty key forwarding

data.description: Summary: Change wl next behavior for in-progress traversal so that when a deepest in-progress item is selected, we choose the best scored direct child of that item rather than searching leaf descendants. Expected behavior: if the deepest in-progress item has open children, select the highest score child (using existing score/recency policy). If it has no open children, fall back to the in-progress item itself. Acceptance criteria: 1) wl next picks highest-score direct child under the deepest in-progress item; 2) leaf descendant search is removed from this path; 3) behavior remains unchanged for critical selection and for no in-progress items.
data.title: Adjust wl next child selection under in-progress

data.description:
Summary
Modify the OpenCode prompt in TUI to send prompts to a running OpenCode server via HTTP/WebSocket instead of spawning a new CLI process. This will enable better integration, session persistence, and real-time streaming of responses.

Blocked by: WL-0MKWCW9K610XPQ1P (Auto-start OpenCode server if not running)

User Stories
- As a TUI user, I want my OpenCode prompts to be sent to a persistent server so I can maintain context across multiple prompts
- As a developer, I want the TUI to connect to an OpenCode server for better performance and session management
- As a user, I want to see streaming responses from the server in real-time

Expected Behavior
- When OpenCode dialog opens, check if an OpenCode server is running (configurable URL/port)
- If server is available, send prompts via HTTP POST or WebSocket to the server
- Stream responses back to the TUI pane in real-time
- Show connection status in the UI
- Fall back to CLI execution if server is not available (optional)
- Support configuration for server URL (default: http://localhost:3000 or similar)

Suggested Implementation Approach
- Add server connection logic to tui.ts
- Implement HTTP/WebSocket client for OpenCode server communication
- Create configuration for server URL (environment variable or config file)
- Add connection status indicator to the OpenCode dialog
- Modify runOpencode() to use server API instead of spawn()
- Handle streaming responses and display them in the pane
- Implement error handling and fallback behavior

Acceptance Criteria
1. OpenCode prompts are sent to a configurable server endpoint
2. Responses stream back in real-time to the TUI pane
3. Connection status is visible to the user
4. Error handling for server unavailability
5. Configuration option for server URL
6. Existing slash command autocomplete continues to work
data.title: Send OpenCode prompts to server instead of CLI

data.description:
Summary
Automatically detect and start an OpenCode server when the TUI OpenCode dialog is opened, ensuring a server is always available for prompt processing. This is a prerequisite for sending prompts to the server.

User Stories
- As a TUI user, I want the OpenCode server to start automatically so I don't have to manage it manually
- As a developer, I want seamless server lifecycle management integrated into the TUI
- As a user, I want to know when the server is starting, running, or has failed to start

Expected Behavior
- When OpenCode dialog opens, check if an OpenCode server is already running
- If no server is detected, automatically start one in the background
- Display server status (starting, running, error) in the UI
- Keep the server running for the duration of the TUI session
- Optionally stop the server when TUI exits (configurable)
- Reuse existing server if one is already running on the configured port
- Handle port conflicts gracefully

Suggested Implementation Approach
- Add server detection logic (check if server responds at configured URL/port)
- Implement server spawning using child_process to run 'opencode serve' or similar
- Track server process lifecycle (PID, status)
- Add server health check endpoint polling
- Create visual indicator for server status in OpenCode dialog
- Store server configuration (port, host) in config or environment
- Implement cleanup on TUI exit
- Add error handling for server start failures

Acceptance Criteria
1. Server is automatically started when needed
2. Server status is visible in the OpenCode dialog
3. Existing running servers are detected and reused
4. Server start failures are handled gracefully with user feedback
5. Server process is properly managed (no orphaned processes)
6. Configuration options for server auto-start behavior
7. Health checks confirm server is ready before sending prompts

Blocks
This item blocks WL-0MKWCQQIW0ZP4A67 (Send OpenCode prompts to server) as the server must be running before prompts can be sent to it.

data.description:
Summary
Implement bidirectional communication between the TUI and OpenCode server to allow agents to request and receive user input during prompt execution. The TUI needs to detect when the server session is waiting for input and provide an interface for users to respond.

Blocked by: WL-0MKWCQQIW0ZP4A67 (Send OpenCode prompts to server instead of CLI)

User Stories
- As a user, I want to provide input when OpenCode agents ask questions during execution
- As an agent, I want to receive user responses to continue processing tasks
- As a user, I want clear visual indication when the agent is waiting for my input
- As a user, I want to type and send responses without interrupting the agent's output

Expected Behavior
- TUI monitors server session for input requests
- When input is needed, display a clear prompt/indicator in the OpenCode pane
- Show the agent's question or prompt clearly
- Provide an input field for user response
- Allow user to type response and send with Enter (or Ctrl+Enter for multiline)
- Send response back to server session
- Continue displaying agent output after input is provided
- Handle multiple input requests in a single session
- Escape or cancel option to abort input request

Suggested Implementation Approach
- Monitor server WebSocket/SSE stream for input request markers
- Create input mode in OpenCode pane when input is requested
- Add input textarea that appears below or within the output pane
- Implement input capture and submission logic
- Send input responses via server API/WebSocket
- Handle input timeout scenarios
- Add visual indicators (color change, prompt symbol, etc.)
- Preserve output history while in input mode
- Queue multiple input requests if needed

Acceptance Criteria
1. TUI detects when OpenCode server session needs user input
2. Clear visual indication when waiting for input
3. User can type and submit responses
4. Input is sent to the server and processed by the agent
5. Session continues after input is provided
6. Multiple input requests handled correctly
7. Cancel/escape mechanism available
8. Input history preserved in output pane
9. Works with both single-line and multi-line input

Technical Considerations
- Requires WebSocket or SSE connection to monitor session state
- Need to parse server messages for input request protocol
- Input UI should not block viewing previous output
- Consider input validation and error handling
- Handle disconnection during input request
data.title: Enable user input for OpenCode server agents in TUI
