data.description:
Summary:
As a user I want a predictable sort order that represents the order of work that needs to be done. The sort order should be enforced by the `wl` CLI so users can rely on the list order to plan and pick the next task.

User stories:
- As a contributor I can sort work items deterministically so the top of the list is the most important work to do next.
- As a producer I can set and persist the ordering of items to reflect priorities that are not captured by the `priority` field alone.

Expected behaviour:
- `wl list` and `wl next` return items in a consistent, deterministic order that represents work sequencing.
- Owners and producers can adjust order (e.g. move up/down, set position) and changes are persisted.
- The CLI provides flags to view and modify order and respects ordering when filtering and paging.

Suggested implementation approach:
- Add an integer `sort_index` field to work items stored by Worklog; lower numbers = earlier.
- Add CLI commands/flags: `wl move <id> --before <id|position>` and `wl reorder <id> --position <n>` or `wl swap <id1> <id2>`; also `wl list --sort=order`.
- When inserting without explicit position, append to end (highest index) or use priorities to compute default index.
- Ensure `wl next` picks the lowest `sort_index` among ready items, break ties by priority and created_at.
- Provide migration to populate `sort_index` from existing priorities/created_at.

Acceptance criteria:
1) A new feature work item exists and is linked as a child of WL-0MKVZ55PR0LTMJA1 (this item).
2) `wl list` and `wl next` document that order is enforced and show deterministic sorting using `sort_index`.
3) CLI commands exist to move/reorder items and persist changes.
4) Migration plan documented and tested on a staging dataset.

Related:
- discovered-from:WL-0MKVZ55PR0LTMJA1
data.title: Sort order for work item list (enforced by wl CLI)

data.description:
Summary: Add template-based validation for work items to enforce minimum required content and constrain field values.

Requirements:
- Define a template format that specifies required fields, default values, and allowed ranges/sets (e.g., priority, status, issueType, stage, tags).
- Validate new and updated work items against the template.
- Apply defaults for missing optional fields.
- Reject or surface validation errors when requirements are not met.

Expected behavior:
- Work item creation/update fails with clear validation errors when required content is missing or field values are out of bounds.
- Defaults are applied consistently when fields are omitted.

Acceptance criteria:
- Template format is documented and supports required fields, defaults, and limits.
- Validation is enforced on create and update paths.
- Error messages are actionable and list which fields failed validation.
- Tests cover valid/invalid cases and default application.
data.title: Validate work items against templates

data.description:
Summary:
Provide a template-driven validation system for Worklog so work items meet minimum content requirements and enforce defaults and limits for field values. Templates define required fields, types, default values, bounds, and regex/format constraints. Validation runs on create/update and can be applied retroactively.

User stories:
- As a contributor I want new work items to require the fields my team needs (e.g., summary, acceptance criteria, effort) so items are actionable.
- As a producer I want to enforce value limits (e.g., effort range, tag whitelist) and provide defaults for missing fields to reduce friction.
- As an operator I want to validate existing items against a template and receive a report of violations.

Expected behaviour:
- Templates are stored (YAML/JSON) and can be managed via the `wl` CLI: `wl template add|update|remove|list|show` and `wl template set-default <name>`.
- On `wl create` and `wl update` the CLI validates input against the active template and returns human-friendly errors for missing/invalid fields.
- Templates define: required fields, field types, default values, min/max for numeric fields, max-length for strings, allowed values (enums), regex patterns, and whether a field is editable after creation.
- Defaults are applied automatically when creating an item unless `--no-defaults` is passed.
- Provide a `wl template validate --report` command to check existing items and output a machine-readable report (JSON) and human summary.
- Validation is configurable per-project or global and supports template inheritance/variants (e.g., `bug`, `feature`, `chore`).

Suggested implementation approach:
- Add a `templates` store in the Worklog data model; templates versioned and referenced by work items.
- Implement a validation engine using JSON Schema or a small custom validator that supports the required rules and default application.
- Integrate validation into CLI create/update paths; fail fast with clear messages and exit codes suitable for automation.
- Provide tooling to scan and report violations and a migration assistant to fill defaults and flag unsafe auto-fixes.
- Add tests for schema parsing, CLI validation messages, and the validation report command.

Acceptance criteria:
1) A feature work item exists and is linked as a child of WL-0MKVZ55PR0LTMJA1.
2) CLI commands to manage templates are specified and implemented docs drafted.
3) `wl create` and `wl update` validate against the active template, applying defaults and rejecting invalid values with actionable errors.
4) `wl template validate` reports violations for existing items and supports a `--fix` mode that applies safe defaults after review.
5) Tests cover validator behavior, default application, and report generation.

Related:
- discovered-from:WL-0MKVZ55PR0LTMJA1
data.title: Validate work items against a template (content, defaults, limits)

data.description:
Summary:
Clicking anywhere inside the application's TUI causes the TUI to immediately close and return the user to their shell.

Environment:
- Worklog TUI (terminal UI)
- Reproduced on Linux terminal (tty/gnome-terminal), likely when mouse support or click events are enabled

Steps to reproduce:
1. Launch the TUI (run the usual command to start the app's TUI).
2. Hover over any area of the UI and click with the mouse (left-click).
3. Observe that the TUI closes immediately (no confirmation, no error message).

Actual behaviour:
- Any mouse click inside the TUI causes the TUI process to exit and control returns to the shell.

Expected behaviour:
- Mouse clicks should be handled by the UI (focus, selection, or ignored) and must not close the TUI unless the user explicitly invokes the close action (e.g., pressing the quit key or choosing Quit from a menu).

Impact:
- Critical: blocks interactive usage for users who have mouse support enabled or who accidentally click; data loss risk if users are mid-task.

Suggested investigation & implementation approach:
- Reproduce and capture terminal logs and stderr output (run from a shell and capture output).
- Check TUI library (ncurses / termbox / tcell / blessed or similar) mouse event handling and configuration; ensure mouse events are not mapped to a quit/exit action.
- Audit input handling: confirm click events are routed to UI components instead of closing the main loop.
- Add a regression test exercising mouse clicks (or disable mouse-driven quit) and a unit/integration test that verifies TUI remains running after a click.
- Add logging around main event loop to capture unexpected exits and surface the exit reason.

Acceptance criteria:
- Clicking inside the TUI no longer causes the application to exit.
- Repro steps no longer trigger a shutdown on supported terminals (verified by QA).
- Unit/integration tests added to prevent regression.
- Changes documented in the changelog and a short note added to TUI usage docs if behaviour changed.

Notes:
- If you want, I can attempt to reproduce locally and attach logs; tell me the command to launch the TUI if different from the repo default.
data.title: Critical: TUI closes when clicking anywhere

data.description:
Summary:
When comments are displayed by the  CLI they should be presented in reverse chronological order (newest first). Currently the CLI sometimes presents comments in chronological order or leaves ordering unspecified, which makes it harder for users to see recent discussion immediately.

User story:
As a user of the  CLI I want comments to appear newest-first so I can quickly read the latest discussion without scrolling.

Steps to reproduce (example):
1. Run  or  for a work item with multiple comments.
2. Observe the order of returned/displayed comments.

Actual behaviour:
- The CLI may present comments in chronological order (oldest first) or in an unspecified order depending on the backend or client path.

Expected behaviour:
- The CLI should display comments in reverse chronological order (most recent first) whenever comments are shown.
- For JSON outputs () the  array should be ordered newest-first.
- For human-readable CLI output () the printed comments should be displayed newest-first.

Suggested implementation approach:
- Preferred: Implement server-side ordering so all clients (CLI, web, API) receive comments newest-first. Ensure API docs reflect ordering.
- Alternative: If server change is not possible immediately, sort comments in the CLI client before rendering/printing and for  outputs implement a post-fetch ordering step. Note: prefer server-side fix to keep API contract consistent.
- Add tests: unit tests for ordering behavior in the client and integration tests (or API tests) verifying the server returns ordered comments.
- Update documentation and changelog mentioning that comments are returned newest-first.

Acceptance criteria:
-  returns a  array ordered newest-first.
-  displays comments newest-first in the terminal UI.
- Tests covering the ordering are added and passing.
- Documentation updated to state the ordering guarantee.

Notes:
- If you want me to implement the change, tell me whether to modify the server/API or implement a client-side sort in the CLI. I recommend server-side where possible.
,priority:medium,issue-type:feature
data.title: Present comments in reverse date order in CLI

data.description:
Summary:
Full refactor and modularization of the terminal UI (TUI) to make it easier for multiple agents to work on and to remove code smells.

Context:
- Current TUI implementation is in `src/commands/tui.ts` (very large, many responsibilities).
- A recent crash was caused by direct reassignment of `opencodeText.style` (see existing bug WL-0MKX2C2X007IRR8G).

Goals:
- Break `src/commands/tui.ts` into smaller modules (components, layout, server comms, SSE handling, utils).
- Improve TypeScript typings and remove wide use of `any`.
- Remove code smells (long functions, duplicated logic, magic numbers, global mutable state).
- Add tests (unit and integration) to validate mouse/keyboard behaviour and prevent regressions.

Acceptance criteria:
- New module boundaries documented and approved in PR description.
- Each logical area has its own file (UI components, opencode server client, SSE handler, layout helpers, types).
- Codebase compiles with no new TypeScript errors and existing tests pass.
- Regression tests added that capture the mouse-click crash scenario.

Initial pass findings (recorded as child tasks):
- See child tasks for individual opportunities and suggested scope.

Related: parent WL-0MKVZ5TN71L3YPD1
data.title: Refactor TUI: modularize and clean TUI code

data.description: Move UI element creation out of src/commands/tui.ts into modular components (List, Detail, Overlays, Dialogs, OpencodePane). Each component exposes lifecycle methods (create, show/hide, focus, destroy) and accepts typed props. This reduces file size and isolates visual logic for parallel work by multiple agents.
data.title: Extract TUI UI components into modules

data.description: Remove all HTTP/SSE server interaction (startOpencodeServer, createSession, sendPromptToServer, connectToSSE, sendInputResponse) into a dedicated module src/tui/opencode-client.ts. Provide a typed API, clear lifecycle, and tests for SSE parsing. This separates networking from UI logic.
data.title: Extract OpenCode server client and SSE handler

data.description: Replace wide 'any' types with specific interfaces (Item, VisibleNode, Pane, Dialog, ServerStatus). Add types for event handlers and opencode message parts. This improves compile-time safety and discoverability.
data.title: Tighten TypeScript types; remove 'any' usages in TUI

data.description: Search for places that reassign widget.style (e.g., opencodeText.style = {}), preserve existing style objects instead of replacing them, and add unit tests. Specifically fix opencodeText style replacement which caused 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading "bold")' from blessed. Add a lint rule or code review checklist to avoid direct style replacement.
data.title: Fix style mutation bug and audit style assignments

data.description: Identify duplicated layout logic (updateOpencodeInputLayout vs openOpencodeDialog), extract shared helpers (layout calculators, paneHeight, inputMaxHeight), and centralize constants (MIN_INPUT_HEIGHT, FOOTER_HEIGHT). Aim to reduce duplicated property assignments and ensure consistent behavior across modes.
data.title: Consolidate layout and remove duplicated layout code

data.description: Replace synchronous filesystem calls (fs.readFileSync, fs.writeFileSync, fs.existsSync) in state persistence with an async persistence module. Provide a small wrapper API for read/write state so UI code remains non-blocking and testable.
data.title: Refactor persistence: replace sync fs calls with async layer

data.description: Add unit/integration tests that exercise mouse events and ensure the TUI does not exit on clicks. Use a headless terminal runner (e.g., xterm.js or node-pty + test harness) to simulate click/mouse events and verify stability. Add a test that reproduces the opencodeText.style crash and asserts the fix.
data.title: Add regression tests for mouse click crash and TUI behavior

data.description: Move AVAILABLE_COMMANDS, keyboard shortcuts, and other magic values into a single src/tui/constants.ts. Replace inline arrays with references to constants and document each command. This simplifies changes and localization in future.
data.title: Centralize commands and constants

data.description:
Ensure all event listeners (.on, .once) registered on blessed widgets or processes are removed when widgets are destroyed or on program exit. Audit for potential memory leaks or dangling callbacks (e.g., opencodeServerProc listeners, SSE request handlers) and add cleanup hooks.

Acceptance Criteria:
1) Audit completed: list of files/locations where .on/.once are used and a short justification for each whether a cleanup hook is required.
2) SSE & HTTP streaming cleanup: opencode-client.connectToSSE must abort the request and remove response listeners when the stream is closed or when stopServer()/sendPrompt teardown occurs; add a unit test that simulates an SSE response and ensures no further payload handling after close.
3) Child process lifecycle: startServer()/stopServer() must attach and remove listeners safely; when stopServer() is called the child process should be killed and no stdout/stderr listeners remain.
4) TUI widget listeners: for at least two representative TUI components (dialogs and modals) add cleanup on destroy to remove listeners added to widgets and confirm with tests that repeated create/destroy cycles do not accumulate listeners.
5) Tests: Add unit tests that fail before the change and pass after (include a new test file tests/tui/event-cleanup.test.ts).
6) No new ESLint/TypeScript errors; full test suite passes.

Notes: I propose to start by auditing occurrences and updating this work item with the audit results, then implement targeted fixes with tests. If you approve I will proceed to add the audit as a worklog comment and create small commits on branch feature/WL-0MKX5ZVGH0MM4QI3-event-cleanup.,
data.title: Improve event listener lifecycle and cleanup

The list currently registers overlapping handlers for selection and click events (select, select item, click, click with data). This causes multiple render/update paths to fire and makes behavior harder to reason about.

data.description:
Summary:
The TUI reads rendered lines from blessed private fields (_clines.real/_clines.fake) in getRenderedLineAtClick/getRenderedLineAtScreen. This is brittle across blessed versions and increases maintenance risk.

- Click-to-open details still works reliably.

data.description:

Clipboard copy uses spawnSync for pbcopy/clip/xclip/xsel in the UI thread. This is blocking and platform-specific logic is inline in tui.ts.

data.description:

data.description:

Exit logic is duplicated for q/C-c/escape and includes direct process.exit calls. This should be centralized to guarantee cleanup (persist state, stop server, destroy screen).

data.description:

The file maintains extensive mutable module-level state (items, expanded, showClosed, opencode state). This complicates reasoning and refactor work.

data.description:

data.description:
## Problem
Users need predictable, customizable sort order for work items representing actual work sequence. Current priority/date ordering doesn't capture nuanced sequencing needs, and custom ordering decisions cannot be persisted.

## Users
- **Contributors**: Need work items in execution order to pick next task efficiently
- **Producers**: Need to set/maintain custom ordering reflecting untracked dependencies and priorities
- **Team members**: Need consistent, deterministic ordering across views for planning

## Success criteria
- `wl list` and `wl next` return items in a consistent, deterministic order based on `sort_index` field
- Users can reorder items using `wl move` commands with changes persisted to database
- Custom order is maintained across filters unless explicitly overridden with `--sort` flag
- TUI supports interactive reordering with keyboard shortcuts
- Migration preserves logical ordering of existing items using current "next item" calculation logic
- System handles up to 1000 items per hierarchy level efficiently
- Sort order syncs correctly across team members via Git
- Documentation updated to explain new sort behavior and commands
- Regression tests added for sort operations

## Constraints
- Must maintain backward compatibility with existing CLI commands
- Sort_index gaps must use large intervals (100s) to minimize reindexing
- Parent items moving must bring their children as a group
- Reindexing on sync must preserve relative ordering while resolving conflicts
- Database schema changes require migration for existing installations
- Performance must remain acceptable for up to 1000 items per level

## Risks & Assumptions
- **Risk**: Migration failure could corrupt existing work item ordering
- **Risk**: Concurrent edits by multiple users could create sort_index conflicts
- **Risk**: Large hierarchies (>1000 items) may experience performance degradation
- **Risk**: Gap exhaustion between frequently reordered items may trigger frequent reindexing
- **Assumption**: Current "next item" logic can be extracted and reused for sort calculation
- **Assumption**: SQLite can efficiently handle index-based sorting at scale
- **Assumption**: Users will understand the difference between custom order and priority-based order
- **Mitigation**: Include rollback capability in migration script
- **Mitigation**: Add performance benchmarks before/after implementation

## Existing state
- Work items currently ordered by combination of priority and creation date
- No persistent custom ordering capability
- `wl list` and `wl next` use different ordering logic
- No ability to manually reorder items
- TUI displays items in tree structure but doesn't support reordering

## Desired change
- Add integer `sort_index` field to work items table
- Implement hierarchical sorting where items are ordered by sort_index within their level
- Add CLI commands: `wl move <id> --before <id>` and `wl move <id> --after <id>`
- Calculate initial sort_index values using existing "next item" calculation logic applied hierarchically (level 0 items first, then level 1 under each parent, etc.)
- New items inserted at appropriate position using same "next item" calculation for their hierarchy level
- Add TUI keyboard shortcuts for interactive reordering (accessible to all users)
- Implement both automatic and manual (`wl move auto`) redistribution when gaps exhausted
- Add `--sort` flag to override default ordering (e.g., `--sort=priority`, `--sort=created`)
- Reindex automatically after sync operations to resolve conflicts

## Likely duplicates / related docs
- README.md (contains existing CLI command documentation)
- src/database.ts (database schema and operations)
- src/commands/list.ts (current list implementation)
- src/commands/helpers.ts (likely contains sorting/next item logic)
- src/commands/next.ts (next item selection logic to extract)
- src/tui/components/ (TUI components for keyboard interaction)
- AGENTS.md (work item management documentation)

## Related work items
- WL-0MKVZ55PR0LTMJA1: Epic: CLI usability & output (parent epic)
- WL-0MKWYVATG0G0I029: Sort order for work item list (current work item/user story)

## Recommended next step
NEW PRD at: docs/prd/sort_order_PRD.md (no existing PRD found for this feature)
data.title: Implement sort_index field and custom ordering for work items

This epic will be the parent for existing TUI epics and tasks such as: WL-0MKVZ5TN71L3YPD1 (Epic: TUI UX improvements), WL-0MKW7SLB30BFCL5O (Epic: Opencode server + interactive 'O' pane), and other TUI-focused work.

data.description:
Summary:
When a user types text into the opencode prompt box but does not send it to OpenCode and then closes the opencode UI, the typed content is lost. The prompt input should be preserved so that when the opencode UI is opened again the previous unsent content is still present in the input box.

Expected behaviour:
- If the user has entered text in the opencode prompt and closes the opencode UI without sending, the text is persisted in local session state and restored on next open.
- Persistence should not auto-send or otherwise change the pending input.
- Clearing or sending the input should behave as today (sent input clears the stored draft).

Acceptance criteria:
1) Typing text into opencode input and closing the pane preserves the text and shows it when reopened.
2) Sending the input clears the preserved draft.
3) Behavior documented briefly in TUI usage notes.

Notes:
- Prefer storing the draft in-memory for the TUI session; optionally persist to disk only if session-level persistence is desired.
- Ensure no sensitive data leakage if persisted to disk (prefer ephemeral behavior).
data.title: preserve prompt input when closing opencode UI

data.description:
Summary:
Currently the opencode prompt input box is resized when the user explicitly triggers a resize (e.g. Ctrl+Enter), but it does NOT resize when a typed line naturally word-wraps to the next visual line. This causes the input box to overflow or hide content and makes editing large multi-line prompts awkward.

Expected behaviour:
- The prompt input box should automatically expand vertically when the current input wraps onto additional visual lines, keeping the caret and the newly wrapped content visible.
- Manual resize on Ctrl+Enter should continue to work as before.
- Expansion should be bounded by a sensible maximum (e.g. a configurable max rows or available terminal height) and fall back to scrolling within the input if the maximum is reached.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Typing a long line that word-wraps causes the input box to grow to accommodate the wrapped lines up to the configured max height.
2) When max height is reached, additional wrapped content scrolls inside the input box rather than being clipped off-screen.
3) Ctrl+Enter manual resize remains functional and consistent with automatic resize.
4) Behavior verified on common terminals (xterm, gnome-terminal) and documented briefly in TUI notes.

Notes:
- Prefer an in-memory UI-only solution (no disk persistence) and keep the resize logic decoupled from opencode server communication.
- Consider accessibility and keyboard-only flows (ensure resizing does not steal focus or keyboard input).
data.title: Prompt input box must resize on word wrap

data.description:
Implement real-time progress feedback for OpenCode interactions in the TUI by displaying current activity status and using visual indicators.

## Context

The TUI currently shows basic 'waiting' indicator in prompt label when OpenCode is processing. This work item enhances that to provide richer, real-time feedback about what OpenCode is doing.

Related Work Items:
- Parent: WL-0MKXJETY41FOERO2 (Epic: Platform - TUI)
- Related: WL-0MKW7SLB30BFCL5O (Epic: Opencode server + interactive 'O' pane) - completed
- Discovered-from: Recent commit 1826786 (blocking duplicate prompts)

Related Files:
- src/commands/tui.ts (lines ~973-1270: SSE event handling in connectToSSE function)
- docs/opencode-tui.md (TUI documentation)

## Problem

Users cannot see what OpenCode is currently doing when processing requests. The only feedback is a generic '(waiting...)' label. This creates uncertainty about whether OpenCode is thinking, reading files, writing code, or stuck.

## Solution

Enhance progress feedback using OpenCode's SSE event stream:

### 1. Response Pane Header Progress (Moderate Detail)
Display current activity in the response pane's title/header area:
- Update on activity change only (not every text chunk)
- Show moderate detail: 'Thinking...', 'Using tool: read', 'Writing files', 'Running command'
- Clear when operation completes

### 2. Prompt Label Spinner
Add animated Braille dots spinner after 'waiting' text:
- Pattern: ‚Ýã ‚Ýô ‚Ýπ ‚Ý∏ ‚Ýº ‚Ý¥ ‚Ý¶ ‚Ýß ‚Ýá ‚Ýè
- Shows during any processing activity
- Stops and clears when ready

### 3. Inline Messages (Detailed, Selective)
Insert detailed messages into response stream for:
- File operations: '[Writing: src/file.ts]', '[Editing: package.json]', '[Deleted: temp.js]'
- Questions/input requests: Already handled, ensure formatting consistency

## Event Types to Handle

Based on OpenCode SSE stream analysis:

### session.status (Primary State Indicator)
- status.type: 'busy' ‚Üí Show spinner + activity
- status.type: 'idle' ‚Üí Clear progress, stop spinner

Example events:
```json
{"type": "session.status", "properties": {"sessionId": "abc123", "status": {"type": "busy"}}}
{"type": "session.status", "properties": {"sessionId": "abc123", "status": {"type": "idle"}}}
{"type": "session.status", "properties": {"sessionId": "abc123", "status": {"type": "busy", "activity": "thinking"}}}
```

### message.part.updated (Activity Details)
- part.type: 'text' ‚Üí Header: 'Writing response...'
- part.type: 'tool-use' + tool.name ‚Üí Header: 'Using tool: {name}', Inline: '[Using {name}: {description}]' (for file ops only)
- part.type: 'tool-result' ‚Üí Header: 'Processing result...', Inline: Show file ops results
- part.type: 'step-start' ‚Üí Header: Show step description

Example events:
```json
{"type": "message.part.updated", "properties": {"part": {"type": "text", "text": "Let me help...", "messageID": "m1"}}}
{"type": "message.part.updated", "properties": {"part": {"type": "tool-use", "tool": {"name": "read", "description": "Reading file.ts"}, "messageID": "m1"}}}
{"type": "message.part.updated", "properties": {"part": {"type": "tool-use", "tool": {"name": "write", "description": "Writing src/test.ts"}, "messageID": "m1"}}}
{"type": "message.part.updated", "properties": {"part": {"type": "tool-result", "content": "File written successfully", "messageID": "m1"}}}
```

### message.updated (Message Lifecycle)
- Track message completion via time.completed field
- Clear progress when assistant message completes

Example events:
```json
{"type": "message.updated", "properties": {"info": {"id": "m1", "role": "assistant", "time": {"started": 1234567890}}}}
{"type": "message.updated", "properties": {"info": {"id": "m1", "role": "assistant", "time": {"started": 1234567890, "completed": 1234567900}}}}
{"type": "message.updated", "properties": {"info": {"id": "m2", "role": "user", "time": {"started": 1234567880, "completed": 1234567881}}}}
```

### question.asked (Already Handled)
- Ensure inline message formatting is consistent
- Example already in code at line ~1154

## Implementation Approach

1. Add spinner animation to prompt label
   - Use setInterval for Braille dots animation
   - Start when isWaitingForResponse = true
   - Stop when isWaitingForResponse = false
   - Update label: 'OpenCode (waiting {spinner})' or 'OpenCode Prompt'

2. Add activity tracking in connectToSSE
   - Track current activity state (idle, thinking, using_tool, writing, etc.)
   - Add function to update response pane title/label based on activity
   - Update only when activity changes (debounce)

3. Enhance event handlers (lines ~1053-1253)
   - session.status: Update activity state, start/stop spinner
   - message.part.updated: Update activity based on part.type and tool.name
   - For write/edit/delete tools: append inline message to stream
   - message.updated: Check for completion, clear progress

4. Add cleanup on operation complete
   - Reset activity state to idle
   - Stop spinner animation
   - Clear response pane header progress
   - Restore default labels

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Response pane header shows current activity (thinking, using tool: X, writing, etc.)
- [x] Activity updates on state change only, not on every text chunk (smooth performance)
- [x] Prompt label shows animated Braille dots spinner during processing
- [x] Spinner stops when operation completes or errors
- [x] File operations (write, edit, delete) show inline messages with file names
- [x] Questions/input requests display with consistent inline formatting
- [ ] No UI flickering or performance degradation during high-frequency SSE events
- [x] Progress indicators clear properly when operation completes
- [ ] No regressions to existing features (autocomplete, input requests, streaming, etc.)
- [ ] Code follows existing TUI patterns and blessed.js conventions

## Testing

Manual testing:
1. Open TUI, press 'o' to open OpenCode
2. Send prompt: 'Read the package.json file and tell me the version'
   - Verify spinner appears in prompt label
   - Verify header shows 'Using tool: read'
   - Verify response streams correctly
   - Verify spinner stops when complete

3. Send prompt: 'Create a new file test.ts with a hello function'
   - Verify header shows 'Using tool: write'
   - Verify inline message: '[Writing: test.ts]'
   - Verify spinner animation is smooth
   - Verify everything clears when done

4. Send prompt while previous is processing
   - Verify blocked with toast message (existing feature)
   - Verify spinner continues for original request

5. Test with rapid streaming (long response)
   - Verify no flickering or performance issues
   - Verify header updates appropriately

## Updates
- Prompt spinner implemented in the OpenCode prompt label and clears on completion/errors.
- Default OpenCode port is now unset unless OPENCODE_SERVER_PORT is provided, allowing server auto-selection.
data.title: Enhanced OpenCode Progress Feedback in TUI

data.description:
When a persisted local history exists but the server session is missing, allow the user to restore context safely by generating a concise summary of the history and sending it as a single system/assistant prompt to the new server session. Flow:

1) Detect local history for selected work item.
2) Generate a one-paragraph summary of the persisted messages (auto-generate + allow user edit).
3) POST a single prompt to the new session with the summary: "Context: <summary>. Continue the conversation about work item <id>."
4) Mark the session as hydrated and persist the mapping.

Acceptance criteria:
- A feature work-item exists under the 'Opencode Integrtion' epic.
- TUI shows an option when local history exists: Show Only / Restore via Summary / Full Replay (disabled by default).
- Choosing Restore
data.title: Restore session via concise summary

data.description:
When the worklog database is modified (create/update/delete), the TUI should automatically refresh the work items tree so users see changes without manual refresh. Implementation notes:

- Detect DB writes (create/update/delete) and notify the running TUI instance. Options: emit events from DB layer, wrap db methods, or use filesystem watch on the DB file.
- Debounce refreshes to avoid excessive redraws during bulk operations (e.g. 200-500ms).
- Preserve current selection and expanded nodes where possible after refresh.
- Test by creating/updating/deleting items while TUI is open and verify the UI updates automatically.

Acceptance criteria:
- TUI refreshes automatically after create, update, delete operations without user action.
- Selection is preserved when possible; if the selected item is deleted, selection moves to a sensible neighbor.
- Debounce prevents excessive refreshes on bulk writes.
data.title: Auto-refresh TUI on DB write

data.description:
Validate and test the TUI OpenCode restore flow end-to-end.

Goal:
- Exercise and verify the safe restore UI when the OpenCode server session cannot be reused: Show-only / Restore via summary (editable) / Full replay (danger, require explicit YES).

Acceptance criteria:
- When no server session is reused, locally persisted history renders read-only.
- The modal offers: Show only / Restore via summary / Full replay / Cancel.
- Restore via summary: opens an editable summary; if accepted, server receives a single prompt containing the summary + user's prompt.
- Full replay: requires user to type YES; replaying may re-run tools; confirm side-effects are explicit.
- SSE handling: finalPrompt is used so SSE does not echo redundant messages and the conversation resumes correctly.

Files/paths of interest:
- src/commands/tui.ts
- .worklog/tui-state.json
- .worklog/worklog-data.jsonl

Steps to test manually:
1) Start or let TUI start opencode server on port 9999.
2) In TUI, create an OpenCode conversation while attached to a work item (sends a prompt & persists mapping + history).
3) Stop the opencode server.
4) Re-open TUI and open OpenCode for same work item; verify the persisted history appears and the restore modal is shown.
5) Test each modal choice and observe server behaviour.

If issues are found, create child work-items for fixing UI/behavior and attach logs/steps to reproduce.
data.title: Test: TUI OpenCode restore flow

data.description:
# Delegate to GitHub Coding Agent

Add a `wl github delegate <work-item-id>` subcommand that pushes a Worklog work item to GitHub (creating or updating the issue as needed) and assigns it to the GitHub Copilot Coding Agent (`copilot`), enabling one-command delegation of work to Copilot from the Worklog CLI.

## Problem Statement

There is no way to delegate a Worklog work item to GitHub Copilot Coding Agent from the CLI. Users must manually push the item to GitHub with `wl github push`, then navigate to the GitHub UI (or run separate `gh` commands) to assign the issue to Copilot. This multi-step process is error-prone and slow, especially when delegating multiple items.

## Users

- **Worklog CLI users** who want to offload implementation work to GitHub Copilot Coding Agent.
  - *As a developer, I want to delegate a work item to Copilot with a single command so that I can quickly hand off well-defined tasks without leaving my terminal.*
  - *As a team lead, I want the local Worklog state to reflect that an item has been delegated so that team dashboards and `wl in-progress` queries accurately show who is working on what.*

## Success Criteria

1. Running `wl github delegate <work-item-id>` pushes the work item to GitHub (smart sync: only if stale or not yet created) and assigns the resulting issue to the `copilot` GitHub user.
2. The local Worklog item is updated: status set to `in_progress`, assignee set to `@github-copilot` (or the appropriate local convention).
3. If the work item has a `do-not-delegate` tag, the command warns and exits unless `--force` is provided.
4. If the work item has children, the command warns about them and lets the user decide whether to proceed (delegates only the specified item by default).
5. The command supports both human-readable output (progress messages + GitHub issue URL) and `--json` output, consistent with other `wl github` subcommands.
6. If the GitHub assignment fails (e.g., `copilot` user not available on the repository), the command reports a clear error and does not update local Worklog state.

## Constraints

- The delegation target is hardcoded to the `copilot` GitHub user. No configurable target is needed at this time.
- Assignment is done via `gh issue edit --add-assignee copilot` using the existing `gh` CLI wrapper infrastructure in `src/github.ts`.
- Must reuse the existing `wl github push` sync logic (`upsertIssuesFromWorkItems` in `src/github-sync.ts`) rather than reimplementing push behavior.
- Must respect the existing incremental push pre-filter (`src/github-pre-filter.ts`) for the "smart sync" behavior.
- The command must be registered as a subcommand of the existing `wl github` command group in `src/commands/github.ts`.

## Existing State

- `wl github push` can push work items to GitHub issues (create/update), including labels, comments, and parent-child hierarchy.
- The `workItemToIssuePayload()` function in `src/github.ts` does **not** currently map the `assignee` field to the GitHub issue payload.
- The `do-not-delegate` tag exists as a local convention (toggle via `wl update --do-not-delegate` or TUI `D` key) but has no effect on GitHub operations.
- No `gh issue edit --add-assignee` calls exist anywhere in the codebase.
- The `gh` CLI wrapper (`runGh`, `runGhAsync`, etc.) in `src/github.ts` supports running arbitrary `gh` subcommands with rate-limit retry/backoff.

## Desired Change

- Add a `delegate` subcommand to the `wl github` command group.
- The command flow:
  1. Resolve the work item by ID.
  2. Check for `do-not-delegate` tag; warn and exit unless `--force`.
  3. Check for children; warn and let the user decide.
  4. Push the work item to GitHub using the existing sync logic (smart sync: skip if already up to date).
  5. Assign the GitHub issue to `copilot` via `gh issue edit <issue-number> --add-assignee copilot`.
  6. Update local Worklog state: set status to `in_progress` and assignee to `@github-copilot`.
  7. Output success message with GitHub issue URL (human) or structured result (JSON).
- Add a new `assignGithubIssue` (or similar) helper function to `src/github.ts` to wrap the `gh issue edit --add-assignee` call.

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Assumption: `copilot` user is available.** The target repository must have GitHub Copilot Coding Agent enabled and the `copilot` user must be assignable. If not, the `gh issue edit --add-assignee` call will fail. Mitigation: clear error message (covered by SC #6).
- **Assumption: `gh` CLI is authenticated.** The existing `gh` wrapper handles auth, but delegation requires write access to the repository. Mitigation: rely on existing `gh` auth error reporting.
- **Risk: scope creep toward generic delegation.** The current scope is Copilot-only; future requests may ask for configurable targets, batch delegation, or automatic Copilot session monitoring. Mitigation: record these as separate work items linked to this one rather than expanding scope.
- **Risk: children warning UX in non-interactive mode.** When running with `--json` or in a pipeline, interactive prompts ("delegate children too?") may not be appropriate. Mitigation: default to single-item-only in non-interactive mode; require explicit flag for recursive behavior if added later.
- **Assumption: smart sync reuses existing pre-filter.** The incremental push pre-filter compares timestamps to decide whether to push. If the pre-filter skips an item that has never been pushed, the delegate command must still create the GitHub issue. This should work since `upsertIssuesFromWorkItems` creates issues for items without a `githubIssueNumber`, but this path should be tested.

## Related work (automated report)

### Work items

- **Scheduler: output recommendation when delegation audit_only=true (WL-0MLI9B5T20UJXCG9)** [open] -- The scheduler's delegation subsystem decides which items to delegate and to whom. The new `wl github delegate` command will be the mechanism for acting on those recommendations when the target is Copilot. These two features are complementary: the scheduler recommends, delegate executes.

- **Do not auto-assign shortcut (WL-0MLHNPSGP0N397NX)** [completed] -- Introduced the `do-not-delegate` tag and the TUI `D` toggle. The delegate command must respect this tag (SC #3), so the tag's semantics and storage (in the `tags` array) are a direct dependency for the guard-rail logic.

- **Next Tasks Queue (WL-0MLI9QBK10K76WQZ)** [open] -- Defines a priority queue that the scheduler and agents use to select the next item to work on. Items promoted via this queue may be prime candidates for delegation to Copilot, making the queue a natural upstream for the delegate command in future automation flows.

### Repository files

- **`src/commands/github.ts`** -- Registers the `wl github push` and `wl github import` subcommands. The new `delegate` subcommand will be added here alongside them, following the same registration pattern and sharing the config/output infrastructure.

- **`src/github-sync.ts`** -- Contains `upsertIssuesFromWorkItems()`, the push engine that creates/updates GitHub issues. The delegate command will call this function for its smart-sync step rather than reimplementing push logic.

- **`src/github.ts`** -- Low-level GitHub API layer with `runGh`/`runGhAsync` wrappers, issue CRUD, label management, and hierarchy operations. A new `assignGithubIssue` helper will be added here to wrap `gh issue edit --add-assignee`. No assignee-related API calls currently exist in this file.

- **`src/github-pre-filter.ts`** -- Implements the incremental push pre-filter that skips items unchanged since the last push. The delegate command's smart-sync behavior depends on this filter to avoid redundant API calls.

- **`src/commands/update.ts`** -- Implements `--do-not-delegate` flag that adds/removes the tag. Relevant as the authoritative CLI surface for the tag that the delegate command's guard rail checks.

- **`docs/tutorials/03-building-a-plugin.md`** -- Plugin authoring guide. Relevant if the delegate command is considered for extraction as a plugin in the future, though the current plan is to implement it as a built-in subcommand.

data.description:
## Headline
Add a local shell execution path for OpenCode prompt input that begins with `!`, streaming raw output to the response pane and allowing Ctrl+C cancellation without leaving the TUI.

## Problem statement
Users need a fast, low-friction way to run ad-hoc shell commands from the OpenCode prompt without leaving the TUI. Today the OpenCode prompt only sends text to the OpenCode server, so local command execution is out of band.

## Users
- TUI users who want quick command execution while reviewing or updating work items.

Example user stories
- As a TUI user, I want to type `!ls` in the OpenCode prompt to run the command locally and see the output in the response pane.
- As a keyboard-first user, I want to cancel a long-running `!` command with Ctrl+C without closing the prompt.

## Success criteria
- A prompt starting with `!` is interpreted as a local shell command and is not sent to the OpenCode server.
- The command runs in the project root and streams stdout/stderr to the response pane as raw output.
- Ctrl+C cancels a running `!` command without exiting the TUI.
- `!` handling is a hard prefix only (only when the first character is `!`).

## Constraints
- Use the default system shell.
- No confirmation dialog required.
- Do not attach OpenCode context or work item context to `!` command execution.
- Do not decorate output with exit codes or error labels; show raw stdout/stderr only.

## Existing state
- The OpenCode prompt sends text to the OpenCode server and streams responses in the response pane.
- The response pane already supports streaming output and focus management.

## Desired change
- Add a local shell execution path for prompts prefixed with `!` in the OpenCode prompt.
- Stream output to the response pane and allow Ctrl+C cancellation.

## Related work
- `docs/opencode-tui.md` ‚Äî documents OpenCode prompt behavior and response pane usage.
- `TUI.md` ‚Äî lists OpenCode prompt access and response pane behavior.
- Integrated Shell (WL-0MKYX0V5M13IC9XZ) ‚Äî current work item for this intake.
- TUI (WL-0MKXJETY41FOERO2) ‚Äî parent epic for TUI work.

## Risks & assumptions
- Assumes the default system shell is available and safe to invoke for local execution.
- Long-running commands may emit large output; streaming should not degrade TUI responsiveness.

## Suggested next step
- Define implementation approach and tests for `!` command execution in the OpenCode prompt and response pane handling.

## Related work (automated report)

- TUI (WL-0MKXJETY41FOERO2) ‚Äî parent epic for TUI work, including OpenCode prompt and response pane behavior.
- Send OpenCode prompts to server instead of CLI (WL-0MKWCQQIW0ZP4A67) ‚Äî establishes OpenCode prompt routing and streaming responses in the TUI.
- Auto-start OpenCode server if not running (WL-0MKWCW9K610XPQ1P) ‚Äî manages OpenCode server lifecycle used by the prompt flow.
- Prompt input box must resize on word wrap (WL-0MKXJPCDI1FLYDB1) ‚Äî impacts OpenCode prompt input behavior in the TUI.
- TUI: Escape key behavior for opencode input and response panes (WL-0MKX6L9IB03733Y9) ‚Äî defines key handling for prompt input and response pane focus/closure.

Docs:
- /home/rogardle/projects/Worklog/docs/opencode-tui.md ‚Äî documents OpenCode prompt behavior and response pane usage.
- /home/rogardle/projects/Worklog/TUI.md ‚Äî documents TUI controls, including response pane behavior.

data.description:
# TUI: Reparent items without typing IDs

Add a keyboard-driven move/reparent mode to the TUI tree view so users can reorganize work items under different parents (or detach to root) without copying or typing item IDs.

## Problem Statement

Reorganizing the work item hierarchy in the TUI currently requires the user to manually copy or type worklog item IDs to reparent items via the CLI (`wl update <id> --parent <target-id>`). This is slow, error-prone, and breaks the keyboard-driven workflow the TUI is designed to support.

## Users

**Primary:** TUI power users who manage and reorganize work item hierarchies regularly.

**User stories:**

- As a TUI user, I want to move a work item to a different parent by selecting items visually, so I can reorganize my work hierarchy without leaving the TUI or typing IDs.
- As a TUI user, I want to detach an item from its parent (move to root), so I can promote items to top-level when they no longer belong under a specific parent.
- As a TUI user, I want clear visual feedback during a move operation, so I know which item I am moving and what the target will be.

## Success Criteria

1. **Move mode activation:** In the main tree view, pressing `m` on a selected item enters move mode with the source item visually highlighted and footer instructions displayed.
2. **Target selection and confirmation:** Navigating to a valid target and pressing `m` (or Enter) executes `wl update <source-id> --parent <target-id>`, refreshes the tree, and auto-expands the new parent to show the moved item. A toast confirms success or shows an error.
3. **Unparent (detach to root):** Selecting the source item itself as the target while in move mode detaches it from its parent (moves to root level).
4. **Invalid target prevention:** Descendants of the source item are greyed out / non-selectable in move mode to prevent circular parent-child relationships.
5. **Cancellation:** Pressing `Esc` during move mode cancels the operation, leaving all items unchanged and restoring normal navigation.

## Constraints

- **Scope:** Main tree view only; move mode does not need to work in filtered views or other panes.
- **Keyboard-first:** The feature must be fully operable via keyboard. Mouse support is optional and out-of-scope for initial implementation.
- **No batch moves:** Multi-select / batch move is a future enhancement, out-of-scope for this work item.
- **Existing patterns:** Implementation should follow the established TUI module patterns from the completed refactor (WL-0MKX5ZBUR1MIA4QN) and keyboard navigation patterns from the update dialog work (WL-0ML1K74OM0FNAQDU).

## Existing State

- The TUI is modularized into components under `src/tui/` (list, detail, overlays, dialogs, opencode pane) with a `TuiController` class.
- Existing keybindings include `C` (copy ID), `R` (refresh), `I/A/B` (filters), `U` (update dialog), `N` (next item), `p` (parent preview), arrow keys, Enter, Esc.
- The `m` key is currently unbound.
- Reparenting is supported by the CLI via `wl update <id> --parent <target-id>` but has no TUI affordance.
- A previous deleted work item (WL-0MKXUOYLN1I9J5T3) proposed a `wl move` CLI command with `--before/--after` semantics; this was deleted and is not related to the current feature.

## Desired Change

- Add a `MoveMode` state to the TUI tree view component.
- When `m` is pressed on a selected item, enter move mode: store the source item, highlight it distinctly, grey out invalid targets (descendants), and display instructions in the footer ("Move mode: select target and press 'm' to confirm; Esc to cancel").
- Navigation continues normally in move mode (arrow keys to move cursor through the tree).
- Pressing `m` or Enter on a valid target executes the reparent via `wl update <source-id> --parent <target-id>`, shows a success/error toast, refreshes the tree, and auto-expands the new parent.
- Pressing `m` or Enter on the source item itself (self-select) detaches the item from its parent (unparent to root).
- Pressing `Esc` cancels move mode and restores normal navigation.
- Errors from the `wl update` command are surfaced via toast and the UI remains consistent.

## Risks & Assumptions

**Assumptions:**
- The `wl update <id> --parent <target-id>` CLI command handles the backend logic for reparenting correctly, including removing the old parent relationship.
- The `wl update` command supports clearing the parent (setting to no parent / root) via an appropriate flag or empty value.
- The tree view component exposes sufficient API to programmatically expand a specific node after refresh.

**Risks:**
- If `wl update` does not support clearing the parent field, the unparent-to-root feature will require a CLI change first (mitigation: verify CLI capability early in implementation).
- Move mode introduces a new modal state in the TUI; if other keybindings are not properly suppressed during move mode, unintended actions could occur (mitigation: ensure move mode captures/blocks conflicting keys).
- Greying out descendants requires traversing the item tree to identify all descendants of the source; for deeply nested hierarchies this could have a minor performance cost (mitigation: the traversal is in-memory and the item count is typically small).

## Suggested Next Step

Plan and break down the implementation into sub-tasks under WL-0MKZ34IDI0XTA5TB, then implement starting with the move mode state and keybinding in the tree view component.

## Related Work

- **TUI** (WL-0MKXJETY41FOERO2) -- parent epic
- **Refactor TUI: modularize and clean TUI code** (WL-0MKX5ZBUR1MIA4QN) -- completed; established module structure
- **M2: Field Navigation & Submission** (WL-0ML1K74OM0FNAQDU) -- completed; established keyboard nav patterns in dialogs
- **TUI: Reparent items without typing IDs** (WL-0MKZ3531R13CYBTD) -- duplicate, deleted
- **Implement wl move CLI** (WL-0MKXUOYLN1I9J5T3) -- deleted; different scope (sort order, not reparent)

## Related work (automated report)

The following items were identified as related through automated keyword and code analysis. Items already listed in the manual Related Work section are excluded.

### Work items

- **Tree State Module** (WL-0MLARGFZH1QRH8UG) -- Extracted tree-building logic into `src/tui/state.ts` including `buildVisibleNodes`, expand/collapse state, and parent/child mapping. Move mode will need to interact directly with this module to identify descendants and manage tree state after reparenting.

- **Interaction Handlers** (WL-0MLARGYVG0CLS1S9) -- Extracted keybinding and interaction handling into `src/tui/handlers.ts`. The new `m` keybinding for move mode should follow the patterns established in this module.

- **TuiController Class** (WL-0MLARH59Q0FY64WN) -- Created `src/tui/controller.ts` composing state, handlers, and UI components. Move mode state and the `m` keybinding will be wired through the controller.

- **Refactor TUI keyboard handler into reusable chord system** (WL-0ML04S0SZ1RSMA9F) -- Created `src/tui/chords.ts` for chord-based keybindings. The `m` key binding may leverage this system if move mode is treated as a chord/modal sequence.

- **Extract TUI keyboard shortcuts to constants** (WL-0MLK58NHL1G8EQZP) -- Centralized keyboard shortcut constants in `src/tui/constants.ts`. The new `m` key constant should be added here.

- **Add TUI parent preview shortcut** (WL-0MKVVJWPP0BR7V9S) -- The `p` shortcut for parent preview navigates parent relationships and provides a precedent for parent-aware TUI interactions.

- **TUI: interactive reordering and keyboard shortcuts** (WL-0MKXTSXGN0O9424R) -- Deleted work item that proposed keyboard-based reordering including move-to-parent; overlapped in scope with this reparent feature.

### Repository code

- `src/tui/controller.ts` -- TuiController class; keybindings, parent navigation logic (lines ~1842-1847, ~2232-2274, ~2491-2495). Primary file for adding move mode keybinding and state.
- `src/tui/state.ts` -- Tree state building with `parentId`-based parent/child mapping (lines ~32, 40, 91-93). Needed for descendant identification and tree rebuild after reparent.
- `src/tui/types.ts` -- Item type definition with `parentId` field (line ~40). Type definitions for any new move mode state.
- `src/tui/handlers.ts` -- Interaction handlers module; patterns for new keybindings.
- `src/tui/constants.ts` -- Centralized keyboard shortcut constants; add `m` key constant here.
- `src/tui/chords.ts` -- Keyboard chord handler module; potential integration point for modal move mode.
data.title: TUI: Reparent items without typing IDs

data.description:
Summary:
Provide a keyboard-driven way in the TUI to reparent (move) worklog items without copying or typing item IDs.

User story:
As a user of the TUI, I want to move an item to a different parent without having to copy or manually type any worklog IDs, so I can reorganize items quickly using only keyboard (or mouse) selection.

Expected behaviour:
- The user selects an item in the TUI (the item to move).
- The user triggers a move action (suggested key: m).
- The UI enters a move-target selection mode (visually highlighted), allowing the user to select a new parent item.
- The user confirms the target by hitting the same key again (or an explicit Confirm action).
- The client performs `wl update <source-id> --parent <target-id>` and updates the UI to reflect the new parent.
- The action is cancellable (Esc or explicit Cancel) and shows a small confirmation/undo affordance or success/failure message.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Start from any TUI view that lists items. Select an item and press the move key; the UI clearly indicates move mode.
2) Selecting a target and confirming runs the equivalent "wl update <source> --parent <target>" and the item appears under the new parent in the UI.
3) Cancelling move mode leaves items unchanged and returns the UI to normal navigation.
4) Errors from `wl update` are surfaced to the user and the UI remains consistent.
5) The feature can be exercised entirely with keyboard.

Suggested implementation details:
- Add a move-mode state to the TUI. When active, the selected source item is stored and the UI highlights potential target items.
- Use a single key to toggle move-mode and also confirm the selection (eg. `m` to start, `m` to confirm). Allow Enter as alternative confirm.
- On confirm, run: `wl update <SOURCE-ID> --parent <TARGET-ID>` using existing client logic that issues wl commands. Show a short success or error toast.
- Provide clear visual affordances: source highlight, target highlight, instructions in the footer ("Move mode: select target and press 'm' to confirm; Esc to cancel").
- Consider multi-select or batch move as future enhancement (out-of-scope for initial implementation).

Notes:
- This request is intentionally flexible about exact keybindings or UI details ‚Äî the above is a suggested flow. Other UXs that avoid typing IDs are acceptable.

Related tags: tui, ux, feature

data.description:
Create a generalized keyboard handler that can manage multi-key sequences (chords) to support future vim-style keybindings beyond just Ctrl-W.

## Context
This work item is a follow-up to WL-0MKVZ3RBL10DFPPW which implemented Ctrl-W window navigation using scattered key handling logic in tui.ts. The current implementation works but would benefit from being refactored into a reusable, extensible keyboard handler.

**Related Issue:** WL-0MKVZ3RBL10DFPPW
**Commit:** f2b4117e9e16d747d019c3f6df5cfcc6a28a3f7c

## Current Implementation Files
The following files contain keyboard handling logic that should be refactored:
- src/commands/tui.ts (primary implementation)
- src/tui/components/detail.ts
- src/tui/components/help-menu.ts
- src/tui/components/list.ts
- src/tui/components/opencode-pane.ts
- TUI.md (documentation)
- docs/opencode-tui.md (OpenCode-specific docs)
- test/tui-integration.test.ts (tests)

## Goals
1. Extract keyboard chord handling into a reusable  class or module
2. Create a registry/mapping system for chord definitions (e.g., 'Ctrl-W' -> { 'w': action, 'h': action, ... })
3. Support configurable timeouts for pending keys
4. Support for modifier keys, nested chords, and edge cases
5. Improve testability of keyboard sequences in isolation
6. Enable future vim-style keybindings (g chord, z chord, etc.)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Keyboard handler abstraction created and documented
- [ ] Ctrl-W implementation refactored to use the new handler
- [ ] All existing keyboard shortcuts continue to work as before
- [ ] All 201+ tests pass
- [ ] Handler supports at least 2 different chord types (Ctrl-W and one other example)
- [ ] Code is well-tested with unit tests for chord matching and sequence handling
data.title: Refactor TUI keyboard handler into reusable chord system

data.description:
When wl init runs in a new git worktree, it incorrectly places .worklog in the main repository instead of the worktree. 

The issue is in resolveWorklogDir() in src/worklog-paths.ts. Currently, the function uses git rev-parse --show-toplevel which returns the main repository root even when called from within a worktree. This causes all worktrees to share the same .worklog directory, leading to data conflicts and initialization failures.

The solution is to detect when we're in a git worktree (by checking if .git is a file rather than a directory) and skip the repo-root .worklog lookup in that case.

## Acceptance Criteria
- wl init in a new worktree places .worklog in the worktree directory, not the main repo
- wl init in the main repository still places .worklog in the main repo
- wl init in a subdirectory of the main repo finds the repo-root .worklog if it exists
- Each worktree maintains independent worklog state
- All existing tests pass
- Code handles both main repos and worktrees correctly

## Testing Scenarios
1. Initialize worklog in main repo - .worklog created in main repo root
2. Initialize worklog in existing worktree - .worklog created in worktree root
3. Run wl commands in worktree - uses worktree's .worklog, not main repo's
4. Switch between worktrees - each maintains separate state

## Related Files
- src/worklog-paths.ts (getRepoRoot, resolveWorklogDir)
- src/config.ts (uses resolveWorklogDir)
- tests/ (add worktree-specific tests)
data.title: Fix wl init to support git worktrees

data.description:
When git worktree add or git checkout triggers the post-checkout hook, wl sync fails silently with 'sync failed or not initialized; continuing'. 

For users who just created a new worktree or cloned the repo, this message doesn't help them understand what to do next.

## Problem
Current behavior: 'worklog: sync failed or not initialized; continuing'
This doesn't tell the user that they need to run 'wl init' in the new worktree.

## Solution
Improve the error detection and output in:
1. The post-checkout hook script in src/commands/init.ts
2. The sync command error handling to distinguish between 'not initialized' vs 'sync failed'
3. Provide a helpful message like: 'worklog: not initialized in this checkout/worktree. Run "wl init" to set up this location.'

## Acceptance Criteria
- When wl sync fails due to missing initialization in a new worktree/checkout, display a helpful message
- Message should suggest running 'wl init'
- Message should be different from general sync failures

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Extend Update Dialog (Phase 2)

## Problem Statement

The current Update dialog (Phase 1, WL-0MKVYN4HW1AMQFAV) only supports quick stage changes via the `U` keyboard shortcut. Users need to quickly modify multiple essential work-item fields (status, priority, and add comments) without opening a full edit page. This phase extends the dialog to support these common quick-edits while maintaining keyboard accessibility and a compact UX.

## Users & User Stories

- **Producer/Agent triaging work:** As a producer, I want to quickly update a work-item's status and priority from the TUI list view (e.g., mark as blocked, escalate priority, add a note) without leaving the tree view.
- **Keyboard-focused user:** As a keyboard-first TUI user, I want to make multiple field changes in a single dialog session and submit them together (e.g., change stage to in_progress, status to open, and add a comment all at once).
- **Busy team lead:** As a team lead reviewing work-items, I want to add quick comments (multi-line notes) to items without switching contexts.

## Expected Behavior

- Pressing `U` with a work-item focused opens the expanded Update dialog.
- The dialog shows all fields: **stage** (existing), **status**, **priority**, and **comment**.
- All fields are visible in a single dialog (expanded height as needed).
- User navigates via Tab/Shift-Tab to select a field, arrow keys to navigate options, Enter to confirm selection.
- Status/stage combinations are validated (e.g., warn if user selects conflicting status/stage pairs like status=open + stage=done).
- Comment is a multi-line text box embedded in the dialog.
- On submit, all selected changes are sent in a single API call (reusing existing db.update).
- Dialog shows success feedback (toast) or errors if the update fails.
- Dialog remains keyboard accessible, mobile-friendly, and consistent with existing TUI patterns.

## Constraints

- Dialog must remain usable in typical terminal windows; both height (currently 14) and width (currently 50%) will need to increase to accommodate multiple fields and multi-line comment input. Consider reasonable maximums (e.g., 60% width, ~20-25 lines height) and plan for graceful degradation in smaller terminals.
- Only status, priority, and comment are in scope; **parent field is deferred to Phase 3**.
- Keyboard navigation must follow existing TUI patterns (Tab, arrow keys, Enter) used by the close dialog.
- Status/stage validation must happen on the frontend (show which combinations are invalid) to prevent backend errors.

## Existing State

- Phase 1 (WL-0MKVYN4HW1AMQFAV) implemented the basic Update dialog with stage-only quick-edit via the `U` shortcut.
- `src/tui/components/dialogs.ts:102‚Äì139` defines the update dialog structure; currently shows only stage options and is 14 lines tall.
- Tests exist (`tests/tui/tui-update-dialog.test.ts`) that verify stage-change updates via db.update().
- The close dialog (lines 63‚Äì99) provides a UX/pattern reference for how to structure the multi-option dialog.

## Desired Change

1. Extend `updateDialog` and `updateDialogOptions` in `dialogs.ts` to display and manage status, priority, and comment fields.
2. Increase dialog height and implement field navigation (Tab selects field, arrow keys navigate options within field, Enter confirms).
3. Add frontend validation logic to prevent invalid status/stage combinations; surface warnings in the UI.
4. Implement a multi-line comment input box within the dialog using blessed text input or similar.
5. Update the keyboard shortcut handler to collect changes from all fields and submit as a single db.update call.
6. Extend test coverage to verify multi-field edits, status/stage validation, and comment addition.
7. Update any documentation or in-TUI help text to reflect the new fields.

## Related Work

- **WL-0MKVYN4HW1AMQFAV** (Phase 1 - completed): Initial stage-only Update dialog implementation.
- **WL-0MKWDOMSL0B4UAZX** (completed): Tests for TUI quick-edit via shortcut U.
- **WL-0MKVZ5TN71L3YPD1** (parent epic): TUI UX improvements.
- **Document:** `src/tui/components/dialogs.ts` ‚Äî Update dialog component.
- **Document:** `tests/tui/tui-update-dialog.test.ts` ‚Äî Update dialog test patterns.

## Success Criteria

1. Update dialog displays status, priority, and comment fields alongside stage.
2. User can navigate fields with Tab/Shift-Tab and select options with arrow keys and Enter.
3. Status/stage combination validation is enforced (invalid combinations are disabled or warned about).
4. Multi-line comment text can be entered and submitted with other field changes.
5. All field changes are sent in a single db.update call and persist correctly.
6. Dialog is keyboard accessible and follows existing TUI keyboard patterns.
7. Tests cover multi-field edits, validation logic, and comment addition.
8. Dialog grows appropriately in both height and width; consider reasonable maximums (e.g., 60% width, ~20-25 lines height) with graceful degradation for smaller terminals.
9. Success/error feedback is shown to user (toast or dialog message).

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Risk:** Dialog height and width may become unwieldy if not carefully designed. **Mitigation:** Plan layout carefully; consider field grouping or scrolling within the dialog if needed. Start with reasonable dimensions (e.g., 60% width, ~20-25 lines) and adjust based on usability testing.
- **Assumption:** Status/stage combinations have well-defined rules in the codebase (e.g., only certain status values are valid for each stage). **Mitigation:** Verify these rules exist and document them before implementing validation.
- **Assumption:** The existing db.update API accepts multiple field changes in one call. **Mitigation:** Confirm this is supported; if not, batch calls or refactor the API.
- **Risk:** Comment input may conflict with dialog keyboard navigation (e.g., Tab inside comment box vs. Tab to next field). **Mitigation:** Use a blessed Box or similar that respects parent modal focus; test thoroughly.

## Suggested Next Step

Proceed to **Planning Phase**: Break this work into discrete sub-tasks (e.g., extend dialog UI, implement field navigation, add validation, implement comment input, add tests, update docs) and create child work-items for each sub-task.
data.title: Extend Update dialog to include additional fields (Phase 2)

data.description:
## Problem

Work items with 'in-progress' status are not appearing blue in the TUI tree view, despite having the correct status. This affects WL-0ML16W7000D2M8J3 and other in-progress items.

## Root Cause

The titleColorForStatus() function in src/commands/helpers.ts uses chalk to generate ANSI color codes, but blessed's list component doesn't properly interpret ANSI codes in text strings. Blessed expects its own markup tags (e.g. {cyan-fg}text{/cyan-fg}) instead.

When colored text with ANSI codes is passed to blessed, the codes are lost or misinterpreted, causing the TUI list to not display the correct colors.

## Solution

Replace chalk color codes with blessed markup tags in the titleColorForStatus() and renderTitle() functions. This allows blessed's built-in tag parser (tags: true is already enabled on the list component) to properly render the colors.

The mapping should be:
- 'in-progress' ‚Üí {cyan-fg}text{/cyan-fg} (was chalk.cyan)
- 'completed' ‚Üí {gray-fg}text{/gray-fg} (was chalk.gray)
- 'blocked' ‚Üí {red-fg}text{/red-fg} (was chalk.redBright)
- 'open' (default) ‚Üí {green-fg}text{/green-fg} (was chalk.greenBright)

Note: This change only affects TUI output. Other uses of chalk in helpers.ts (console output, conflict resolution display) should remain unchanged since those aren't going to blessed.

## Related Files

- src/commands/helpers.ts - titleColorForStatus() and renderTitle() functions (lines 61-80)
- src/commands/tui.ts - uses formatTitleOnly() to render tree items (line 949)
- src/tui/components/list.ts - blessed list component with tags: true enabled (line 24)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Work items with 'in-progress' status appear cyan/blue in the TUI tree view
2. Work items with 'completed' status appear gray in the TUI tree view
3. Work items with 'blocked' status appear red in the TUI tree view
4. Work items with 'open' status appear green in the TUI tree view (or other default color)
5. WL-0ML16W7000D2M8J3 now appears blue in the tree
6. All existing tests pass
7. No changes to console output formatting (chalk usage outside TUI remains unchanged)
data.title: Fix TUI work item colors: use blessed markup instead of chalk ANSI codes

data.description:
Problem statement
The Worklog CLI lacks a dependency management command, preventing users from recording and inspecting dependency edges via the CLI. This intake defines a minimal `wl dep` command set and outputs to enable dependency tracking workflows without edge types.

Users
- Developers and agents managing work items who need to declare and view dependencies from the CLI.
- Maintainers who need a human-readable and JSON output for automation.

User stories
- As a developer, I want to add a dependency so I can track what blocks a work item.
- As a maintainer, I want to list both inbound and outbound dependencies so I can see what is blocked and what is blocking.
- As an automation user, I want JSON output with key fields to parse dependencies programmatically.

Success criteria
- `wl dep add <item> <depends-on>` creates a dependency edge where item depends on depends-on.
- `wl dep rm <item> <depends-on>` removes the dependency edge if present.
- `wl dep list <item>` shows two sections: ‚ÄúDepends on‚Äù and ‚ÄúDepended on by‚Äù, even when empty.
- Human output lists ids, titles, status, priority, and direction; JSON includes the same fields.
- Missing ids produce warnings and the command exits 0.

Constraints
- No dependency types or type validation.
- Minimal CLI surface (add/rm/list only) with existing global flags support (e.g., --json).
- Non-destructive behavior for missing ids (warn and continue).

Existing state
- Worklog has blocked status handling and parses blocking ids from descriptions/comments.
- No `wl dep` CLI exists in `CLI.md`, and no dependency edge storage is implemented in code.

Desired change
- Implement `wl dep add|rm|list` commands for dependency edges without types.
- Ensure list output includes inbound and outbound sections with detailed fields.
- Add warnings for missing ids without failing the command.

Related work
- WL-0ML2VPUOT1IMU5US ‚Äî Add wl dep command (this intake)
- WL-0ML4PH4EQ1XODFM0 ‚Äî Doctor: detect missing dep references (child item)
- WL-0MKRPG64S04PL1A6 ‚Äî Feature: worklog doctor (integrity checks)
- `CLI.md` ‚Äî CLI reference, currently missing dep command

Suggested next step
- Proceed to the five review passes for this intake draft, then update WL-0ML2VPUOT1IMU5US with the approved brief.
data.title: Add wl dep command

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Standardize Status/Stage Labels From Config

## Problem Statement
Status and stage labels (and their legal combinations) are currently hard-coded in the TUI, creating inconsistent formatting and a split source of truth across TUI and CLI. This work makes labels and compatibility config-driven so both TUI and CLI render and validate against one shared, repo-specific definition.

## Users
- Contributors and agents who update work items via TUI or CLI and need consistent labels and rules.

### Example user stories
- As a contributor, I want status/stage labels to be consistent and configurable so TUI/CLI use a single source of truth.

## Success Criteria
- Status and stage labels and compatibility rules are sourced from `.worklog/config.defaults.yaml`.
- TUI update dialog renders labels from config and validates status/stage compatibility using config rules.
- CLI `wl create` and `wl update` reject invalid status/stage combinations with a clear error listing valid options.
- CLI accepts kebab-case values that map to valid snake_case values, with a warning on conversion.
- No remaining hard-coded status/stage label or compatibility arrays in the TUI/CLI code path.

## Constraints
- No backward compatibility: remove hard-coded defaults; if config is missing required sections, fail with a clear error.
- Canonical values and labels use `snake_case`.
- Compatibility is defined in one direction in config (status -> allowed stages); derive the reverse mapping in code.
- If CLI inputs are kebab-case equivalents of valid values, accept with warning; otherwise reject with error.
- Doctor validation depends on this config work landing first.

## Existing State
- TUI uses hard-coded status/stage values and compatibility in `src/tui/status-stage-rules.ts` and validation helpers.
- Inventory exists in `docs/validation/status-stage-inventory.md` describing current rules and gaps.

## Desired Change
- Extend config schema to include status labels, stage labels, and status->stage compatibility in `.worklog/config.defaults.yaml`.
- Refactor TUI update dialog and validation helpers to read labels and compatibility from config.
- Refactor CLI create/update flows to validate status/stage compatibility from config, including kebab-case normalization with warnings.
- Remove hard-coded status/stage labels and compatibility arrays from TUI/CLI runtime path.

## Risks & Assumptions
- Risk: Existing work items may contain invalid status/stage values and will fail validation; remediation will be required.
- Risk: Missing config sections will cause immediate failure by design.
- Assumption: Config defaults will include the full, canonical status/stage values and compatibility mapping.
- Assumption: `snake_case` is the canonical value format for statuses and stages.

## Related Work
- Standardize status/stage labels from config (WL-0ML4CQ8QL03P215I)
- Validation rules inventory (WL-0ML4W3B5E1IAXJ1P)
- Doctor: validate status/stage values from config (WL-0MLE6WJUY0C5RRVX)
- wl doctor (WL-0MKRPG64S04PL1A6)
- `docs/validation/status-stage-inventory.md` ‚Äî current rules and gaps
- `src/tui/status-stage-rules.ts` ‚Äî current hard-coded values and compatibility

## Suggested Next Step
Proceed to review and approval of this intake draft, then run the five review passes (completeness, capture fidelity, related-work & traceability, risks & assumptions, polish & handoff) before updating the work item description.
data.title: Standardize status/stage labels from config

Summary: Add a wl list --parent <id> command/flag to return only the children of a parent work item, without including the parent itself.

User story: As a user, I want a quick way to list only the children of a work item, without the parent details, to avoid noise and focus on subitems.

Background:
- This can be achieved today with wl show <parent> --children, but that includes the parent item details.

Expected behavior:
- wl list --parent <id> returns only the direct children of the specified parent.
- Output supports current list modes (JSON and human-readable) consistent with wl list.
- Errors if the parent id does not exist or is invalid.

Suggested implementation:
- Add --parent <id> option to wl list.
- Filter items by parentId matching the provided id.
- Keep other list filters compatible (status, priority, assignee, tags) if provided.

Acceptance criteria:
- wl list --parent <id> lists only direct children.
- Parent item is not included in the output.
- Works in JSON and non-JSON modes.
- Tests cover valid parent with children, parent with no children, and invalid parent id.
data.title: Add wl list --parent <id> for child-only listing

Summary: Investigate why wl update changes appear to succeed but later appear overwritten (sync/conflict issue).

User story: As a user, I want wl update changes to persist reliably so work item status/stage updates are not lost or reverted.

Observed issue:
- Multiple wl update commands report success, but later items appear not updated.
- Suspected sync/merge issue in worklog data.

Expected behavior:
- wl update should persist changes locally and after sync they should not be reverted by subsequent merges.

Investigation scope:
- Review wl update flow: local write, merge/sync behavior, and conflict resolution.
- Reproduce scenario where updates are overwritten.
- Identify any background sync or auto-merge that could revert fields.
- Check how worklog data is merged during git operations/push.

Acceptance criteria:
- Root cause identified and documented.
- Proposed fix or mitigation outlined.
- Tests or validation steps to confirm fix.
- Any necessary work items created for follow-up fixes.
data.title: Investigate wl update changes being overwritten

Summary: TUI details pane shows only the first comment for an item; subsequent comments (newer entries) are not rendered even though wl show --json shows them.

User story: As a user, I want the TUI to display all comments for a work item so I can see recent updates.

Observed behavior:
- For work item WL-0ML4DXBSD0AHHDG7, TUI shows the first comment but not the second.
- wl show --json confirms both comments exist.

Expected behavior:
- TUI renders all comments in order (newest-first or oldest-first, but consistent).

Repro:
1) Add a second comment to an item via wl comment add.
2) Open the item in TUI details pane.
3) Only the first comment appears.

Acceptance criteria:
- TUI displays all comments for a work item.
- New comments appear after refresh/reopen.
- Ordering matches backend (documented in UI or consistent with wl show).

Notes:
- Current item example: WL-0ML4DXBSD0AHHDG7 with comments WL-C0ML4MFGU90HD9XSJ and WL-C0ML4LWC1P0Z7XU1E.
