Summary: Add an integrity check that detects dependency edges referencing missing work items (created by wl dep or data edits).

Problem:
- wl dep will allow warnings-and-continue behavior when an id is missing, so we need a durable diagnostic to surface these errors later.

Scope:
- Add doctor check that scans dependency edges and reports edges where either endpoint id is missing.
- Output includes edge endpoints and location (if available).
- JSON output includes a stable type identifier and severity.

Acceptance criteria:
- worklog doctor reports dangling dependency references with a clear message and ids.
- JSON output includes type (e.g., missing-dependency-endpoint) and severity.
- Check is non-destructive and does not alter data.

Related: discovered-from:WL-0ML2VPUOT1IMU5US

## Summary
Implement wl dep add and wl dep rm commands.

## User Experience Change
Users can add or remove dependency edges from the CLI, with consistent success output and status updates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- wl dep add <item> <depends-on> creates an edge when ids exist.
- wl dep add errors (exit 1) if ids are missing or the dependency already exists.
- wl dep rm <item> <depends-on> removes the edge if present.
- wl dep rm warns and exits 0 when ids are missing.
- When adding a dependency, if the depends-on item stage is not in_review or done, the dependent item becomes blocked.
- When removing a dependency, if no remaining blocking dependencies exist, the dependent item becomes open.
- JSON output is available for add and rm.

## Minimal Implementation
- Add CLI handlers for add and rm.
- Wire to dependency edge storage.
- Emit human and JSON output.
- Update status based on dependency stages.

## Dependencies
- Persist dependency edges.

## Deliverables
- CLI command implementation and tests.

Summary: Add a '/' command palette to OpenCode so users can press '/' to open a dialog and choose a command.

User story: As a user, I want to press '/' to open a command picker so I can quickly choose common workflow commands and have them inserted and executed in the OpenCode input.

Behavior:
- Pressing '/' opens a modal/dialog with a list of commands.
- Initial command list: intake, plan, prd, implement.
- Selecting a valid command via Enter or Ctrl+S should:
  - Open the OpenCode interface (currently opened by 'o').
  - Type the chosen command into the input box, prefixed with '/', followed by a space and the currently selected work item id.
  - Example input: '/plan WL-0ML2V8MAC0W77Y1G'
  - Submit the command so OpenCode processes it.

Acceptance criteria:
- '/' opens the command picker dialog.
- Command list includes intake, plan, prd, implement.
- Enter or Ctrl+S confirms selection.
- OpenCode interface opens if not already open.
- Input is populated with '/<command> <current-work-item-id>' and submitted.
- Works with the currently selected work item in the UI.

Notes: Ensure the dialog only accepts valid commands from the list; no freeform command entry in this initial version.

data.description:
Summary: Extend wl dep list to support directional filtering for dependency edges.

User story:
- As an agent, I want to query only outbound dependencies for a work item so I can check whether a dependency edge already exists without client-side filtering.

Expected behavior:
- wl dep list <itemId> --outgoing --json returns only outbound edges (item depends on dependsOn).
- wl dep list <itemId> --incoming --json returns only inbound edges (items that depend on item).
- If neither flag is provided, retain current behavior (both directions).
- If both flags are provided, return an error (preferred behavior).
- JSON output shape unchanged aside from filtered contents.

Acceptance criteria:
- CLI help documents --outgoing and --incoming for wl dep list.
- Filtering works in both human and --json output.
- Unit tests cover outbound-only, inbound-only, and default behavior.
- Backward compatibility: existing usage without flags continues to work.

Notes:
- Ensure error messaging is clear when both flags are provided.
data.title: Add directional filtering to wl dep list

data.description:
Summary: Add a --body alias for wl comment add/create that maps to the existing --comment option.

User story:
- As an agent, I want to use --body when adding comments so CLI usage is consistent with other systems and less error-prone.

Expected behavior:
- wl comment add <workItemId> --body "text" behaves exactly like --comment "text".
- If both --body and --comment are provided, return a clear validation error.
- Help text documents --body as an alias for --comment.

Acceptance criteria:
- Alias works for wl comment add and wl comment create.
- Help output lists --body in both commands.
- Unit tests cover alias use and conflict error.
- Backward compatibility: --comment continues to work unchanged.
data.title: Add --body alias for wl comment add/create

data.description:
Summary: Add a new dialog option and keybinding in the TUI Next Item dialog to advance to the next recommended item (next-next).

User story:
- As a user, I want to skip a recommended item in the Next Item dialog so I can move to subsequent recommendations when the first is blocked.

Expected behavior:
- The Next Item dialog includes an option (e.g., 'Next recommendation') that advances to the next recommended work item.
- Pressing 'n' while the Next Item dialog is open triggers the same behavior.
- Each activation advances the dialog to show the next recommendation (second, third, etc.).
- Existing options (e.g., view selected item, cancel) continue to work.
- If there is no further recommendation, show a clear message and keep the dialog open.

Acceptance criteria:
- New dialog option present and labeled clearly.
- 'n' keybinding works while the Next Item dialog is open.
- Dialog updates to show the next recommended item on each use.
- Behavior is covered by unit or integration tests.
- Backward compatibility: existing dialog behavior unchanged when not using the new option.
data.title: Add next-next option to Next Item dialog

data.description:
Summary:
Add two optional CLI flags to work item create/update commands to control ordering:  and .

User stories:
- As a user I can set an explicit numeric ordering when creating or updating a work item using  so items appear in a predictable position.
- As a user I can insert a new or updated item after an existing sibling using , which computes a  between the referenced item and its next sibling.

Expected behaviour:
- Both flags are optional; omit both and current behaviour is unchanged.
-  accepts a validated integer and sets the work item's  to that value.
-  resolves the referenced work item to its numeric , then computes a new  as the midpoint between that value and the next sibling's  (or a defined max/default if no next sibling).
- Resolve  to a numeric  before creating/updating to ensure atomic update and avoid races.
- If  is provided together with ,  takes precedence and  is ignored.

Implementation notes:
- Validate integer input for  at CLI parsing layer; reject non-integers with a clear error.
- : Accepts a work item id; resolve to numeric  server-side (or via an atomic server call) before creating/updating the new item to avoid races.
- When computing midpoint, use a numeric scheme that maintains precision and avoids collisions on concurrent inserts (e.g., use large integer space or rationals; consider fallback rebalancing when no midpoint available).
- Add unit tests for CLI parsing and sort index calculation and integration tests that simulate concurrent inserts.
- Backwards-compatible: both flags optional; no change to existing behaviour when omitted.

Acceptance criteria (testable):
1) CLI accepts  as integer; creating/updating an item with this flag sets  to the provided value.
2) CLI accepts ; creating an item with this flag places it after the referenced sibling by computing an appropriate .
3) When both flags are omitted, behaviour unchanged.
4) Input validation tests for invalid integers and non-existent  ids return user-friendly errors.
5) Concurrency integration test: two concurrent inserts using  against the same predecessor produce distinct orderings and remain stable.

Tests to add:
- Unit tests: CLI parser accepts flags and validates types;  midpoint calculation returns expected numeric values and handles edge cases.
- Integration tests: simulate concurrent creation with  to assert no collisions and acceptable ordering.

Backwards compatibility: both flags are optional and do not change current APIs when omitted.

Original proposal comment:
[SA-C0ML648B1S0DLXBAT] @AGENT at 2026-02-03T04:45:42.256Z
Proposal:\n- : set work item  explicitly on create/update.\n- : insert new/updated item after work item ; implementation computes midpoint between  and the next sibling's  (or  if no next sibling).\nImplementation notes:\n- Validate integer input for .\n-  should be resolved to numeric  before creating/updating work item; ensure atomic update to avoid races.\n- Add unit tests for CLI parsing and sortIndex calculation, and integration tests that simulate concurrent inserts.\n- Backwards-compatible: both flags optional; when omitted current behavior remains unchanged.
data.title: Add CLI options --sort-index and --after for ordering work items

data.description:
Problem statement

When dependency edges are removed, or when an item that was blocking another moves to a non-blocking state, blocked items are not consistently returned to an unblocked status. This causes work to remain incorrectly marked as `blocked` and prevents it from being surfaced by `wl next` or other discovery flows.

Users

- Producer/triager: As a producer, I want blocked items to automatically become unblocked when their blockers are resolved so they reappear in work discovery.
- Developer/assignee: As an assignee, I want the item's status to reflect current reality without manual changes after blockers are removed.

Success criteria

- When a dependency is removed (via `wl dep rm`) a dependent item is marked `open` if no remaining active blockers exist.
- When a blocking item's stage or status changes to an inactive state (stage in `in_review` or `done`, or status `completed`/`deleted`) the dependent item is marked `open` if no other active blockers exist.
- The change is idempotent and makes no status change if other active blockers remain.
- Automated logic runs on dependency removal and on updates to work items that may affect blocking status.

Constraints

- Preserve existing `wl dep` behavior except add unblocking side-effects when appropriate.
- Do not change other status/stage semantics; use existing stage/status values as signals.
- Keep changes minimal: prefer simple `status` updates over storing historical status unless explicitly requested.

Existing state

- Dependency edges are persisted and exposed via `db.listDependencyEdgesFrom` / `listDependencyEdgesTo` (see `src/database.ts`).
- `wl dep add` and `wl dep rm` update work item statuses in some cases already (`src/commands/dep.ts`).
- `src/database.ts` contains helper functions to list dependency edges and to inspect items/comments for blocking references.

Desired change

- Add an idempotent reconciliation step that runs:
  - after `dep rm` completes and
  - whenever a work item is updated (status or stage changes) and that work item is the target of dependency edges.

- The reconciliation will:
  1. For each item that depends on the changed/removed item, collect all outbound dependency edges from that dependent item.
  2. Treat a dependency edge as inactive if the target item's stage is `in_review` or `done`, or its status is `completed` or `deleted`.
  3. If none of the remaining outbound dependencies are active blockers, update the dependent item's `status` to `open` (no status history restoration).
  4. If at least one active blocker remains, leave the dependent item `blocked`.

- Prefer small, testable helpers in `src/database.ts` (e.g., `getInboundDependents(id)`, `hasActiveBlockers(itemId)`), and call them from `dep rm` and from the general `update` path where status/stage changes are handled.

Related work

- WL-0ML4TFSQ70Y3UPMR `wl dep add/rm` ‚Äî CLI surface implemented and partially updates status on add/rm.
- WL-0ML4TFSGF1SLN4DT `Persist dependency edges` ‚Äî edge persistence is implemented and exported to JSONL.
- WL-0MKRPG64S04PL1A6 `Feature: worklog doctor` ‚Äî integrity checks and periodic reconciliation may be relevant for a delayed fallback.

Suggested next step

1) Proceed to implement small database helpers and wire the reconciliation into `dep rm` and the item `update` path. Implementation includes unit tests for edge cases (multiple blockers, missing targets, already-open items).

Risks & assumptions

- Risk: Race conditions if multiple updates/removals happen concurrently; Mitigation: Ensure database operations are atomic and write-through (DB layer functions call `exportToJsonl()` and `triggerAutoSync()` consistently).
- Risk: False unblocking if stage/status semantics change elsewhere; Mitigation: Keep the active-blocker definition centralized in DB helpers and document behavior.
- Assumption: Dependency edges are correctly persisted and queryable via existing store accessors.
data.title: Auto-unblock on dependency changes

data.description:
Add an embedded multi-line comment textbox to the Update dialog.

Summary:
Integrate a blessed multi-line input into `src/tui/components/dialogs.ts` used by the Update dialog. The textbox must accept multi-line text, expose focus/blur events, and surface its value to the dialog submit handler.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A multi-line textbox renders inside the Update dialog and accepts input.
- Pressing Enter inserts a newline; Tab/Shift-Tab exits the textbox to the next/previous field.
- The textbox value is included in the `db.update` payload when the dialog is submitted.
- Tests under `tests/tui/tui-update-dialog.test.ts` cover these behaviours.

Minimal Implementation:
- Add `src/tui/components/multiline-text.ts` wrapper for blessed `textarea`.
- Render it inside the Update dialog in `src/tui/components/dialogs.ts`.
- Hook value into dialog state and submission.

Dependencies:
- parent: WL-0ML1K7CVT19NUNRW

Deliverables:
- Component code, dialog changes, tests, README demo.

data.description: User reports update dialog comment box partially visible: bottom border and title visible, appears overlapped by lists above. Plenty of space; lists should be smaller and comment box moved up. Goal: adjust TUI update dialog layout so comment textarea is fully visible and not overlapped; lists height reduced as needed. Acceptance criteria: (1) Comment box fully visible within update dialog (title and border not overlapped). (2) Lists above do not overlap comment box and are reduced if needed. (3) Layout adapts to terminal size without overlap.
data.title: Fix update dialog comment box overlap

data.description: User reports Tab enters update dialog comment box but cannot Tab out to other fields. Goal: ensure Tab/Shift-Tab move focus out of comment textarea back to lists in update dialog. Acceptance criteria: (1) Tab from comment box moves focus to next field in update dialog. (2) Shift-Tab moves focus to previous field. (3) Comment box still supports multiline input with Enter.
data.title: Fix tab navigation out of update dialog comment box

data.description: User reports pressing Escape while update dialog is open exits the TUI app. Expected: Escape closes the update dialog and returns focus to list without exiting. Acceptance criteria: (1) Escape in update dialog or its fields closes update dialog and keeps app running. (2) Escape still closes app when no dialog is open (unchanged behavior). (3) No regression to other dialogs' Escape handling.
data.title: Escape in update dialog should close dialog, not app

data.description: User reports after entering the comment textbox, there is no way to submit the update; Enter inserts newline in the comment box and doesn't trigger submit. Goal: provide a clear submit action that works after interacting with comment box. Acceptance criteria: (1) User can submit update dialog after focusing comment box (via Enter or explicit keybind/button). (2) Comment box still supports multiline input. (3) Update action behavior unchanged otherwise.
data.title: Restore update dialog submit after comment focus

data.description:
Goal: ensure a work item‚Äôs updatedAt reflects comment activity.

Context: The CLI/API comment operations (create/update/delete) currently do not modify the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt.

User story: As a user, when I add/update/delete a comment on a work item, the work item should show a refreshed last-modified timestamp so recent activity is visible.

Expected behavior:
- On comment create, update the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt to now.
- On comment update, update the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt to now.
- On comment delete, update the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt to now.
- No other work item fields change.

Acceptance criteria:
- Adding a comment changes the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt.
- Updating a comment changes the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt.
- Deleting a comment changes the parent work item‚Äôs updatedAt.
- Work item data remains otherwise unchanged.
- Behavior applies to CLI and API flows (shared database layer).
data.title: Update work item timestamps on comment changes

data.description:
Summary: Remove automated test output from PR comments to avoid noise and leaking environment details.

User story:
As a developer I want PR comments to not contain raw test output so reviewers see concise summaries and logs remain in CI artifacts.

Acceptance criteria:
- Automated processes no longer post full test results into PR comments.
- Existing PRs with test-result comments are identified and optionally a script can remove or redact them (manual approval required).
- CI publishes test artifacts/logs to CI provider and links are included in PR comments instead of full logs.
- Add CI checks to prevent posting raw test outputs to comments.

Implementation notes:
- Search repo for ,  calls, or scripts that post test output to PRs.
- Replace behavior with artifact uploads and link posting.
- Create follow-up items for CI infra changes if needed.
data.title: Remove test results from PR comments

data.title: Flaky tests causing CI timeouts (CLI suites)

data.description:
Summary:
Investigate and refactor long-running CLI tests to reduce test runtime and flakiness. Several CLI tests legitimately perform integration steps (git ops, file IO, DB exports) and take multiple seconds when run as part of the full suite. Increasing timeouts or running tests serially reduces flakes but is a workaround. This task scopes a refactor to mock/stub external dependencies and optimize test setup/teardown to make the CLI test suite fast and reliable without relying on serial execution or increased per-test timeouts.

Goals / Acceptance Criteria:
- Identify the slow tests and the underlying reasons (git operations, DB file writes, external process spawning, plugin loading).
- Replace or mock external operations (git, file system heavy setups, remote sync) where feasible to reduce test duration.
- Where mocking is not feasible, move tests to an integration-only folder and ensure fast unit tests remain fast.
- Achieve full test-suite run time reduction of CLI tests by at least 30% on CI or reduce the number of tests that require >5s to run.
- Keep tests deterministic: run the full suite 3x under CI-like environment without intermittent timeouts.

Suggested implementation approach:
1) Audit tests to produce per-test timings and identify top slow tests.
2) For each slow test, attempt to stub external commands (spawn/exec) and external services or replace with in-memory equivalents.
3) Consolidate repeated heavy setup/teardown into shared fixtures (reusable temp dirs, seeded DB snapshots).
4) Add targeted unit tests if integration tests are split out.
5) Document CI changes or rerun strategies if needed.

Files/Areas to review:
- tests/cli/init.test.ts
- tests/cli/status.test.ts
- tests/cli/worktree.test.ts
- tests/cli/* helpers: tests/cli/cli-helpers.ts, tests/test-utils.js
- vitest.config.ts and CI scripts

Notes:
- This is a non-blocking task for WL-0MLB5ZIOO0BDJJPG; it is an improvement idea and should be evaluated for scope.,
data.title: Refactor slow CLI tests

data.description:
Add automated unit tests for the TUI '/' search/filter flow:

- Verify opening the search modal with '/' and canceling returns focus to the main list.
- Verify submitting an empty term clears the active filter and restores previous items.
- Verify submitting a non-empty term uses 'wl list <term> --json' and that the code handles payload shapes: array, {results:[]}, {workItems:[]}, {workItem}.
- Verify state.showClosed is set to false after applying a filter and footer displays active filter.
- Mock child_process.spawn to simulate stdout/stderr and exit codes.

Acceptance criteria:

data.description:
After using the TUI '/' search filter, global shortcuts (A/I/B, ?, /) stop responding and the user cannot exit the filtered view. Expected: filter shortcuts, help, and search continue working after applying or clearing a filter. Investigate focus/handler state after filter apply/clear and ensure global key handlers remain active.

Additional report: Ctrl-C does not exit the TUI after filtering.
data.title: Fix TUI filter mode blocking shortcuts

data.description:
Summary:
Enable a modal interactive shell that reuses the existing opencode prompt textarea as both input and output.

User story:
As a CLI user, I want to quickly open an interactive shell in the opencode prompt so I can run commands without leaving the TUI.

Expected behavior:
- A keybinding or command (e.g. Ctrl-\ or :shell) opens the shell modal using the opencode textarea.
- The textarea acts as both input and output; outputs are appended and support scrolling.
- Enter submits commands; Esc or :exit closes the shell and restores normal opencode behavior.
- Commands run in a child PTY/subprocess and stream stdout/stderr into the textarea.

Acceptance criteria:
1) Keybinding/command opens the shell modal and focuses the opencode textarea.
2) Typing "echo hello" and submitting displays "hello" in the textarea.
3) Multi-line output is appended and scrollable.
4) Exiting the shell restores normal opencode behavior.
5) Existing opencode key handlers (Ctrl-W, tab, etc.) remain functional while shell is active.

Implementation notes:
- Reuse OpencodePaneComponent and its textarea/dialog (see src/tui/layout.ts and src/commands/tui.ts).
- Add a shell manager at src/tui/shell.ts that spawns a PTY and wires IO to the textarea.
- Add command/keybinding in src/commands/tui.ts to toggle the shell and swap input handlers.

References:
- src/tui/layout.ts
- src/commands/tui.ts
- src/tui/components/opencode-pane.js

This work item focuses on UI/IO plumbing; follow-ups may add sandboxing or permissions.
data.title: Enable interactive shell in opencode prompt

data.description:
Problem statement

Navigation inside the TUI "Update" window is inconsistent for keyboard and mouse users: focus order, selection-list keyboard behaviour, and focus restoration when navigating between items are unclear or broken. This causes friction, accessibility regressions for screen-reader users, and risks accidental data loss when controls unexpectedly steal or lose focus.

Users

- Editors (power users) who use the TUI to update items via keyboard and mouse.
- Screen-reader users who rely on predictable focus and dialog announcements.
- Mobile/touch users who interact with inline lists and fields in constrained viewports.

Example user stories

- As a keyboard-only editor, I want Tab and Shift+Tab to move predictably between the three item-setting lists and other controls so I can make changes without losing context.
- As a screen-reader user, I want the Update dialog announced on open and each control to expose role/state so I can navigate and confirm changes.
- As a mobile user, I want touch interactions with lists and fields to behave consistently and not hide focused fields behind the keyboard.

Success criteria

- Focus and tab order: Tab/Shift+Tab moves in a logical, documented order across header controls ‚Üí primary editor ‚Üí the three item-setting lists ‚Üí metadata fields ‚Üí Save/Cancel; no controls are skipped.
- Selection-list behaviour: For the three inline item-setting lists (project/type/assignee or as-implemented), lists are treated as already-open interactive areas ‚Äî Tab moves between lists (not to open them); Arrow keys navigate list options; Enter selects; Escape cancels; behaviour consistent across all three lists and documented in component README.
- Accessibility: Opening the Update window announces dialog title/role; lists and controls expose roles/states; screen-reader walkthrough of the primary flows succeeds.
- Safety: Navigating Next/Previous item preserves unsaved changes (confirmation shown) and focus lands in the primary editor of the new item.
- Tests: Unit/integration tests cover keyboard handlers and focus management; at least one end-to-end Playwright/Cypress test automates open ‚Üí edit ‚Üí list selection ‚Üí save flows on desktop and mobile viewports.

Constraints

- Respect existing TUI patterns (mouse-guard, overlay-dismiss) implemented in related work; do not redesign dialog modality unless necessary.
- Keep changes minimal and backwards-compatible with existing keyboard shortcuts unless an explicit improvement is approved.
- Avoid introducing heavy runtime dependencies; prefer incremental tests that run in CI.

Existing state

- `src/tui/controller.ts` registers `updateOverlay` click handlers and contains dialog open/close logic; tests mock `updateOverlay` in multiple places.
- Mouse-guard and overlay click-to-dismiss behaviours have been implemented (see WL-0MLRFF0771A8NAVW and child tasks), including unit tests and a PR; keyboard/tab-order and selection-list ARIA/roving-tabindex patterns are not fully specified or tested.

Desired change

- Audit the Update window to define a clear tab order and per-control keyboard semantics.
- Implement keyboard handlers for the three inline selection lists so Arrow keys, Enter, Escape and Tab behave consistently with the chosen pattern (Tab moves between lists; lists are treated as interactive areas that are already open).
- Add ARIA roles/attributes and ensure dialog is announced on open.
- Add unit and integration tests for keyboard handlers and focus cycles; add a Playwright/Cypress e2e test covering desktop and mobile viewport flows.
- Document the final tab order and list interaction rules in the component README.

Related work

- TUI: prevent mouse click-through from dialogs to underlying widgets (WL-0MLRFF0771A8NAVW) ‚Äî implemented mouse guard and overlay-dismiss; reduces overlap but does not fully address keyboard/tab behaviour.
- Overlay click-to-dismiss (WL-0MLYZQS741EZPASH) ‚Äî added `updateOverlay` click handler and tests.
- Discard-changes confirmation dialog (WL-0MLYZR6NH182R4ZR) ‚Äî implements Yes/No confirmation when overlay clicked with unsaved changes.
- Guard screen mouse handler (WL-0MLYZQI9C1YGIUCW) and mouse-guard tests (WL-0MLYZQ52Q0NH7VOD) ‚Äî relevant for click handling and tests.

Notes / open questions

- Confirm the canonical names and exact identities of the three item-setting lists (e.g., Project, Type, Assignee) to reference them precisely in tests and docs. If names differ, tests should refer to the component IDs/selectors used in `src/tui/components`.
- Confirm whether Escape should always return focus to the list trigger (recommended) or follow a different repo pattern.

data.description:
If we delete an item via the TUI it is removed as expected, but using the CLI `wl delete <id>` reports success while the item remains.

Reproduction steps:
1. In the TUI, mark an item with `x` and select `Close (deleted)` (or the UI equivalent). The item should disappear from the list/view.
2. Note the item's id (e.g. WL-123).
3. From the shell run: `wl delete <id>`.
4. Observe: the CLI reports success but the item still appears in lists or the TUI after refresh.

Observed behavior:
- TUI: item is removed from view (deleted).
- CLI: `wl delete <id>` prints success, but the item is not deleted.

Expected behavior:
- `wl delete <id>` should permanently delete the item (or mark it as deleted) and it should no longer appear in the TUI or `wl list` results.

Impact:
- Critical: automation and scripts that rely on the CLI to delete items silently fail, causing inconsistent state between UI and CLI workflows.

Suggested debugging steps:
- Compare the API calls / code paths used by TUI vs CLI for deletion.
- Check for differences in flags/parameters or required permissions between the two codepaths.
- Verify persistence layer (database) change is applied when `wl delete` runs.
- Collect logs or run: `wl delete <id> --verbose` and include output.

Please investigate as a critical bug affecting CLI deletion behavior.
data.title: Deletion in TUI works, in CLI it does not

data.description:
# Problem statement
Add a `workflow_dispatch` trigger to the CI workflow so developers can manually re‚Äërun CI on any ref via API/CLI.

# Users
Developers who need to re‚Äërun CI for debugging, testing, or verification purposes.

# Success criteria
- `workflow_dispatch` entry is present in `.github/workflows/tui-tests.yml`.
- Existing `pull_request` trigger continues to function unchanged.
- CI can be started manually using the GitHub UI or `gh workflow run` without errors.
- The workflow runs to completion on a manually dispatched ref.

# Constraints
None.

# Existing state
The CI workflow (`.github/workflows/tui-tests.yml`) currently triggers only on `pull_request` events; no manual dispatch capability exists.

# Desired change
Add a top‚Äëlevel `workflow_dispatch:` key to `.github/workflows/tui-tests.yml` (and any other CI workflow files as appropriate) preserving existing triggers.

# Related work
- Work item WL-0MM5J7OC31PFBW60 ‚Äì Diagnostic test instrumentation (logs‚Äëonly) (mentions using `workflow_dispatch` for manual CI runs).
- File `.github/workflows/run-npm-tests.yml` ‚Äì contains an example `workflow_dispatch` entry.
- Work item WL-0MLC7I1V31X2NCIV ‚Äì current work item.

# Risks & assumptions
- **Risk:** Developers may manually trigger many CI runs, increasing load on CI resources.
  **Mitigation:** Encourage use only when needed and monitor CI usage.
- **Assumption:** The CI infrastructure supports manual dispatches on any branch.

## Related work (automated report)
- WL-0MM5J7OC31PFBW60 ‚Äì Diagnostic test instrumentation (logs‚Äëonly): mentions using `workflow_dispatch` to manually run a CI job for diagnostics.
- .github/workflows/run-npm-tests.yml ‚Äì example workflow file containing a `workflow_dispatch` entry, useful as a reference for proper syntax.

data.description:
Problem: comment syncing lists all GitHub comments for each issue needing comment sync, which is slow for issues with long comment histories.

Proposed approach:
- Store per-worklog-comment GitHub comment ID and updatedAt in worklog metadata to avoid listing all comments.
- When comment mapping exists, update/create comments directly; only fallback to list when mapping is missing.
- Alternatively, query comments since last synced timestamp if API supports, and only scan recent comments.

Acceptance criteria:
- Comment list API calls are reduced on repeated runs.
- Existing comment edits continue to be detected and updated.
- Worklog comment markers remain the source of truth for mapping.
data.title: Optimize comment sync to avoid full comment listing

data.description:
Problem: hierarchy linking currently calls getIssueHierarchy for every parent-child pair and verifies each link, creating many GraphQL requests.

Proposed approach:
- Cache hierarchy by parent issue number (fetch once per parent) and reuse for all children.
- Only verify link creation when the link mutation returns success; skip redundant re-fetches or batch verifications.
- Consider GraphQL query batching for multiple parents in one request if feasible.

Acceptance criteria:
- Hierarchy check API calls scale by number of parents, not number of pairs.
- Links are still created and verified correctly.
- No regressions in parent/child linking behavior.
data.title: Batch or cache hierarchy checks for parent/child links

data.description:
Problem: current GitHub sync performs all API calls serially via execSync, increasing total runtime.

Proposed approach:
- Replace execSync with async calls and a bounded concurrency queue.
- Batch read operations with GraphQL where possible (e.g., issue nodes, hierarchy, comment metadata).
- Add rate-limit backoff handling to avoid 403s.

Acceptance criteria:
- Push runtime improves on large worklogs without exceeding GitHub rate limits.
- Errors are surfaced clearly with the failing operation.
- No change to sync correctness across create/update/comment/hierarchy phases.
data.title: Introduce concurrency/batching for GitHub API calls

data.description:
Problem: wl github push performs multiple GitHub API calls per issue update (edit, close/reopen, label remove/add, view), leading to slow syncs. Goal: reduce per-issue API round trips while preserving current behavior.

Proposed approach:
- Fetch current issue once when needed and diff title/body/state/labels to decide minimal updates.
- Avoid close/reopen when state already matches.
- Avoid label add/remove when labels already match; ensure labels once per run.
- Consider GraphQL mutation to update title/body/state/labels in a single request when supported.

Acceptance criteria:
- Per-updated-issue API calls reduced vs current baseline (document before/after in timing output).
- No functional regressions in labels/state/body/title synchronization.
- Update path still respects worklog markers and label prefix rules.
- Add or update tests covering update/no-op paths.
data.title: Optimize issue update API calls in wl github push

data.description:
Problem: label creation checks call gh api repos/.../labels --paginate for each issue update, which is slow for large repos.

Proposed approach:
- Load existing repo labels once per push, cache in memory, and reuse for all updates.
- Only call label creation APIs for labels missing from the cached set.
- Ensure cache updates when new labels are created.

Acceptance criteria:
- Label list API call happens once per wl github push run.
- New labels are still created when missing.
- No change to label prefix handling or label color generation.
data.title: Cache/skip label discovery during GitHub push

data.description:
Problem: Slowness needs concrete measurements by phase and API call counts.

Proposed approach:
- Add per-operation timing and API call counters (issue upsert, label list/create, comment list/upsert, hierarchy fetch/link/verify).
- Add summary to verbose output and log file to compare runs.
- Optionally add env flag to enable debug tracing without verbose UI noise.

Acceptance criteria:
- wl github push --verbose shows per-phase timings and API call counts.
- Logs include enough data to compare before/after optimization work.
data.title: Instrument and profile wl github push hotspots

data.description:
Problem: Deleting a work item from the TUI appears to succeed briefly, then the item reappears in the tree view.

Context: Only action performed was deleting an item from the Work Items list. Selecting an item, pressing x to close/delete, and confirming leads to a momentary removal followed by reinstatement.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Create a work item.
2) Select it in the Work Items list.
3) Press x to close/delete.
4) Observe: item disappears briefly, then reappears in the tree view.

Expected: Item remains deleted/closed and is removed from the tree view.
Actual: Item is reinstated after a brief disappearance.

Acceptance criteria:
- After confirming delete/close from the Work Items list, the item is removed from the tree view and does not reappear on subsequent refreshes.
- The TUI UI state remains consistent after the delete (selection focus moves predictably, no flicker/reinsert).
- Deletion updates the underlying worklog data so that a reload does not restore the item.

Notes:
- Reporter indicated this was the only action performed.
- tui-debug.log may contain useful information; reporter can rerun with verbose logging if required.
data.title: TUI delete action restores removed item

data.description:
Problem statement
-----------------
`wl init` currently copies the global `AGENTS.md` into new projects (or appends it) which duplicates guidance and can create contradictory or stale local rules. We need `wl init` to prefer a single canonical global `AGENTS.md` while allowing a project's local `AGENTS.md` to declare project-specific overrides via a short pointer line.

Users
-----
- Project maintainers who want a single source of truth for agent guidance and the ability to declare small local exceptions.
- Agent authors and automation that rely on `AGENTS.md` to behave consistently across repositories.

Example user stories
- As a project maintainer, when I run `wl init` I want the project to reference the global `AGENTS.md` and allow me to add local exceptions so I avoid duplicated guidance.
- As an automation author, I want `wl init` behaviour to be idempotent so repeated runs do not create duplicate pointer lines or duplicate content.

Success criteria
----------------
- The local `AGENTS.md` begins with this exact pointer line (or preserves an existing exact match):
  "Follow the global AGENTS.md in addition to the rules below. The local rules below take priority in the event of a conflict."
- If a local `AGENTS.md` already exists, `wl init` prompts the operator before modifying it (non-destructive by default); if approved, the pointer is inserted near the top and the existing content is preserved unchanged except for trimming trailing whitespace.
- Running `wl init` multiple times is a no-op with respect to `AGENTS.md` (idempotent): no duplicate pointer lines, no duplicated global content.
- Unit and integration tests validate pointer insertion and idempotence on POSIX shells (Linux/macOS) and a minimal integration test demonstrates `wl init` behavior in CI.
- Documentation and release notes updated to describe the pointer requirement and the interactive behaviour for existing files.

Constraints
-----------
- Pointer text must match the exact line above for acceptance criteria (projects may have equivalent wording but the implementation will look for the exact pointer string to guarantee idempotence).
- Tests are focused on POSIX shell environments (Linux/macOS); Windows behaviour is out of scope for this change.
- For repositories with an existing `AGENTS.md`, `wl init` must not modify files without operator approval (interactive prompt or documented non-interactive flag to skip changes).
- Changes must be idempotent and safe for automated CI runs (non-destructive by default).

Existing state
--------------
- Repository root contains a global `AGENTS.md` with the agent workflow and WL conventions.
- Current `wl init` behaviour copies global `AGENTS.md` content into projects or appends content, causing duplicated guidance (described in work item SA-0MLCUNY9M0QN37A7). Several repository files and skills mention `AGENTS.md` and depend on consistent agent guidance.

Desired change
--------------
- Update `wl init`/template generation logic to:
  1. Detect whether a local `AGENTS.md` exists.
  2. If none exists, create `AGENTS.md` that begins with the exact pointer line and then (optionally) append any minimal project-specific starter rules.
  3. If a local `AGENTS.md` exists, do not modify it silently: prompt the operator during `wl init` (non-interactive runs may skip or record a suggested change) and, if approved, insert the exact pointer at the top and preserve the remainder of the file.
  4. Ensure the insertion logic is idempotent (no duplicate pointers on re-run).

Related work
------------
- Potentially related docs:
  - `AGENTS.md` ‚Äî canonical global guidance used by `wl init` (`AGENTS.md`).
  - `skill/create-worktree-skill/README.md` ‚Äî notes about `wl init` usage in CI and worktree setup (`skill/create-worktree-skill/README.md`).
  - `skill/create-worktree-skill/SKILL.md` and `scripts/run.sh` ‚Äî places where non-interactive `wl init` is invoked and where insertion behavior matters (`skill/create-worktree-skill/SKILL.md`, `skill/create-worktree-skill/scripts/run.sh`).

- Potentially related work items:
  - `wl init: prefer global AGENTS.md and make local workflow rules override` ‚Äî SA-0MLCUNY9M0QN37A7 (this item).
  - `Orchestration` ‚Äî SA-0MKXVC7NA0UQLDR7 ‚Äî broader agent workflow conventions that rely on `AGENTS.md`.
  - `Swarmable Plans` ‚Äî SA-0MKXVTHF70EXG01D ‚Äî references `AGENTS.md` for agent workflow guidance in planning contexts.
  - `Create worktree and branch` skill items ‚Äî SA-0ML0502B21WHXDYA / SA-0ML05054Q0S4KAUD ‚Äî integration tests and CI harnesses that run `wl init` and assume consistent `AGENTS.md` behaviour.
data.title: wl init: prefer global AGENTS.md pointer

data.description:
Headline

Introduce a structured per-item `audit` field ({ time, text, author }) and CLI support (`--audit-text`) so tooling and automation can reliably read/write and surface audit metadata; do not perform automated migration of legacy comment-based audits in this change.

Problem statement

Replace the brittle practice of recording audits inside free-form comments with a structured per-work-item `audit` field so tools can reliably read/write audit metadata (ISO8601 time, text, author) and surface it in CLI output. This item focuses on adding the field, CLI support, validation, redaction, and tests ‚Äî it does not include automatic migration of existing comment-based audits.

Users

- Operators and maintainers who need a reliable, machine-readable audit for handoffs and automation (triage bots, Producers).
- CLI and automation authors who need to set and read audits programmatically.

Example user stories

- As a maintainer, when I mark an item audited I want the audit stored as structured metadata so scripts can detect and summarize the latest audit without parsing comments.
- As an automation, I want to set an audit via CLI/API and have a consistent timestamp and author recorded so downstream reports are accurate.

Success criteria

- Work item model and persistent store include a new `audit` field storing `{ time: ISO8601, text: string, author: string }` and a DB migration is created under `src/migrations`.
- CLI: `wl update <id> --audit-text "..."` records/overwrites the structured `audit` field; server/CLI sets `time` to current UTC ISO8601 and `author` to the actor display name automatically.
- `wl show <id>` (human readable) and `wl show <id> --json` include the `audit` field in output.
- Input `text` has simple auto-redaction of email addresses before storage; unit tests cover timestamp generation/format, overwrite behaviour, redaction, and CLI help includes `--audit-text` docs.

Constraints

- Backward-compatible: legacy comment-based audits remain in history and are not deleted; no automatic migration is performed in this item.
- Permission model: only users with existing `update` permission may add/update the audit field.
- PII mitigation: only the actor display name is stored in `author` (no emails); email addresses found in `text` are masked by simple redaction.
- Schema changes must be delivered as a migration in `src/migrations` (no runtime silent ALTER TABLE actions).

Existing state

- Repo already references audit-like content in comments and skill output; a number of related work items and docs exist that changed how audits are produced and consumed.
- Persistent store is SQLite via `src/persistent-store.ts`; migrations live under `src/migrations` and upgrades are applied via `wl doctor upgrade`.
- Current CLI and tooling surface audits as free-text comments and several skills produce audit comments wrapped by markers (recent work added structured-report extraction flows).

Desired change

- Add structured `audit` field to work item model and DB schema (migration in `src/migrations`).
- Implement CLI support: `wl update <id> --audit-text "..."` which records the audit (server/CLI sets `time` and `author`), validates inputs, masks email addresses in `text`, and overwrites existing `audit` values.
- Ensure `wl show` (default and `--json`) surfaces the field and update CLI `--help` and docs.
- Add unit and integration tests for validation, overwrite behaviour, redaction, and CLI help.
- Do NOT perform automated migration of comment-based audits in this change; operators may extract manually or a follow-up work item can be created.

Related work

- Potentially related docs:
  - `src/github-sync.ts` ‚Äî builds GitHub comment bodies and references audit-like output used in JSON exports and syncing.
  - `src/persistent-store.ts` ‚Äî persistent store & migration policy; migrations are under `src/migrations`.
  - `tests/github-import-label-resolution.test.ts` ‚Äî tests that reference FieldChange records and audit output expectations (useful for import/audit cases).
  - `skill/audit/SKILL.md` (and `docs/triage-audit.md`) ‚Äî recent changes require structured, delimiter-bounded audit reports; helps define desired report shape.

- Potentially related work items:
  - Audit comment improvements (WL-0MLG60MK60WDEEGE) ‚Äî prior work improving audit comment formatting and structured skill output.
  - Marker extraction in triage runner (WL-0MLYTL4AI0A6UDPA) ‚Äî extraction logic that isolates structured content in comments (relevant for future migration/extraction tools).
  - Structured audit report skill instructions (WL-0MLYTKTI20V31KYW) ‚Äî guidance about structured audit reports for skills.
  - Structured audit logging for import (WL-0MM369NX61U76OVY) ‚Äî related to emitting structured audit-like logs during imports.

Decisions captured from intake interview

- CLI flag UX: Use `--audit-text "..."` (server/CLI populates time and author automatically).
- Migration: Do not include automatic migration; any migration/extraction of comment-based audits will be a separate follow-up work item.
- Author metadata: store only the actor display name in the `author` field to reduce PII surface.

Open questions

- None remain from the interview ‚Äî please review and indicate any edits or accept the draft.

Related artefacts discovered during intake (short summaries)

- `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` ‚Äî canonical worklog store containing the WL-0MLDJ34RQ1ODWRY0 record and history.
- `.worklog/logs/sync.log` ‚Äî sync conflicts referencing this item; useful when reconciling local vs remote edits.
- `.worklog/logs/github_sync.log` ‚Äî GitHub sync attempts/errors referencing this work item.

data.description:
Summary:
Using the internal opencode interface (`o`) to create a work item results in the item being created in the Sorra Agents repository located under the user's config directory (~/.config/opencode / "Sorra Agents").

Impact:
- Work items intended for this repository are leaked into the user's global opencode configuration repository (Sorra Agents).
- Causes data leakage, confusion, and potential permission/ownership issues.
- High risk for privacy and integrity; affects all users running the internal `o` tool.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Run the internal opencode interface `o` and create a new work item (e.g. `o create "Test bug" --description "..."`).
2. Observe that the created work item appears in the Sorra Agents repo under `~/.config/opencode` instead of the target repository's worklog.

Expected behaviour:
- Work items created via `o` should be created in the active project repository's worklog, not in the global `~/.config/opencode` directory.

Suggested root causes to investigate:
- Hardcoded path or environment variable pointing to `~/.config/opencode` when resolving the wl data store.
- Incorrect handling of relative vs absolute paths when invoked from the internal interface.
- Use of a global configuration store without respecting the current project's working directory or repository context.

Acceptance criteria:
- Fix implemented so `o` creates work items in the active repository's worklog by default.
- Add tests that verify work item creation context for `o` in both project and global contexts.
- Update documentation to clarify where `o` stores work items and how the context is determined.
- No user data is written to `~/.config/opencode` unless explicitly requested.

Notes:
- Create any follow-up tasks for code audit, tests, and a patch to correct path resolution.
data.title: Critical: opencode 'o' creates work items in Sorra Agents (~/.config/opencode)

data.description:
Summary:
Display a contextual message in the work items pane when current filters return no visible open items.

Behavior:
- If filters match zero open items AND zero closed items -> show: "No items are available." (prominent, centered).
- If filters match zero open items but one or more closed items -> show: "Only closed items are available ‚Äî click \"Closed\" (bottom right) to view them." The UI should visually indicate the Closed control (bottom-right) and make the control keyboard accessible; clicking it toggles the closed view.

Acceptance criteria:
1) When openCount == 0 and closedCount == 0: pane shows "No items are available." and no list rows.
2) When openCount == 0 and closedCount > 0: pane shows the "Only closed items are available..." message with an inline CTA or visible pointer to the Closed filter/button; clicking it shows closed items.
3) Messages are localized and announced to screen readers (use ARIA live region or role="status").
4) Styling follows existing empty-state patterns (icon, small explanatory copy, inline CTA when applicable).
5) Tests: unit tests for message selection logic (based on openCount and closedCount) and an integration test for CTA toggling closed-state.

Implementation notes:
- Implement logic where the work items list decides empty-state copy; expose derived props: openCount, closedCount, currentFiltersDescription.
- Extract copy to i18n strings.
- Ensure accessibility and keyboard operability.

QA steps:
1) Apply filters that exclude all open and closed items -> verify "No items are available." appears.
2) Apply filters that exclude all open but match some closed items -> verify the "Only closed items are available..." message and that the CTA toggles closed view.

Related: discovered-from:WL-000
data.title: Work items pane: contextual empty-state message

Expected behavior:

- Reports items with invalid status or stage values, or incompatible combinations.

- JSON output uses fields: checkId, itemId, message, proposedFix, safe, context (no severity).

## Acceptance Criteria
1) Findings are produced for invalid status/stage pairs per docs/validation/status-stage-inventory.md.

4) JSON output uses required fields only.

data.description:
Summary

Pressing the Escape (ESC) key causes the application to exit entirely. ESC should not terminate the program; at most it should close a modal, cancel an in-progress action, or be ignored when no cancellable UI is open.

User stories

- As a user, I want pressing ESC to cancel or close the current dialog instead of quitting the app so I don't lose work unexpectedly.
- As a user, I expect explicit quit actions (menu > Quit, Ctrl+C in terminal, or a dedicated shortcut) to be required to terminate the program.

Expected behaviour

- Pressing ESC will close the topmost transient UI element (modal, dropdown, inline editor) or do nothing if there is no such element.
- Pressing ESC must not cause the application process to exit.

Steps to reproduce

1. Start the application.
2. Ensure there is no explicit quit confirmation shown.
3. Press the ESC key.
4. Observe that the application exits (current behaviour).

Suggested implementation

- Add a global key handler that intercepts ESC key events and routes them to UI components/menus rather than allowing process-level exit.
- Ensure terminal-level handlers (if any) do not translate ESC into SIGINT or process termination.
- Add unit/integration tests covering key handling and a manual QA checklist for desktop and terminal environments.

Acceptance criteria

1. Reproduction: before the fix, ESC exits the app on the reported platform(s).
2. After the fix, pressing ESC does not terminate the process in any tested environment.
3. Pressing ESC closes modals or cancels in-progress operations when appropriate.
4. Automated tests for ESC handling are added and pass in CI.
5. A comment or short note is added to the relevant input/key handling code explaining the change.

Notes / Implementation hints

- If the app uses a UI framework that already maps ESC to window close, override that mapping only in places where the behaviour is undesirable.
- Investigate whether terminal/TTY libraries (if applicable) are translating ESC sequences into control sequences that lead to exit.
- Add `discovered-from: none` in description if creating additional follow-ups is necessary.
data.title: ESC should not exit the program

data.description:
Summary:
Under certain circumstances the update dialog's keyboard handling registers every keypress three times (e.g. typing 'a' results in 'aaa'). This is reproducible and breaks text input in the dialog.

Why it's critical:
- Blocks users from reliably entering text into the update dialog
- Can cause data corruption or repeated commands

Reproduction steps:
1. Open the application and navigate to the Update dialog (Settings ‚Üí Update).
2. Click into the text input field (or focus it via keyboard).
3. Perform the steps that trigger the bug: observed when the dialog is opened while a background input listener is active ‚Äî preconditions: open Update dialog while global keyboard shortcut handler is enabled and the devtools console shows no errors.
4. Type any character; each keypress is inserted three times.

Clarification (2026-02-09):
- Triple registrations start after exiting the comment box; reproduce by focusing the comment box, then leaving it, then typing into the Update dialog input.

Observed behavior:
- Each single keypress is registered and inserted three times into the input field.

Expected behavior:
- Each keypress should be registered once.

Suggested root causes to investigate:
- Duplicate event listeners attached to the input or window (listener attached on each dialog open without removal).
- Global keyboard shortcut handler forwarding events to the dialog as well as the input.
- Race condition causing event handler to be bound multiple times.

Acceptance criteria:
- Fix prevents triple key registration for all input fields in the Update dialog under all app states where it previously occurred.
- Add unit or integration tests to cover single keypress behavior in the dialog.
- Add a small manual test checklist in the issue to verify behavior across platforms (Linux/macOS/Windows if supported).
- Add a short note to the changelog or release notes if behaviour change is user visible.

Suggested implementation approach:
1. Audit the update dialog lifecycle to find where keydown/keypress/keyup listeners are registered and ensure they are only added once and removed on dialog close.
2. Prefer attaching listeners to the input element rather than the global window if appropriate.
3. If global listeners must remain, guard handlers with a check to ignore events originated from input elements or ensure event.stopPropagation/stopImmediatePropagation is used properly.
4. Add automated tests that mount the dialog, simulate a single key event, and assert a single insertion.

Manual verification checklist:
- Open Update dialog, focus input, type text -> each key appears once.
- Reopen dialog multiple times -> typing still registers single keypress.
- Toggle global shortcuts on/off and verify behavior.

References and context:
- Observed during QA session 2026-02-08; no existing work item found in repo referencing this exact failure.

Origin: accidentally submitted to another project as SA-0MLDICHPG1GR6J8G.
data.title: Update dialog keyboard registers triple keypresses

- A documented explicit refresh API or command exists for manual/automated refresh.
- Tests verifying reduced import windows and absence of re-import loops.

data.description:
Introduce a file-based (flock) process-level mutex to serialize access to the JSONL data file so concurrent processes don't cause merge races or data corruption.

## Problem
Multiple CLI processes or the API server can simultaneously read/modify the shared worklog-data.jsonl file. The exportToJsonl() method in database.ts performs a read-merge-write cycle that is susceptible to TOCTOU races. refreshFromJsonlIfNewer() can interleave with exports from other processes. The sync command performs fetch-merge-import-export-push without any lock.

## User Story
As a developer using Worklog in a team environment, I want concurrent wl commands to safely serialize their access to the shared JSONL data file so that no data is lost or corrupted due to race conditions.

## Implementation Approach
- Create a new src/file-lock.ts module that implements file-based locking using Node.js fs.open with O_EXCL (advisory lock file)
- The lock file will be placed alongside the JSONL data file (e.g., worklog-data.jsonl.lock)
- Provide withFileLock(lockPath, fn) helper that acquires the lock, runs the callback, and releases it (with proper cleanup on error)
- Include retry logic with configurable timeout and backoff for waiting on a held lock
- Include stale lock detection (process ID recorded in lock file, checked on acquisition failure)
- Integrate locking into WorklogDatabase.exportToJsonl() and WorklogDatabase.refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()
- Integrate locking into the sync, import, and export command handlers
- Write tests that simulate concurrent processes to validate serialization

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new src/file-lock.ts module exists with withFileLock() and related helpers
- Import/export/sync operations acquire the lock before running and release after
- Stale locks (from crashed processes) are detected and cleaned up
- Tests simulate concurrent processes to validate serialization
- All existing tests continue to pass
- The lock does not introduce noticeable latency for single-process usage
data.title: Process-level mutex for import/export/sync

data.description:
# Audit comment improvements

Replace noisy, full-dump audit comments with structured, delimiter-bounded reports that include deep code-review verdicts for acceptance criteria on both parent and child work items.

## Problem statement

The AMPA triage scheduler's audit comments currently contain the entire raw output of `opencode run "/audit <id>"`, which includes tool call logs, intermediate reasoning, and other noise alongside the actual audit summary. Additionally, the audit skill's acceptance criteria validation is shallow -- it relies primarily on work item descriptions and comments rather than performing a thorough code review. This makes audit comments both noisy and insufficiently rigorous for verifying whether work is actually complete.

## Users

- **Producers reviewing work items:** As a producer, I want audit comments on work items to contain only a clear, structured status report with code-validated acceptance criteria verdicts so I can quickly and confidently understand the true state of each item without sifting through tool call noise.
- **Agents consuming audit comments:** As an automated agent, I want audit comments to follow a predictable structure with per-criterion verdicts so I can reliably extract status information for downstream processing (e.g. cooldown detection, delegation decisions, auto-close evaluation).
- **Discord notification consumers:** As a team member receiving Discord notifications, I want the triage audit summary to be extracted from a well-structured report so the notification is concise and accurate.

## Success criteria

1. The audit skill (`skill/audit/SKILL.md`) produces output with clearly defined delimiter markers (`--- AUDIT REPORT START ---` / `--- AUDIT REPORT END ---`) around the final structured report.
2. The structured report between the markers contains these sections in markdown: **Summary**, **Acceptance Criteria Status**, **Children Status**, and **Recommendation** (where applicable).
3. For the parent work item, the audit skill performs a deep code review for each acceptance criterion: reading the actual implementation code, assessing correctness, completeness, and edge cases. Each criterion receives a verdict (met/unmet/partial) with a one-line evidence note referencing the relevant file and line number.
4. For each child work item, the audit skill performs the same deep code review of acceptance criteria as for the parent. Each child's acceptance criteria are individually validated against the codebase with per-criterion verdicts and file references.
5. The triage audit runner (`ampa/triage_audit.py`) extracts only the content between the delimiter markers and posts that as the WL comment (under the existing `# AMPA Audit Result` heading).
6. The Discord notification is updated to extract the Summary section from the structured report (between delimiters) rather than applying regex heuristics to the full raw output.
7. When a work item or child has no acceptance criteria defined, the audit report notes this explicitly (e.g. "No acceptance criteria defined") rather than silently skipping the item.
8. Unit tests cover the marker extraction logic (including edge cases: missing markers, malformed output, empty report). A lightweight integration test verifies end-to-end format from the audit skill through to the posted comment structure.

## Constraints

- **Short-term fix:** This is an incremental improvement to comment quality and audit rigor. The broader initiative to replace comment-based audits with a structured `audit` field on work items (WL-0MLDJ34RQ1ODWRY0) remains a separate, future effort.
- **Breaking change acceptable:** The triage runner does not need to handle the old unstructured format. Once the audit skill is updated, old-format output can be dropped.
- **Truncation default unchanged:** The existing `truncate_chars` default (65536) is retained. Structured reports are expected to be much shorter in practice but the safety limit remains.
- **Audit skill is an OpenCode skill:** Changes to the audit skill output format are made in `~/.config/opencode/skill/audit/SKILL.md`, which is an instruction file for the AI agent -- not executable code. The skill instructions must be updated to mandate the structured output format with delimiters and the deep code review approach.
- **Triage runner is AMPA Python code:** The extraction and comment-posting logic lives in `~/.config/opencode/ampa/triage_audit.py` (and the legacy path in `~/.config/opencode/ampa/scheduler.py`).
- **Code review depth is instruction-driven:** The depth of code review depends on the AI agent following the skill instructions. The instructions should be explicit about reading implementation files, checking function signatures, verifying test coverage, and assessing edge cases -- not just checking that files exist.

## Existing state

- The audit skill (`skill/audit/SKILL.md`) produces freeform markdown output with a `# Summary` section. It does not use delimiter markers and does not enforce a consistent structure for all sections.
- The current skill mentions validating acceptance criteria "where appropriate, code in the repository" but this is vague. In practice the agent often relies on descriptions and comments rather than performing actual code review.
- The skill does not instruct the agent to review children's acceptance criteria against code -- it only lists child items by title, id, status, and stage.
- The triage audit runner (`triage_audit.py`) posts the entire stdout+stderr of `opencode run "/audit <id>"` as a WL comment under `# AMPA Audit Result`. It uses a regex-based `_extract_summary()` function to find a `Summary` section for Discord, but this is fragile and operates on the full raw output.
- Discord notifications currently receive the regex-extracted summary or a fallback of `<work-id> -- <title> | exit=<code>`.

## Desired change

1. **Audit skill update:** Modify `skill/audit/SKILL.md` to instruct the AI agent to:
   - Wrap the final report in `--- AUDIT REPORT START ---` and `--- AUDIT REPORT END ---` delimiters.
   - Structure the report with markdown headings: `## Summary`, `## Acceptance Criteria Status`, `## Children Status`, `## Recommendation`.
   - For the parent work item's acceptance criteria: perform a deep code review by reading the actual implementation code, assessing correctness and completeness against each criterion. Report each criterion as met/unmet/partial with a one-line evidence note including `file_path:line_number`.
   - For each child work item: extract the child's acceptance criteria and perform the same deep code review. Report per-criterion verdicts with file references. Present children as subsections under `## Children Status` with their own acceptance criteria tables.
   - Keep the report concise and actionable despite the deeper analysis.

2. **Triage runner update:** Modify `triage_audit.py` to:
   - Extract content between the `--- AUDIT REPORT START ---` and `--- AUDIT REPORT END ---` markers from the raw `opencode run` output.
   - Post only the extracted report as the WL comment (still under the `# AMPA Audit Result` heading).
   - If markers are not found, fall back to posting the full output (defensive behavior) but log a warning.

3. **Discord extraction update:** Update the Discord summary extraction to:
   - Parse the structured report (between markers) and extract the `## Summary` section.
   - Use this instead of the current regex heuristic on raw output.

4. **Tests:**
   - Unit tests for the marker extraction function covering: happy path, missing start marker, missing end marker, empty content between markers, multiple marker pairs (take first).
   - Unit tests for the updated Discord summary extraction.
   - Lightweight integration test verifying the audit skill output format contains the expected markers and sections.

## Risks & assumptions

- **AI compliance:** The deep code review behavior is instruction-driven. The AI agent may not consistently follow the skill instructions, producing varying output quality or omitting markers. Mitigation: the triage runner falls back to posting the full output and logs a warning when markers are missing.
- **Token/context budget:** Deep code review of parent + all children may exceed the AI agent's context window for work items with many children or large codebases. Mitigation: the skill instructions should cap the review to a reasonable depth (e.g. direct children only, not recursive grandchildren) and note when truncation occurs.
- **Runtime increase:** Deep code review will increase the duration of each audit run compared to the current shallow approach. Mitigation: the existing `_audit_timeout` / `AMPA_AUDIT_OPENCODE_TIMEOUT` configuration bounds execution time.
- **Scope creep:** This work item covers structured output format + deep code review of acceptance criteria. Additional ideas (e.g. structured audit field, delegation recommendations, auto-close improvements) should be tracked as separate work items (WL-0MLDJ34RQ1ODWRY0, WL-0MLI9B5T20UJXCG9) rather than expanding scope here.
- **Assumption: acceptance criteria format.** The skill assumes acceptance criteria are in a markdown section starting with `## Acceptance Criteria` formatted as a list. Work items that use a different format may have criteria missed or misidentified.

## Related work

- **Replace comment-based audits with structured audit field** (WL-0MLDJ34RQ1ODWRY0) - open, medium priority. Future effort to move audits out of comments entirely. This work item is a short-term improvement that does not conflict with that direction.
- **Scheduler: output recommendation when delegation audit_only=true** (WL-0MLI9B5T20UJXCG9) - open, medium priority. Discovered from this work item. Enhances audit output with delegation recommendations.
- **Only send in_progress report to Discord if content changed** (WL-0MLX37DT70815QXS) - open, medium priority. Involves Discord notification from the scheduler/triage system. The Discord summary extraction changes in success criterion 6 intersect with this item's shared notification logic.
- **Audit: SA-0MLHUQNHO13DMVXB** (WL-0MLOWKLZ90PVCP5M) - open, medium priority. An active audit task that will produce output in the new structured format once the skill is updated. Serves as a downstream consumer of these changes.

## Related work (automated report)

The following related items and files were discovered by automated search. Each entry describes its relevance to WL-0MLG60MK60WDEEGE.

### Related work items

| ID | Title | Status | Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| WL-0MLDJ34RQ1ODWRY0 | Replace comment-based audits with structured audit field | open | Future effort to move audits from comments to a structured field. This work item is the short-term incremental fix; that item is the long-term replacement. Both improve audit data quality but are independently scoped. |
| WL-0MLI9B5T20UJXCG9 | Scheduler: output recommendation when delegation audit_only=true | open | Discovered from this item. Adds a Recommendation section to audit output. The `## Recommendation` heading in the structured report format directly supports this item's goals. |
| WL-0MLX37DT70815QXS | Only send in_progress report to Discord if content changed | open | Involves Discord notification and shared helper code (`_summarize_for_discord`). Success criterion 6 changes how Discord summaries are extracted, which may interact with this item's deduplication logic. |
| WL-0MLOWKLZ90PVCP5M | Audit: SA-0MLHUQNHO13DMVXB | open | Active audit task that will consume the new structured output format. Once the audit skill is updated, this and all future audit runs produce delimiter-bounded reports. |

### Related files

| Path | Relevance |
|---|---|
| `~/.config/opencode/skill/audit/SKILL.md` | Primary target. Audit skill instructions to be updated with structured output format, delimiters, and deep code review mandates. |
| `~/.config/opencode/ampa/triage_audit.py` | Primary target. Contains `_extract_summary()` regex and comment-posting logic that must be updated for delimiter-based extraction. |
| `~/.config/opencode/ampa/scheduler.py` | Legacy triage-audit path with duplicate `_extract_summary()` and comment-posting code. Must be updated in parallel or confirmed as dead code. |
| `~/.config/opencode/tests/test_triage_audit.py` | Existing test file for the triage audit runner. Must be extended with marker extraction and Discord summary extraction tests. |
| `~/.config/opencode/ampa/delegation.py` | Contains `_summarize_for_discord()` used by `triage_audit.py`. Discord extraction update may change how this function's input is prepared. |
| `~/.config/opencode/docs/triage-audit.md` | Documentation for the AMPA triage-audit flow. Currently describes posting full audit output; must be updated to reflect delimiter-based extraction. |
| `~/.config/opencode/docs/workflow/examples/02-audit-failure.md` | Workflow example with sample `# AMPA Audit Result` comment format. Should be reviewed for consistency with the new structured report format. |

## Key files

- `~/.config/opencode/skill/audit/SKILL.md` - audit skill instructions (primary target)
- `~/.config/opencode/ampa/triage_audit.py` - triage audit runner, comment posting, Discord extraction (primary target)
- `~/.config/opencode/ampa/scheduler.py` - scheduler, legacy triage-audit path (primary target)
- `~/.config/opencode/tests/test_triage_audit.py` - existing tests to extend
- `~/.config/opencode/ampa/delegation.py` - Discord summarization helper
- `~/.config/opencode/docs/triage-audit.md` - triage-audit documentation
- `~/.config/opencode/docs/workflow/examples/02-audit-failure.md` - workflow example

data.description:
Add async versions of comment helpers and migrate comment upsert logic to use them.

## Details
- Implement listGithubIssueCommentsAsync, createGithubIssueCommentAsync, updateGithubIssueCommentAsync, getGithubIssueCommentAsync in src/github.ts using runGhJsonAsync helpers.
- Update src/github-sync.ts to use the async comment helpers and perform comment listing/upserts concurrently with a bounded worker pool.
- Add unit tests for the new async comment helpers and integration tests for comment upsert flows.

## Gap Analysis (Feb 2026)
### Already implemented:
- listGithubIssueCommentsAsync (src/github.ts:736)
- createGithubIssueCommentAsync (src/github.ts:756)
- updateGithubIssueCommentAsync (src/github.ts:770)
- upsertGithubIssueCommentsAsync in src/github-sync.ts already uses async helpers
- Bounded concurrency via WL_GITHUB_CONCURRENCY (default 6) is wired

### Remaining:
1. Add getGithubIssueCommentAsync (async variant of getGithubIssueComment)
2. Add dedicated unit tests for async comment helpers
3. Add integration tests for comment upsert flows
4. Verify no behavioral regressions

## Acceptance Criteria
1. New async comment helper functions exist and are exported (list, create, update, get).
2. github-sync.ts uses the async helpers for comments and runs comment upserts concurrently.
3. Tests added cover list/create/update/get comment flows.
4. No behavioral regressions in existing tests.

Parent: WL-0MLGAYUH614TDKC5

data.description:
# Async list issues and repo helpers / central throttler ‚Äî WL-0MLGBAPEO1QGMTGM

Problem statement
- GitHub-related async helpers are in place, but requests are coordinated only via per-function bounded concurrency (e.g., `WL_GITHUB_CONCURRENCY` in `src/github-sync.ts`). There is no central throttler/token-bucket to enforce global request rates and bursts across all async GitHub calls, which makes it harder to reason about and control overall API usage and to simulate rate-limit behavior in tests.

Users
- Engineers and automation that run `wl github push`, `wl github sync`, and other GitHub-backed sync flows; CI or agent-run automation performing bulk syncs; maintainers who need predictable, testable API usage.

Example user stories
- As an engineer running `wl github push`, I want requests to GitHub to be globally rate-limited so I avoid secondary-rate-limit/abuse responses during large syncs.
- As a maintainer, I want a central throttler utility with clear config (env or config.yaml) so I can tune behavior per-environment.
 
Success criteria
1) Implement a reusable token-bucket throttler utility and export a single shared instance for GitHub calls; default behaviour uses token-bucket refill.
2) Migrate all async GitHub call sites (github helpers and callers, including `src/github-sync.ts`) to use the throttler so concurrency/rate is enforced globally.
3) Add unit tests for the throttler (token-bucket semantics) and integration tests exercising `github-sync` under simulated load and rate-limit conditions.
4) Document the configuration surface: support `WL_GITHUB_CONCURRENCY` (concurrency cap), `WL_GITHUB_RATE` (requests/sec) and `WL_GITHUB_BURST` (bucket size); provide defaults: `WL_GITHUB_CONCURRENCY=6`, `WL_GITHUB_RATE=6`, `WL_GITHUB_BURST=12`.
5) Update repository docs to explain throttler behaviour and how to tune the env vars.

Constraints
- Must avoid changing GitHub API semantics; throttler only changes client-side scheduling.
- Keep implementation small, dependency-free (vanilla TS/JS), and testable offline (use injectable clocks/mocks for timing in unit tests).
- Backwards-compatible: keep honoring `WL_GITHUB_CONCURRENCY` where used, but prefer centralized throttler when available.

Existing state
- `src/github-sync.ts` already uses `Number(process.env.WL_GITHUB_CONCURRENCY || '6')` in two places to bound concurrency for upserts and hierarchy checks. Many async helpers exist in `src/github.ts` (async variants, create/update/get/list helpers). No central throttler file or tests for a throttler were found.

Desired change
- Add `src/github-throttler.ts` (or similar) implementing a token-bucket with optional concurrency cap; export a default/shared instance wired by `config` helpers or env vars. Replace local concurrency pools in `src/github-sync.ts` and update other async GH callers to use the centralized throttler API (e.g., `throttler.schedule(() => helperAsync(...))`). Add unit and integration tests and documentation updates.

Related work
- `src/github-sync.ts` ‚Äî existing sync logic and current per-function concurrency usage (see `upsertConcurrency` & `concurrency`).
- `src/github.ts` ‚Äî async GH helpers to be wrapped or called via throttler.
- WL-0MLGBABBK0OJETRU ‚Äî "Async comment helpers and comment upsert migration" (completed) ‚Äî shows async migration pattern and tests for comments; may be useful for migration examples.

Please review this draft and confirm or provide edits for scope, defaults, and test expectations.

data.description:
Summary:
Add a boolean field `needs_producer_review` to work items to indicate when an AI has performed an action and requires a Producer's sign-off.

User story:
As an AI agent, when I perform an action that requires a Producer to sign off, I want to mark the work item so Producers can quickly find and review these items.

Expected behavior / acceptance criteria:
- Add a persistent boolean field `needs_producer_review` (default: false) to the work item model/storage.
- APIs and CLI that return work item data include the new field where applicable.
- UI (TUI/other interfaces) display the flag on work item views and lists.
- When an AI performs an automated action that requires sign-off, it will set the flag to `true`.
- Producers can observe, filter and act on flagged items.
- Include tests and migration(s) as needed, and update documentation.

Implementation notes:
- Database/schema migration to add the boolean field (or equivalent storage change).
- Update the worklog service/model, CLI output formats, and any JSON APIs to include the flag.
- Ensure appropriate permissions and audit/logging for who sets/unsets the flag.

Risk/notes:

- Update metadata.schemaVersion only after successful application.
- Provide opt-out flags: global  and env var .
- Provide  and  to preview or run migrations manually.
- Log migration actions; include JSON output for machine users.
- Add tests for dry-run, apply, idempotence, and integration tests simulating older schema versions.

Suggested implementation approach:
1. Create a migration runner module (e.g., ) that exposes  and ; each migration is a small function with id, description, and  method.
2. Call the migration runner from  on open and run migrations if needed unless opted out.
3. Keep automatic runner limited to safe, non-destructive migrations; complex migrations require explicit  with backup guidance.
4. Ensure metadata.schemaVersion is updated inside a transaction and migration is idempotent.
5. Add CLI help and documentation.
