data.description:
Problem statement
Run a safe, idempotent schema migration to add the `needsProducerReview` INTEGER column to the `workitems` table and provide a repeatable, documented, and automatable upgrade path via `wl doctor` so the application can run migrations manually or automatically after an upgrade.

Users
- Maintainers and Producers: need the DB to be up-to-date so CLI commands (`wl create`, `wl update`, `wl list`) work without errors and new fields are queryable.
- Developers and automation agents: need an automated, auditable migration path that can run on first command after upgrading the application or be run manually in CI or by operators.

Success criteria
- `wl doctor upgrade --dry-run` lists pending migrations including the `needsProducerReview` ADD COLUMN migration; `wl doctor upgrade --confirm` applies them.
- After applying the migration, `PRAGMA table_info('workitems')` shows the `needsProducerReview` column and existing rows report 0 (false) by default.
- The migration process creates a timestamped backup of `.worklog/worklog.db` (retain last 5 backups) before applying changes and fails if backup cannot be created.
- Automatic run behavior: on first command after an upgrade, non-safe migrations are notified and applied only after acknowledgement; safe non-destructive migrations may be auto-applied per policy; CI environments do not auto-apply migrations.
- Operations are idempotent: re-running the same migration does not fail or create duplicate schema changes.

Constraints
- Migration tooling must respect environment and flags: WL_AUTO_MIGRATE (opt-out), CI=true disables automatic application, and `wl doctor upgrade --dry-run` must be available.
- Backups must be automatic and pruned to the last 5; a failed backup prevents migration.
- By default the system will notify and then apply destructive migrations only after explicit confirmation (interactive or `--confirm`); non-destructive migrations may be applied automatically per the chosen policy.
- Application version is read from `package.json` and schemaVersion is stored in DB metadata.

Existing state
- The codebase references the new `needsProducerReview` field in several places (CLI flags, persistence, JSONL) but some installations lack the DB column and see SQLite errors referencing a missing column.
- There is an existing `wl doctor` command and a set of doctor checks and a `doctor --fix` pipeline that handles safe fixes (see `src/commands/doctor.ts`, `src/doctor/*.ts`).
- Current manual migration guidance exists in work item WL-0MLGVVMR70IC1S8F (this item) and the immediate problem has an acceptance test suggested (run `ALTER TABLE` and verify PRAGMA).

Desired change
- Implement a migration runner and integrate it into `wl doctor` with:
  - `wl doctor upgrade --dry-run` to preview migrations (JSON output and human summary).
  - `wl doctor upgrade --confirm` to apply migrations non-interactively (for automation) and interactive flow otherwise.
  - Automatic timestamped DB backup creation (retain last 5) before applying migrations.
  - SchemaVersion stored in DB metadata and compared to application `package.json` version; migrations keyed by id and target schemaVersion.
  - Migration policies: notify-then-apply for destructive migrations, allow operator to opt-in, auto-apply non-destructive migrations depending on WL_AUTO_MIGRATE and CI.

Related work
- Work items:
  - WL-0MLGTKW490NJTOAB ‚Äî Add Needs Producer Review flag to work items (parent feature)
  - WL-0MLGW90490U5Q5Z0 ‚Äî Implement migration runner module (planning task created)
  - WL-0MLGW91WT0P3XS5T ‚Äî Wire migration runner into SqlitePersistentStore (planning task created)
  - WL-0MLGW93H91HMMUOT ‚Äî Add 'wl migrate' / 'wl doctor upgrade' CLI commands (planning task created)
  - WL-0MKRPG64S04PL1A6 ‚Äî Worklog doctor intake brief and doctor command (related doctor work)

- Potentially related docs / files:
  - `src/commands/doctor.ts` ‚Äî doctor CLI wiring
  - `src/doctor/fix.ts`, `src/doctor/status-stage-check.ts`, `src/doctor/dependency-check.ts` ‚Äî existing doctor checks/fix pipeline
  - `src/persistent-store.ts` ‚Äî current SQLite schema creation and initializeSchema logic
  - `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` and `.worklog/worklog.db` ‚Äî canonical datastore and examples
  - `CLI.md` ‚Äî CLI docs to update with `wl doctor upgrade` usage

Notes / decisions captured from interview
- Default behavior: Automatic migration defaults to notifying the user then applying migrations for which the user provided consent; destructive migrations require explicit confirmation (interactive or `--confirm`). User chose: allow destructive with confirmation.
- Storage: use DB metadata for schemaVersion and read application version from `package.json`.
- Backup policy: always create an automatic timestamped backup before applying migrations and retain the last 5 backups; fail if backup cannot be made.
- CI: automatic migrations are disabled when CI=true; CI runs should require `wl doctor upgrade --confirm` to proceed.

Suggested next step (implementation)
1) Implement the migration runner module (`src/migrations/index.ts`) that exposes `listPendingMigrations` and `runMigrations({dryRun, confirm, logger})` and includes the `ADD COLUMN needsProducerReview` migration as idempotent.

Present for review
Please review this draft for completeness and correctness. If you approve, I will:
 - Update work item WL-0MLGVVMR70IC1S8F description with this intake brief and move it to `intake_complete`.
 - Create the migration runner implementation and tests (referencing WL-0MLGW90490U5Q5Z0) and add the CLI `wl doctor upgrade` wiring.

Summary: Add a non-fatal warning when an existing sqlite DB opens with schemaVersion < SCHEMA_VERSION, instructing the operator to run 'wl doctor upgrade'.

User story: As an operator, when opening an older DB I want a clear non-fatal warning telling me to run 'wl doctor upgrade' so schema upgrades are applied audibly and backups are created.

Acceptance criteria:
1) When SqlitePersistentStore opens an existing DB and detects metadata.schemaVersion < SCHEMA_VERSION, it logs a single non-fatal warning (console.warn or the repo logger) recommending 'wl doctor upgrade' and pointing to the migration id list.
2) The warning is NOT shown for newly created DBs or when running in test-mode (NODE_ENV=test or JEST_WORKER_ID set) to preserve test compatibility.
3) No automatic ALTERs or schema changes are performed as a result of this check.
4) Unit tests can override behavior via environment variables if needed.

Suggested implementation: Update src/persistent-store.ts to check metadata.schemaVersion after opening DB and emit a clear warning if it's older. Include a brief doc-comment referencing the migrations centralization policy.

Related work items: WL-0MLGVVMR70IC1S8F, WL-0MLGXWJSY1SZCIJ7

data.description:
Problem statement

Provide a single‚Äëkey TUI shortcut to toggle a "do-not-delegate" marker on the currently selected work item so users can prevent the item from being auto-assigned by automation or agents. The toggle should be discoverable in the help overlay, testable, and also exposable from the CLI.

Users
- Producers and Team Leads: want to mark items that should not be automatically assigned by agents or automation (for special handling or manual assignment).
- Keyboard-first TUI users: want a fast, single-key toggle accessible from the item list or detail view.
- Automation scripts / tooling: should be able to set/clear the marker from the CLI for non-interactive flows.

User stories
- As a Producer, I want to mark an item so agents do not auto-assign it, using the keyboard without opening a full edit UI.
- As a keyboard-first user, I want to press a single key to toggle the setting and receive immediate feedback (toast + list marker).
- As an automation operator, I want to set the same marker non-interactively via `wl update <id> --do-not-delegate true` in CI or scripts.

Success criteria
- Pressing `D` while an item is focused toggles the `do-not-delegate` tag on that item and shows a toast: "Do-not-delegate: ON" / "Do-not-delegate: OFF".
- The item's list row and detail pane display a visible marker/icon when the tag is present.
- A CLI convenience flag `--do-not-delegate true|false` can add/remove the tag and is documented in `CLI.md`.
- Unit tests cover TUI key handling, toast feedback, tag add/remove, and the new CLI flag; automated tests pass locally.
- The change is implemented as idempotent tag add/remove and does not require a DB schema migration.

Constraints
- Persist the setting as a tag named `do-not-delegate` on the work item (no DB schema changes).
- Follow existing TUI keybinding patterns and use centralized constants in `src/tui/constants.ts` when present.
- Avoid breaking existing hotkeys and respect help overlay conventions.
- Keep behavior idempotent: toggling an already-present tag is a no-op except for feedback.

Existing state
- There is an existing work item: WL-0MLHNPSGP0N397NX titled "Do not auto-assign shortcut" with a short description; it is at stage `idea` and assigned to Map.
- The TUI already supports many shortcuts (R, N, U, /, etc.); examples and tests exist showing how to add keybindings, update help, and test behavior.
- `src/tui/constants.ts`, `src/tui/controller.ts`, and `src/tui/components/help-menu.ts` are the primary places to add the new binding and help entry.
- The CLI already supports tag add/remove patterns; the proposed `--do-not-delegate` convenience flag will call the existing tag update path.

Desired change
- Add a TUI keyboard shortcut `D` that toggles the `do-not-delegate` tag on the currently selected item.
  - Show a toast message indicating new state and update the row/detail marker immediately.
  - Update the help overlay to include the `D` shortcut entry.
- Add a CLI convenience option: `wl update <id> --do-not-delegate true|false` which maps to tag add/remove.
- Add unit tests for the TUI handler, toast/marker behavior, and the CLI flag.
- Update `CLI.md` and any in-TUI help text to document the flag and tag name.

Related work
- Work items:
  - WL-0MKX5ZV9M0IZ8074 ‚Äî Centralize commands and constants (in_progress) ‚Äî affects where to register/document the shortcut.
  - WL-0MLK58NHL1G8EQZP ‚Äî Extract TUI keyboard shortcuts to constants (in_progress) ‚Äî relevant for implementation location.
  - WL-0MLGW96KF1YLIVQ3 ‚Äî Add 'wl reviewed' CLI helper and TUI support (idea) ‚Äî example pattern for CLI+TUI toggle helpers.
  - WL-0MKVZ5TN71L3YPD1 ‚Äî TUI UX improvements (epic) ‚Äî umbrella for TUI shortcuts and help updates.

Potentially related files
- `src/tui/constants.ts` ‚Äî add/update named constant for `D` key and help text.
- `src/tui/controller.ts` ‚Äî attach handler to toggle tag and update UI.
- `src/tui/components/help-menu.ts` ‚Äî add the `D` entry to help overlay.
- `src/commands/tui.ts` ‚Äî TUI registration entrypoint (for context).
- `CLI.md` ‚Äî document `--do-not-delegate` flag.

Notes / decisions captured from interview
- Persist as a tag: `do-not-delegate` (no DB migration required).
- CLI flag: `--do-not-delegate true|false` to add/remove tag via existing update code path.
- GitHub label sync: handled automatically by existing code when tags map to labels; if not, surface an implementation note ‚Äî but no explicit GitHub label sync is required as part of this work.

Suggested next step (implementation)
1) Implement the TUI and CLI changes on a feature branch:
   - Add `D` key binding to `src/tui/constants.ts` and hook into `src/tui/controller.ts` to toggle the tag using existing tag update helpers.
   - Add help overlay entry in `src/tui/components/help-menu.ts`.
   - Add a CLI convenience flag handler in the update command path to accept `--do-not-delegate true|false` and translate to tag add/remove.
   - Add unit tests for the TUI handler, toast feedback, marker rendering, and the CLI flag.
   - Update `CLI.md` and in-TUI help copy.

Please review this draft for completeness and clarity. If you approve I will run the five conservative reviews and then update the work item WL-0MLHNPSGP0N397NX description and stage to `intake_complete` per the process.

(End of draft)

data.description:
Summary: When the scheduler runs delegation tasks with audit_only=true it currently records audit comments but does not provide a forward-looking recommendation about where work will be focused. Producers need hints about what the system will prioritize next so they can plan and triage proactively.

User story: As a Producer, when I review scheduler audit-only runs, I want to see a concise recommendation of which work items or areas the scheduler intends to act on (e.g., likely delegations, high-priority items), so I can pre-emptively prepare or reassign work.

Expected behaviour:
- When the delegation scheduler runs with  it should create/update an audit comment that includes a short Recommendation section summarizing where the scheduler is likely to focus next (e.g., top 3 items by priority or categories and rationale).
- The recommendation should be concise (1-3 bullets) and derived from the same analysis used for delegation decisions.
- Default to non-actionable phrasing (hints) ‚Äî it must not perform delegations in audit-only mode.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Add a recommendation section to the existing audit comment output when  is set.
2. Recommendation includes up to 3 targeted hints (work item ids/titles or categories) and a one-line rationale.
3. Unit or integration tests cover the scheduling behaviour with  verifying comment content and format.
4. Documentation updated (changelog or scheduler README) describing the new audit recommendation behaviour.

Implementation notes/suggestions:
- Reuse the scheduler's decision scoring logic to select top candidates; redact sensitive details if needed.
- Keep the recommendation generation toggleable by config and/or feature-flag if useful.
- Tag the work item with , , .

Related: discovered-from:WL-0MLG60MK60WDEEGE
data.title: Scheduler: output recommendation when delegation audit_only=true

data.description:
Integrate the existing ChordHandler (src/tui/chords.ts) into the TUI controller to replace the legacy Ctrl-W state machine.

Summary:
- Replace legacy ctrlWPending state and helper functions with a ChordHandler instance.
- Register Ctrl-W sequences (w, p, h, l, j, k) mapping to existing controller actions and preserve guard checks (help menu, modals).
- Wire key events to chordHandler.feed in the central keypress handler so chords are consumed appropriately.
- Preserve existing suppression flags (suppressNextP, lastCtrlWKeyHandled) and timeouts to maintain UX.

Acceptance criteria:
- Ctrl-W chord behavior is handled by ChordHandler instead of legacy state.
- All existing TUI tests pass (npm test).
- No duplicate variables or TypeScript errors introduced.

Suggested approach:
1. Create chordHandler in TuiController.start scope: const chordHandler = new ChordHandler({ timeoutMs: 2000 });
2. Register sequences with closures that call existing functions and set suppression flags where needed.
3. Remove legacy ctrlW* variables/functions and per-widget attach hooks.
4. Feed keys centrally via screen.on('keypress') into chordHandler.feed(key) and treat consumption accordingly.

Related files: src/tui/chords.ts, src/tui/controller.ts
data.title: Integrate ChordHandler into TUI controller

data.description:
Summary:
Provide a project-level, ordered 'next tasks' queue that is independent of an item's stored priority or sort order. The queue gives Producers the ability to nominate specific work-items that agents (and the scheduler) must prefer above all other open items until the queue is empty. The queue will be editable via both a CLI and a small TUI for adding, removing, and re-ordering entries. The scheduler will include the queue in its delegation report and the agents' selection logic will prefer items from this queue (in order) when the queue is non-empty.

User stories:
- As a Producer, I can add existing worklog items to a global Next Tasks Queue so agents focus on a specific feature set.
- As a Producer, I can remove items from the queue.
- As a Producer, I can re-order items in the queue (move up/down or set absolute position).
- As a Producer, I can open a simple TUI to visually reorder and manage the queue.
- As an agent/scheduler, when the Next Tasks Queue is non-empty, selection logic prefers items from this queue (in queue order) regardless of item priority/sort order. If the queue is empty, existing selection criteria are used.

Expected behaviour / Acceptance criteria:
1. Implement a new persistent queue resource (e.g. managed by wl and stored in an internal file or via the worklog backend) that records an ordered list of work-item ids.
2. CLI: , , , ,  and  support. All commands return non-zero exit codes and clear errors on invalid input.
3. TUI: a small curses-based interface  that lists queue entries with controls to add/remove/reorder and confirm changes; works in a standard terminal.
4. Permissions: only users with Producer role can modify the queue; others can view.
5. Scheduler integration: the scheduler includes the queue in its delegation report and selection logic prefers queue items when present. Provide a flag to include/exclude queue items in a run for testing.
6. Persistence & safety: queue survives restarts; conflicting edits are handled safely (optimistic locking or simple single-writer restriction).
7. Tests: unit tests for CLI and scheduler integration tests that validate queue precedence and empty-queue fallback.
8. Documentation: update developer docs and add short usage notes for Producers.

Suggested implementation approach:
- Add a  subsystem in the Worklog code that exposes an API and persistence (file or DB-backed) and a wl command plugin  implementing the CLI and  subcommand for the TUI.
- Integrate scheduler to read  when preparing delegation reports and to prefer listed items.
- Add role checks to the wl CLI to restrict mutation to Producers.
- Add automated tests and CI checks.

Related notes:
- Output of the queue must be included in the scheduler's delegation report (see scheduler/delegation_report integration).
- Keep the queue implementation simple and robust; prefer a single source of truth under  or an internal Worklog backend table rather than ad-hoc file edits.

Acceptance criteria (measurable):
- CLI and TUI commands exist and pass unit tests.
- Scheduler delegation report includes queue content when present.
- Agents select items from the queue in declared order when the queue is non-empty.
- Only Producers can modify the queue.
data.title: Next Tasks Queue

data.description:
Headline
Display clickable dependency links ("Blocking" and "Blocked by") in the TUI Details pane so users can transiently inspect related work without leaving the TUI.

Problem statement
When displaying issue metadata in the TUI Details pane, dependency relationships are not shown as actionable links. Users need clear, clickable dependency output so they can quickly inspect related work from the Details pane (e.g. "Blocking" and "Blocked by").

Users
- Developers and triage engineers who read work item metadata in the TUI.
- Example user stories:
  - As a developer, I want to click a dependency shown in the Details pane and open that work item's details so I can quickly review blockers and follow-ups.
  - As a release coordinator, I want to see whether an item is "Blocking" or "Blocked by" at a glance so I can prioritise actions.

Success criteria
- Details pane shows dependency sections for both "Blocking" and "Blocked by" when applicable.
- Each dependency is rendered as a link labelled "WL-<id> ‚Äî <short title>" and activating it opens the referenced item using the TUI's existing transient details behaviour.
- When there are more than 5 dependencies in a section, the pane shows the first 5 and a "+N more" indicator that can be expanded to reveal the rest.
- If no dependencies exist, the Details pane shows "Dependencies: None" (or equivalent messaging).
- Changes are implemented in the TUI codebase and covered by at least one unit or integration test that verifies rendering and activation behaviour.

Constraints
- Respect existing TUI navigation patterns: activation should use the same transient modal/inspection behaviour already used for clicking IDs in the Details pane.
- Do not open external browsers from the TUI for dependency navigation.
- Keep the Details pane layout responsive to terminal sizes (avoid unbounded growth‚Äîuse +N more or collapse).

Existing state
- Current work item: WL-0MLLGKZ7A1HUL8HU (this intake). Title: "Add links to dependencies in the metadata output of the details pane in the TUI." Description currently says: "When displaying issue metadata in the TUI Details pane include dependencies if there are any. Show \"Dependencies: None\" if there are none and \"Blocking: <ID>, <ID>\" and \"Blocked by: <ID>, <ID>\" if there are some".
- Relevant code locations:
  - src/tui/components/detail.ts ‚Äî Details pane component (rendering area, copy-id button)
  - src/tui/components/index.ts ‚Äî components entry (wiring)
  - src/tui/controller.ts ‚Äî selection/interaction controller
  - src/tui/state.ts ‚Äî work item selection/state handling

Desired change
- Render "Blocking" and "Blocked by" sections in the Details pane when dependencies exist.
- For each dependency, render an interactive label: "WL-<id> ‚Äî <short title>". Activation should open the referenced item using the same transient details/modal behaviour that existing ID clicks use.
- When a section has more than 5 entries, show first 5 and a "+N more" control to expand the rest.
- If a dependency has an associated GitHub issue number, do not expose an external link in the default UI.
- Add tests verifying rendering, truncation (+N more), and activation behaviour.

Related work
- Files (potentially relevant):
  - src/tui/components/detail.ts ‚Äî detail pane implementation used to render content and host interactive widgets.
  - src/tui/controller.ts ‚Äî input handling and navigation (may require wiring to support link activation).
  - src/tui/state.ts ‚Äî work item selection and lookup utilities.
- Worklog items:
  - Add wl dep command (WL-0ML2VPUOT1IMU5US) ‚Äî related feature for dependency tracking; may inform data shapes.
  - Enable description to be a file (WL-0MKXAUQYM1GEJUAN) ‚Äî shows prior work on description handling and intake file conventions.

Related work (automated report)

- WL-0ML2VPUOT1IMU5US ‚Äî Add wl dep command
  The completed 'Add wl dep command' feature implements CLI commands to record, remove, and list dependency edges. This is directly relevant as it defines the CLI-side shape and human/JSON output for dependency edges that the TUI Details pane should display and link to.

- WL-0ML4TFSGF1SLN4DT ‚Äî Persist dependency edges
  This work item implements persistent storage for dependency edges (data layer and JSONL embedding). It is relevant because the Details pane must read and render dependency relationships that are persisted by these storage changes.

- WL-0ML4TFT1V1Z0SHGF ‚Äî wl dep list
  This item implements the 'dep list' human output with inbound/outbound sections. It documents expected presentation of dependency lists (sectioning and fields) which can inform the Details pane layout and truncation behaviour (+N more).

- src/tui/components/detail.ts
  The Details pane component hosts the detail content and interactive widgets. This is the primary location to render dependency sections and attach activation handlers for clickable dependency entries.

- src/tui/controller.ts
  The TUI controller wires selection, input handling, and activation behaviour; it contains utilities for ID decoration and click/activation handling. Activation of a dependency link should reuse the controller's transient details/modal behaviour described here.

- src/tui/state.ts
  State handling (itemsById, childrenMap, lookup utilities) is used by the TUI to resolve work item titles and lookup referenced IDs. Ensure any rendering of "WL-<id> ‚Äî <short title>" uses the same lookup/data shapes provided by this module.

Notes and conservative decisions:
- I only included completed/explicit dependency work items and the small set of TUI files that clearly host the Details pane and interaction logic. I avoided more distant docs or tests even if they mention "dependency" to reduce false positives.
- Suggested next step: append this report under a clearly labelled "Related work (automated report)" section in WL-0MLLGKZ7A1HUL8HU, then (optionally) link these work items in the intake description using their WL-IDs.

Risks & assumptions

- Risk: scope creep ‚Äî adding UI affordances can lead to requests for richer dependency management (edit, add, remove) during implementation. Mitigation: record any additional feature requests as separate work items and keep this work focused on read-only rendering and navigation.
- Risk: performance/latency when resolving many referenced items for titles. Mitigation: use cached lookups where available and limit initial render to first 5 entries per section.
- Risk: terminal size/layout constraints may make lists hard to read. Mitigation: use truncation (+N more) and ensure expand behaviour is keyboard/mouse accessible.
- Assumption: dependency edges are stored and available via existing state/lookup utilities in `src/tui/state.ts` or via controller helpers.

Decisions captured from interview

Decisions captured from interview
- Activation behaviour: Open in the TUI details pane using the existing transient modal/inspection behaviour (consistent with current ID click behaviour).
- Sections to show: both "Blocking" and "Blocked by".
- Large lists: show first 5 with a "+N more" indicator (user can expand to see all).
- Link label: show "WL-<id> ‚Äî <short title>" for context.
- External GitHub links: do not include; keep behaviour internal only.

Next step
This draft has been reviewed and approved for conservative intake reviews. If you want further changes, reply with edits; otherwise the intake is ready for planning and implementation.
data.title: Add links to dependencies in the metadata output of the details pane in the TUI.

data.description:
Summary:
resolveWorklogDir() in  (referenced at line 45) always calls  which runs  unconditionally. That causes a synchronous subprocess invocation on a hot path for every CLI/API call even when a  file exists in the current working directory and no fallback to repo-root is needed.

Why this is a problem:
- Adds a blocking  subprocess to every invocation of CLI/API that uses , increasing latency and CPU usage on hot paths.
- Impacts runtime performance across workflows, particularly in environments with many fast CLI calls.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Ensure a repo with a  file in the current working directory.
2. Instrument or observe  path (or run the CLI that calls it).
3. Observe that /home/rogardle/projects/Worklog (via ) is executed even though  exists in cwd.

Expected behaviour:
- If  exists in the current working directory,  should return that path without invoking .
-  (and therefore ) should only be called lazily when the code actually needs to compare or fallback to the repo root  location.

Suggested fix:
- Modify  to compute  lazily: check for  in cwd first and only call  if the check fails or when an explicit comparison against repo root is required.
- Add unit tests covering both code paths: (a)  exists in cwd ‚Äî no git invocation; (b)  absent in cwd ‚Äî call to  and correct fallback.
- Add a micro-benchmark or integration test that asserts no subprocess is spawned when  is present.

Files/lines of interest:
- : around line 45 (where  calls )

Acceptance criteria:
1) New bug tracked in wl (created).
2) Code change that defers calling  until necessary; no  when  exists in cwd.
3) Unit tests added verifying both paths and asserting that  is not called when not needed (use spies/mocks).
4) Performance regression avoided: baseline benchmark or a CI check that ensures the hot path avoids the subprocess.
5) Commit(s) reference the wl id and a wl comment is added with the commit hash when changes are made.

Related: discovered while reviewing  at line 45; likely introduced as an eager-safety measure but harms hot-path performance.
data.title: resolveWorklogDir calls git unconditionally causing sync subprocess on hot path

data.description:
Summary: Implement a 'wl doctor prune' subcommand that permanently prunes soft-deleted work items older than a specified age and removes their dependency edges and comments.

User story: As a maintainer I want to permanently remove old soft-deleted work items to reclaim storage and remove stale edges/comments, while having a dry-run mode to preview deletions.

Behavior:
- New CLI: 'wl doctor prune'
- Options: '--days <n>' (age threshold in days, default 30), '--dry-run' (preview only), '--prefix <prefix>'
- JSON output supported: returns candidate ids/count for dry-run and prunedIds/count for actual run
- Deleted items must have dependency edges and comments removed to avoid dangling references

Implementation notes (work done):
- Added 'prune' subcommand to src/commands/doctor.ts which: selects items where status === 'deleted' and updatedAt (or createdAt) is older than cutoff; supports dry-run and JSON output; performs deletion via the persistent store when not dry-run.
- Deletion uses the internal persistent store 'deleteWorkItem' (SqlitePersistentStore.deleteWorkItem) when available; this ensures dependency edges and comments are removed via cascade and additional store helpers.
- Fallbacks included: if persistent store delete not available, falls back to WorklogDatabase.delete (soft-delete) to avoid crashing.
- Files changed: src/commands/doctor.ts (added prune command and deletion logic)

Acceptance criteria:
1) 'wl doctor prune --dry-run --days 90' lists candidate ids and count (no DB changes).
2) 'wl doctor prune --days 90' permanently removes candidates and returns pruned ids and count (or human summary).
3) Dependency edges and comments for pruned items are removed from DB.
4) Command supports '--prefix' and JSON output.
5) Unit tests covering dry-run and actual prune behavior are added.
6) CLI docs (CLI.md) updated to document 'wl doctor prune', flags, and recommended backup guidance before running.

Notes/Follow-ups:
- Add unit tests for dry-run, actual prune, and edge/comment removal.
- Add CLI docs update (CLI.md) to document 'doctor prune' and backup guidance.

Files changed during implementation: src/commands/doctor.ts
data.title: Doctor: prune soft-deleted work items

data.description:
TUI: 50/50 split layout with metadata & details panes ‚Äî Intake Draft (WL-0MLORPQUE1B7X8C3)

Problem statement
- The TUI lacks a clear, single-screen layout that shows the Work Items Tree, item metadata, and selected item's description/comments together; users must switch views or context to see related information.

Users
- Primary: TUI users (contributors, producers) who browse, triage, and comment on work items from the terminal.
- Example user stories:
  - As a contributor, I want to view the work items tree and the metadata for the selected item side-by-side so I can triage without switching screens.
  - As a producer, I want to read and add comments to the selected item while seeing its metadata so I can make quick decisions and record rationale.

Success criteria
- On start the layout renders a vertical split: top half (‚âà50% height) and bottom half (‚âà50% height).
- Top half is horizontally split: left pane ‚âà65% width containing the Work Items Tree, right pane the Metadata pane showing state, stage, priority, #comments, tags, assignee, created_at, updated_at.
- Bottom half shows Description (top) and Comments (below), both scrollable; adding a comment via the input updates the comments list and #comments in metadata.
- Selecting an item in the Work Items Tree highlights it, updates the Metadata pane and bottom pane immediately.
- Focus can be moved between panes using Tab/Shift-Tab (tree ‚Üí metadata ‚Üí comment input) and existing tree navigation keys remain unchanged.
- Responsive behaviour: layout adapts to terminal sizes; verified at least on 80x24 and 120x40 terminal sizes.
- Automated test: an integration TUI test exercises selection propagation and comment creation, asserting metadata updates and visible comment count change.

Constraints
- Scope: TUI-only (front-end/layout and wiring). Do not change backend schema or add API endpoints in this work item; if missing fields are discovered create follow-up work items.
- Reuse existing components and CLI/DB wiring where possible (Work Items Tree, description/comment creation endpoints are available via existing `wl` commands / db.update flows).
- Preserve keyboard-first experience and existing keybindings except for adding Tab/Shift-Tab to cycle focus.

Existing state
- Current TUI code is concentrated in `src/commands/tui.ts` with many related modules under `src/tui/*` (layout, state, handlers, persistence). There is existing work to modularize and test TUI components.
- There are related items and PRs in the worklog that document refactors, tests, and smaller TUI features. See "Related work" below.

Desired change
- Implement a layout with: top vertical split (50/50), top-left Work Items Tree (~65% width), top-right MetadataPane, bottom Description+Comments pane with comment input.
- Create or reuse small components: `MetadataPane`, `DescriptionView`, `CommentsList` (integrate with existing comment create API/path).
- Wire selection state so the Metadata pane and bottom pane reflect the currently-selected item immediately.
- Add Tab/Shift-Tab focus cycling and ensure comment input focuses correctly for typing and submission.
- Add a small integration test under `tests/tui/` that opens the TUI in headless/test mode, selects an item, adds a comment, and asserts metadata #comments increments and that the new comment text appears in the comments view.

Related work
- Refactor TUI: modularize and clean TUI code ‚Äî WL-0MKX5ZBUR1MIA4QN (epic) ‚Äî large refactor that extracted layout, state and handlers; implementing this layout should reuse those modules where present.
- TUI: Reparent items without typing IDs / Move mode related ‚Äî WL-0MLQXVUI91SIY9KM / WL-0MLQXW5MX1YKW5H1 ‚Äî shows prior work on tree interactions and move-mode state (useful for selection behavior and rendering hints).
- Add tests for TUI Update dialog / comment textbox ‚Äî WL-0ML8KBZ9N19YFTDO / WL-0ML8KC1NW1LH5CT8 ‚Äî existing test patterns for multi-field dialogs and multi-line comment boxes can be reused for the comment-input test.
- Escape key and mouse-guard fixes ‚Äî WL-0MLOSX33C0KN340D / WL-0MLYZQ52Q0NH7VOD ‚Äî caution: event handling and click-through guards exist and must be considered when wiring mouse/overlay behaviour.

Potentially related docs
- docs/opencode-tui.md ‚Äî TUI design and OpenCode integration notes.
- TUI.md ‚Äî user-facing help and keyboard shortcuts.
- src/commands/tui.ts ‚Äî TUI command entrypoint and existing layout code.
- src/tui/layout.ts, src/tui/state.ts, src/tui/handlers.ts ‚Äî modularized TUI helpers (may already exist in repo).

Derived keywords
- TUI, split-layout, metadata-pane, details-pane, comments, work-items

Verification & manual checks
- Manual verify layout proportions at 80x24 and 120x40 terminals.
- Manual verify Tab/Shift-Tab focus cycling and comment input submission updates metadata.
- Run the new integration test: `npm test tests/tui/tui-50-50-layout.test.ts` (or `vitest` equivalent).

Draft prepared by: OpenCode (agent) ‚Äî please review and provide edits or approve.
data.title: TUI: 50/50 split layout with metadata & details panes

Repro:
1. Open opencode (o)
2. Type a prompt and wait for respons
3. Close opencode (esc)
4. Open opencode again (o)
4. Type.
Expected behaviour is that each keypress enters a single character into the prompt box
Actual behaviour: Each keypress registers 3 characters in the prompt box.
data.title: Reopening opencode results in a single keypress being registered 3 times

data.description:
Summary: Create a reliable, automated regression test that reproduces the issue where each keypress inserts three characters into the opencode prompt after reopening the overlay.

Steps to reproduce (for the test):
1. Open opencode overlay (press 'o')
2. Type a short prompt and wait for a response
3. Close overlay (Esc)
4. Re-open overlay (press 'o')
5. Type a single character and assert that the prompt value length increases by 1 (not 3)

Acceptance criteria:
- New automated test fails on current behaviour and passes after the fix.
- Test added to the opencode overlay test suite with clear setup/teardown.
data.title: Reproduce and add automated regression test for triple keypress

- Proposed fix approach documented and linked to the fix work-item.

data.description:
When working with opencode (press o) in the TUI it always works with the worklog in the directory where wl is running from. We need it to work with the worklog for the project the wl tui command was run from.
Repro:
1) start wl tui in a project other than the Worklog project
2) open opencode (press o)
3) have the agent run wl next
Expected behaviour: a work item from the local project tree is selected
Actual behaviour: a work item from the worklog work items is selected.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Starting the TUI in a different project and opening OpenCode uses that project's worklog data for wl commands.
- The OpenCode server process runs with the TUI project's root as its working directory (parent of the .worklog directory).
- The repro steps return a work item from the local project tree, not the Worklog repo.

Update 2026-02-16: The ID is displayed again, but in the details pane the ID value uses a color that is too dark for some terminal color schemes and is hard to read. We need to make the ID value a much lighter color in the details pane metadata section.

Acceptance criteria:

Summary
- False positives in "wl next" due to scanning comments for blocker phrases.
- Remove heuristic blocker detection based on comments.

Expected behavior
- "wl next" considers blocking only via formal relationships: child work items blocking a parent and dependencies added via "wl dep add" (visible in "wl dep list <id>").
- Comment text is ignored for blocker detection.

Acceptance criteria
- Any logic that infers blockers from comment text is removed.
- Blocking determination uses only work item children and formal dependencies.
- "wl next" no longer flags items as blocked based solely on comments.

Acceptance criteria:

data.description:
## Summary

Add move mode state tracking to TuiState and a utility to compute all descendants of a given item, preventing circular reparenting.

## User Story

As a TUI developer implementing move mode, I need a state model and descendant detection utility so that the move mode UI can track which item is being moved and which items are invalid targets (descendants of the source).

## Acceptance Criteria

- `TuiState` (or a companion type) includes a `moveMode` field with `sourceId`, `active` flag, and `descendantIds` set
- `getDescendants(state, itemId)` returns the complete set of descendant IDs for any item
- Descendant detection correctly identifies all descendants at any nesting depth; tested with a hierarchy of at least 5 levels
- Unit tests verify descendant computation for flat, nested, and edge cases (no children, item not found)

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `MoveMode` type to `src/tui/types.ts`
- Add `getDescendants()` function to `src/tui/state.ts`
- Add `enterMoveMode()` / `exitMoveMode()` state helpers
- Unit tests in `tests/tui/` for state transitions and descendant detection

## Dependencies

None (foundational feature)

## Deliverables

- Types (`MoveMode` in `src/tui/types.ts`)
- State helpers (`src/tui/state.ts`)
- Unit tests

## Related Files

- `src/tui/types.ts` ‚Äî Type definitions
- `src/tui/state.ts` ‚Äî Tree state module with `buildVisibleNodes`, `childrenMap`, parent/child mapping
data.title: Move mode state and descendant detection

## Summary

Wire the `m` key to enter/exit move mode in the controller, integrating with the existing key constant system and chord handler.

## User Story

As a TUI user, I want to press `m` on a selected item to enter move mode so I can begin reparenting that item visually without typing IDs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Pressing `m` on a selected item in the tree view enters move mode and stores the source item
- Pressing `Esc` during move mode cancels and restores normal navigation
- `m` key is registered in `src/tui/constants.ts` alongside existing key constants
- Move mode is suppressed when no item is selected
- When overlays are visible (help, opencode, search), pressing `m` does not enter move mode
- Help menu updated to show `m` shortcut under Actions category

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `KEY_MOVE` constant to `src/tui/constants.ts`
- Add `m` entry to `DEFAULT_SHORTCUTS` in the Actions category
- Wire `m` key handler in `src/tui/controller.ts` to call `enterMoveMode()`
- Guard other key handlers to be aware of move mode state (prevent conflicting actions)
- Integration tests verifying activation/cancellation flow with mocked blessed

## Dependencies

- Move mode state and descendant detection (WL-0MLQXVUI91SIY9KM)

## Deliverables

- Key constant (`src/tui/constants.ts`)
- Controller wiring (`src/tui/controller.ts`)
- Help menu update (`DEFAULT_SHORTCUTS`)
- Integration tests

## Related Files

- `src/tui/constants.ts` ‚Äî Centralized keyboard shortcut constants
- `src/tui/controller.ts` ‚Äî TuiController class; keybinding wiring
- `src/tui/chords.ts` ‚Äî Keyboard chord handler (potential integration point)
data.title: Move mode keybinding and activation

data.description:
## Summary

Render visual indicators during move mode: highlight the source item (color + marker), dim/grey-out descendants, and display contextual footer instructions.

## User Story

As a TUI user in move mode, I want clear visual feedback showing which item I am moving, which targets are invalid (descendants), and instructions for how to complete or cancel the operation.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Source item is rendered with a distinct background/foreground color AND a prefix marker (e.g. `[M]` or `‚ñ∂`)
- Descendant items of the source are visually dimmed/greyed; pressing `m`/Enter on a greyed-out descendant produces no action (no-op)
- Footer displays: `Moving: <title> (<ID>) ‚Äî select target, press m/Enter; Esc to cancel`
- When move mode exits (cancel or success), all visual indicators are removed and the tree renders normally
- Valid target items retain their normal styling

## Minimal Implementation

- Modify tree line rendering logic in controller (the list line building path) to check move mode state
- Apply color styling for source item and dim styling for descendants during rendering
- Add prefix marker to source item line
- Update footer content during move mode
- Restore default rendering on exit
- Integration tests verifying visual output lines contain expected markers/styles

## Dependencies

- Move mode state and descendant detection (WL-0MLQXVUI91SIY9KM)
- Move mode keybinding and activation (WL-0MLQXW5MX1YKW5H1)

## Deliverables

- Rendering changes in controller/list rendering
- Footer update logic
- Integration tests

## Related Files

- `src/tui/controller.ts` ‚Äî Tree rendering and footer management
- `src/tui/state.ts` ‚Äî Move mode state with descendant IDs
- `src/tui/types.ts` ‚Äî MoveMode type
data.title: Move mode visual feedback

data.description:
## Summary

Handle target selection during move mode: pressing `m`/Enter on a valid target executes `wl update <source-id> --parent <target-id>`, refreshes the tree, and follows the moved item.

## User Story

As a TUI user in move mode, I want to select a target parent by navigating to it and pressing `m` or Enter, so the selected item is reparented under the target without me typing any IDs.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Pressing `m` or Enter on a valid (non-descendant, non-source) target executes reparent via `wl update`
- Tree refreshes after successful reparent
- New parent is auto-expanded and cursor follows the moved item (scroll to moved item, select it)
- Success toast is displayed: `Moved <title> under <target-title>`
- Error toast is displayed if `wl update` fails, and the tree remains unchanged
- Move mode exits after execution regardless of success or failure, returning to normal navigation state

## Minimal Implementation

- Add target confirmation handler in controller's move mode key handler
- Execute `wl update <source-id> --parent <target-id>` via the existing CLI/API integration
- Call tree refresh, expand the target parent, and scroll to moved item
- Show toast via existing toast/notification mechanism
- Integration tests for success and error paths with mocked wl update

## Dependencies

- Move mode keybinding and activation (WL-0MLQXW5MX1YKW5H1)
- Move mode visual feedback (WL-0MLQXWHZD107999R)

## Deliverables

- Reparent execution logic
- Tree refresh + auto-expand + cursor follow
- Toast messages (success and error)
- Integration tests

## Related Files

- `src/tui/controller.ts` ‚Äî Controller with existing tree refresh, toast, and CLI execution patterns
- `src/tui/state.ts` ‚Äî Tree state rebuild after reparent
- `src/commands/update.ts` ‚Äî CLI update command that handles `--parent`
data.title: Target selection and reparent execution

data.description:
## Summary

When the source item is selected as its own target in move mode, detach it from its parent and promote it to root level.

## User Story

As a TUI user, I want to detach an item from its parent by selecting the item itself as the target during move mode, so I can promote items to top-level when they no longer belong under a specific parent.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Selecting the source item itself as the target during move mode clears its parent (moves to root)
- The operation calls `wl update <source-id> --parent ""` (or equivalent to clear parentId)
- Success toast: `Moved <title> to root level`
- Tree refreshes and cursor follows the item at its new root position
- If the item is already at root level and self-selected, show toast: `<title> is already at root level` and exit move mode
- Attempting to unparent an item that has children does not orphan the children ‚Äî they remain attached to the moved item

## Minimal Implementation

- Add self-select detection in the target confirmation handler
- Execute parent-clearing update via `wl update`
- Handle already-at-root edge case
- Unit test for self-select detection
- Integration test for full unparent flow including children preservation

## Dependencies

- Target selection and reparent execution (WL-0MLQXWUCY08EREBY)

## Deliverables

- Self-select handler logic
- Edge case handling (already at root, children preservation)
- Unit and integration tests

## Related Files

- `src/tui/controller.ts` ‚Äî Controller with move mode handler
- `src/commands/update.ts` ‚Äî CLI update; passing empty parent clears parentId
data.title: Unparent to root via self-select

data.description:
# TUI: prevent mouse click-through from dialogs to underlying widgets

Mouse clicks inside TUI dialogs propagate to underlying widgets (e.g., the work item list), silently changing the selected item and causing actions like comment submission to target the wrong work item.

## Problem statement

The screen-level mouse handler in `controller.ts:3319` processes click events on the work item list without checking whether a dialog is currently open. When a user clicks inside the update dialog (e.g., clicking into the comment textarea), the click coordinates overlap with the list widget behind the dialog, causing `list.select()` to change the selected item. When the dialog is subsequently submitted, the comment and field changes are applied to the newly selected item rather than the one the user intended. This affects all dialogs, not just the update dialog.

## Users

**TUI users who interact with dialogs using the mouse.**

- As a user editing a work item via the TUI update dialog, I want my mouse clicks inside the dialog to stay within the dialog so that field changes and comments are applied to the correct work item.
- As a user interacting with any TUI dialog, I want clicks inside the dialog to not affect the widgets behind it so that I can trust the UI state.
- As a user who accidentally clicks outside a dialog (on the overlay), I want the dialog to close ‚Äî but if I have unsaved changes, I want a confirmation prompt before my changes are discarded.

## Success criteria

1. Mouse clicks inside any open dialog do not propagate to widgets behind the dialog (list, detail pane, etc.).
2. Clicking the update dialog's overlay (dimmed area outside the dialog box) dismisses the dialog, consistent with close/detail overlay behavior.
3. If the update dialog has unsaved changes (modified fields or non-empty comment), clicking the overlay shows a simple yes/no confirmation dialog before discarding.
4. The screen-level mouse handler (`controller.ts:3319`) guards against processing list/detail clicks when any dialog is open.
5. Existing keyboard-driven dialog interactions (Tab, Enter, Escape, Ctrl-S) continue to work unchanged.

## Constraints

- **Blessed library limitations**: Blessed dispatches mouse events to the topmost clickable widget, but the screen-level `on('mouse')` handler bypasses this by processing all mouse events globally. The fix must work within blessed's event model.
- **Consistency**: The fix should apply uniformly to all dialogs (update, close, next-item, detail) to prevent similar click-through bugs elsewhere.
- **Backward compatibility**: Keyboard-only users must not be affected. All existing keyboard shortcuts and navigation must continue to work.

## Existing state

- **Overlays**: Three overlay widgets (`closeOverlay`, `updateOverlay`, `detailOverlay`) in `src/tui/components/overlays.ts`, plus `nextOverlay` in `src/tui/layout.ts`. All have `mouse: true` and `clickable: true`. All except `updateOverlay` have click handlers that dismiss their dialogs.
- **Screen mouse handler**: `controller.ts:3319-3347` handles mouse events globally. It processes `isInside(list, ...)` and `isInside(detail, ...)` but has no guard for open dialogs. Keyboard handlers already use a guard pattern at lines 540, 548, 560, etc.
- **Comment persistence**: The submission path (`getValue()` -> `buildUpdateDialogUpdates()` -> `db.createComment()`) works correctly. The bug is that click-through changes the selected item before submission.
- **Tests**: `tests/tui/tui-update-dialog.test.ts` covers comment logic but not mouse interaction or click-through.

## Desired change

1. **Guard the screen mouse handler**: Add an early return in the `screen.on('mouse')` handler when any dialog is visible (`!updateDialog.hidden`, `!closeDialog.hidden`, etc.) to prevent list/detail click processing.
2. **Add click handler to updateOverlay**: Register a click handler on `updateOverlay` that dismisses the update dialog, matching the pattern used by `closeOverlay` and `detailOverlay`.
3. **Add discard-changes confirmation**: Before dismissing the update dialog via overlay click, check if any fields have been modified or the comment textarea is non-empty. If so, show a simple yes/no blessed confirmation dialog ("Discard unsaved changes?"). On "Yes", close the dialog. On "No", return focus to the dialog.
4. **Add tests**: Add test cases covering:
   - Mouse events inside a dialog do not change list selection.
   - Overlay click dismisses dialogs.
   - Discard confirmation appears when unsaved changes exist.

## Related work

- Fix update dialog comment box overlap (WL-0ML8R7UQK0Q461HG) ‚Äî completed
- Restore update dialog submit after comment focus (WL-0ML8V0G1A0OAAN05) ‚Äî completed
- Fix tab navigation out of update dialog comment box (WL-0ML8RKFBG16P96YY) ‚Äî completed
- Escape in update dialog should close dialog, not app (WL-0ML8UQW8V1OQ3838) ‚Äî completed
- Comment Textbox M4 (WL-0ML8KBZ9N19YFTDO) ‚Äî completed
- TUI closes when clicking anywhere (WL-0MKX2C2X007IRR8G) ‚Äî completed (related blessed mouse event fix)

## Related work (automated report)

### Work items

- **Critical: TUI closes when clicking anywhere** (WL-0MKX2C2X007IRR8G) ‚Äî completed. The closest precedent: mouse clicks caused the entire TUI to exit. The fix established the current screen-level mouse handler and overlay pattern. This work item extends that same handler with dialog-open guards.
- **Mouse click on Work Items tree does not update details** (WL-0MLAJ3Z750G25EJE) ‚Äî completed. Fixed the `screen.on('mouse')` handler to call `updateListSelection()` on mousedown inside the list. This is the exact code path that now needs a dialog-open guard, since it fires even when a dialog is covering the list.
- **Deduplicate list selection/click handlers** (WL-0MKX63D5U10ETR4S) ‚Äî completed. Consolidated overlapping list selection handlers into the single screen-level mouse handler. Relevant because it explains why list selection is handled at screen level rather than per-widget, which is the root cause of the click-through.
- **Clicking a work item in TUI tree view does not update/select it in detail pane** (WL-0MKVTAH8S1CQIHP6) ‚Äî completed. Earlier fix for the same click-to-select code path. Confirms the `isInside(list, ...)` + `updateListSelection()` pattern is the intended mechanism for list clicks.
- **Fix update dialog comment box overlap** (WL-0ML8R7UQK0Q461HG) ‚Äî completed. Fixed layout overlap between the comment textarea and option lists in the update dialog. Related as prior update-dialog UI fix.
- **Escape in update dialog should close dialog, not app** (WL-0ML8UQW8V1OQ3838) ‚Äî completed. Fixed key event leaking from the update dialog to the screen. Analogous pattern to this bug: events intended for the dialog reaching the wrong target.

### Repository files

- `src/tui/controller.ts:3319-3347` ‚Äî The screen-level `screen.on('mouse')` handler. The primary code that needs modification: add a dialog-open guard before the `isInside(list, ...)` and `isInside(detail, ...)` checks.
- `src/tui/controller.ts:540` ‚Äî Example of the existing dialog-open guard pattern used in keyboard handlers: `if (!detailModal.hidden || !nextDialog.hidden || !closeDialog.hidden || !updateDialog.hidden) return;`
- `src/tui/components/overlays.ts` ‚Äî Overlay widget definitions. `updateOverlay` needs a click handler added.
- `src/tui/controller.ts:1861` ‚Äî The `isInside()` helper used for coordinate hit-testing.
- `tests/tui/tui-update-dialog.test.ts` ‚Äî Existing update dialog tests. Mouse interaction tests should be added here.

## Risks and assumptions

- **Risk: Blessed event model edge cases.** The fix assumes that checking `dialog.hidden` is a reliable indicator of dialog visibility. If blessed defers hide/show state changes, the guard could miss edge cases. **Mitigation**: Verify `hidden` reflects immediate state in blessed's implementation; add integration tests.
- **Risk: Confirmation dialog complexity.** Adding a yes/no confirmation dialog introduces new UI surface area and potential focus management issues. **Mitigation**: Keep the confirmation dialog minimal (reuse existing blessed dialog patterns); test focus restoration after dismiss.
- **Risk: Scope creep.** The fix touches the global mouse handler and all dialogs. Changes could expand beyond the core click-through fix. **Mitigation**: Record additional improvements (e.g., better overlay styling, mouse hover effects) as separate work items linked to this one rather than expanding scope.
- **Risk: Over-aggressive mouse guard.** If the guard blocks all mouse events when a dialog is open, it could prevent legitimate interactions within the dialog (e.g., scrolling, clicking between fields). **Mitigation**: The guard should only suppress the list/detail click-handling code paths, not all mouse processing. Dialog-internal mouse events are handled by blessed's per-widget dispatch and should be unaffected.
- **Assumption**: The `isInside()` helper correctly identifies coordinate overlap. If dialog and list coordinates differ from what's expected, the guard logic may need adjustment.
- **Assumption**: The existing `closeOverlay` and `detailOverlay` click-to-dismiss patterns are the desired UX model for all overlays.
- **Assumption**: The existing dialog-open guard pattern (`if (!detailModal.hidden || !nextDialog.hidden || !closeDialog.hidden || !updateDialog.hidden) return;`) used in keyboard handlers (e.g., `controller.ts:540`) is the correct pattern to replicate for the mouse handler.

## Suggested next step

Plan and implement this work item directly from the acceptance criteria above. The scope is well-defined: guard the screen mouse handler, add the overlay click handler, add the discard-changes confirmation, and add tests. No PRD is required.
data.title: TUI: prevent mouse click-through from dialogs to underlying widgets

data.description:
## Summary

When a work item is unparented (e.g. using the 'm' key in the TUI to set parentId to null) and then `wl sync` is run, the parent link is restored to its previous value even though the removal of the parent is the more recent operation. This means local edits that clear a parent are silently reverted by sync.

## User Story

As a user, when I remove the parent of a work item and then sync, I expect the unparented state to be preserved because my local change is more recent than the previous parent assignment.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Pick a work item that currently has a parent (e.g. `wl show <id>` shows a non-null parentId).
2. Remove the parent: use the 'm' key in TUI or `wl update <id> --parent null` to set parentId to null.
3. Verify the parent is removed: `wl show <id>` should show parentId as null.
4. Run `wl sync`.
5. Check the item again: `wl show <id>`.

## Actual Behaviour

After sync, parentId is restored to the original parent value. The unparent operation is lost.

## Expected Behaviour

After sync, parentId should remain null because the local unparent operation is more recent than the previous parent assignment. Sync should respect the timestamp/ordering of operations and not revert newer local changes.

## Impact

- Data integrity: user intent (removing a parent) is silently overwritten.
- Trust: users cannot rely on sync to preserve their local edits.
- Workflow disruption: items re-appear under parents they were intentionally removed from, breaking planning and hierarchy management.

## Suggested Investigation

- Review the sync merge logic (likely in the JSONL merge/import path) to understand how parentId changes are reconciled.
- Check whether setting parentId to null generates a JSONL event with a timestamp, and whether the merge logic correctly compares timestamps for null vs non-null parentId values.
- A null parentId may be treated as "missing" rather than "explicitly set to null", causing the sync to treat the remote non-null value as authoritative.
- Check `src/persistent-store.ts` and the sync/merge helpers for how parentId is handled during import/refresh.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. Setting parentId to null and running `wl sync` preserves the null parentId when the unparent operation is more recent than the last parent assignment.
2. The JSONL event log correctly records unparent operations with timestamps.
3. The sync merge logic treats an explicit null parentId as a valid value, not as a missing field.
4. A regression test verifies that unparent + sync does not restore the old parent.
5. Existing sync behaviour for re-parenting (changing from one parent to another) is not affected.
data.title: Sync restores removed parent link, overwriting more recent unparent operation
Preserve explicit null parentId during sync merge

## Add /create OpenCode command for creating work items from TUI

### Summary
Create a `/create` OpenCode command that allows users to create new work items directly from the TUI OpenCode prompt. The user presses `o` to open the OpenCode pane, types `/create <description>`, and the command sends a prescribed prompt to OpenCode to create and classify the work item.

### User Story
As a TUI user, I want to type `/create My item description` in the OpenCode prompt so that a new work item is created with appropriate priority, issue-type, and dependencies without leaving the TUI.

### Behaviour
1. User presses `o` to open the OpenCode pane (existing behaviour)
2. User types `/create <description of the new work item>`
3. OpenCode receives the following prompt:

   Create a new work-item for the following description. Assign an appropriate priority and issue-type based on your understanding of the project. Record any dependencies that you can identify. Do not ask clarifying questions, if there is something you truly do not understand use the description verbatum and insert an Open Questions section into the description.

   <user_description>.

4. OpenCode processes the prompt and creates the work item using `wl create`
5. The result is displayed in the OpenCode response pane

### Implementation approach
- Create a new OpenCode command file at `.opencode/command/create.md` following the existing command format (YAML front matter + markdown body)
- The command template should extract `$ARGUMENTS` as the user description and construct the prescribed prompt
- Add `/create` to `AVAILABLE_COMMANDS` in `src/tui/constants.ts` for autocomplete support
- Update `TUI.md` documentation

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A `.opencode/command/create.md` file exists with the prescribed prompt template
- [ ] Typing `/create <description>` in the OpenCode prompt creates a work item via the prescribed prompt
- [ ] `/create` appears in the TUI autocomplete suggestions
- [ ] `TUI.md` documentation is updated to describe the `/create` command
- [ ] All existing tests continue to pass

### References
- Existing command examples: `~/.config/opencode/command/intake.md`, `plan.md`, etc.
- Command format: YAML front matter (description, tags, agent) + markdown prompt body
- Slash command autocomplete: `src/tui/constants.ts` AVAILABLE_COMMANDS
- Previous approach (reverted): dedicated W shortcut with modal dialog
- Related: Slash Command Palette (WL-0ML5YRMB11GQV4HR), Implement / command palette (WL-0MLBTG16W0QCTNM8)
data.title: Add /create OpenCode command for creating work items from TUI

data.description:
Summary:
Write a feature request for the `wl` team to add global plugin directory scanning at `${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-$HOME/.config}/opencode/.worklog/plugins/`.

Requester: SorraAgents (SA-0MLRSH3EU14UT79F)

Parent epic: SA-0MLRONXRF1N732R1 (this item is a blocking dependency for that epic)

User story:
As an operator running multiple projects on the same host, I want `wl` to discover plugins installed in a global directory (`~/.config/opencode/.worklog/plugins/`) in addition to the project-local `.worklog/plugins/` directory, so that I can install a plugin once and have it available across all projects.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Feature request work item exists as a child of SA-0MLRONXRF1N732R1.
2. Specifies the desired resolution order: project-local plugins load first, then global (project overrides global).
3. Specifies fallback behaviour when both directories contain a plugin with the same filename (project-local wins).
4. Specifies that `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` should be respected for the global path.
5. Identifies that this blocks the parent epic from full completion (no interim workaround is being used).
6. Includes a user story from the operator perspective.

Desired behaviour / specification:
- `wl` scans for plugins in both locations, with this resolution order:
  1. `<project>/.worklog/plugins/` (project-local, highest priority)
  2. `${XDG_CONFIG_HOME:-$HOME/.config}/opencode/.worklog/plugins/` (global, lower priority)
- If the same plugin filename exists in both directories, the project-local version takes precedence.
- `wl` should respect `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` when resolving the global path; if `XDG_CONFIG_HOME` is unset, fallback to `$HOME/.config`.
- Optionally expose a configuration key (e.g. `pluginDirs`) to allow custom paths, but the two defaults above must work with zero configuration.

Motivation:
- SA-0MLRONXRF1N732R1 moves AMPA plugin installation to the global directory. Without `wl` scanning the global directory, globally installed plugins remain invisible to `wl`.
- Enables a single-update-propagates-everywhere workflow and reduces duplication across projects.

Dependencies:
- None. This is an external request to the `wl` team.

Deliverables:
- This work item serves as the feature request for the `wl` team.
- Ensure the parent epic SA-0MLRONXRF1N732R1 lists this item as a blocking dependency.
data.title: Global plugin discovery for wl (global ~/.config/opencode/.worklog/plugins)

## Summary

There are TypeScript LSP/compiler errors that need to be resolved across the codebase.

### Identified Errors

1. **tests/cli/cli-inproc.ts:205** - Reference to undefined variable `__inproc_orig_exitcode`. This variable is never declared or assigned anywhere in the codebase. The code should use `process.exitCode` directly since that is the intended mechanism for capturing exit codes in the in-process test runner.

2. **src/tui/layout.ts:92-93** - Property `tput` does not exist on type `BlessedProgram`. The blessed library's `screen.program` object has a `tput` property at runtime but the type declarations (which resolve to `any` via the ambient declaration in src/types/blessed.d.ts) don't surface it properly when `BlessedScreen` is resolved through `Widgets.Screen`.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All TypeScript compiler errors in `tests/cli/cli-inproc.ts` are resolved
- [ ] All TypeScript compiler errors in `src/tui/layout.ts` are resolved
- [ ] `npx tsc --noEmit` passes with zero errors for the src directory
- [ ] Existing tests continue to pass (`npm test`)
- [ ] No behavioural changes - fixes are type-level only
data.title: Fix LSP errors

data.description:
## Summary

On a fresh install of Worklog in a new project (no node_modules, no existing agents), the stats plugin (`stats-plugin.mjs`) is installed by `wl init` into `.worklog/plugins/`. The plugin imports `chalk` (line 15 of `examples/stats-plugin.mjs`), which is a runtime dependency of the Worklog package itself but is NOT available in the target project's `node_modules`. This causes every subsequent `wl` command to emit an error to stderr.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a new empty directory with `git init`
2. Run `wl init` (either interactive mode on first init, or any subsequent `wl init --json` re-init)
3. Run any `wl` command (e.g. `wl list --json`, `wl tui`)

## Observed Behaviour

- Every `wl` command prints to stderr: `Failed to load plugin stats-plugin.mjs: Cannot find package 'chalk' imported from <project>/.worklog/plugins/stats-plugin.mjs`
- The `wl stats` command fails entirely as the plugin never registers
- Other commands continue to work but with the noisy error output on stderr
- The TUI still loads (the error is non-fatal for commands other than `stats`)

## Expected Behaviour

- `wl init` should either:
  - Not install plugins with unresolvable dependencies, OR
  - Ensure plugin dependencies are available (e.g. bundle chalk or remove the dependency), OR
  - Gracefully skip loading plugins with missing dependencies without emitting errors
- All `wl` commands should run cleanly without stderr errors in a fresh project

## Root Cause

The stats plugin (`examples/stats-plugin.mjs`) uses `import chalk from 'chalk'` (ESM import). When Worklog is installed globally via npm, `chalk` exists in Worklog's own `node_modules`. However, when the plugin file is copied to a target project's `.worklog/plugins/` directory, Node.js ESM module resolution tries to find `chalk` relative to the plugin file's location - NOT relative to the `wl` binary's location. Since the target project has no `node_modules` (or no `chalk` in its `node_modules`), the import fails.

## Affected Files

- `src/commands/init.ts` - `ensureStatsPluginInstalled()` (line 654) copies the plugin without checking dependency availability
- `src/plugin-loader.ts` - `loadPlugin()` (line 129) uses dynamic `import()` which fails for plugins with unresolvable dependencies
- `examples/stats-plugin.mjs` - Line 15: `import chalk from 'chalk'` - the dependency that cannot be resolved

## Additional Sub-Issues Discovered

1. **Inconsistent first-init JSON path**: The first-time `wl init --json` code path (init.ts line 1177+) does NOT install the stats plugin, while the interactive path and re-init JSON path DO. This means the statsPlugin field is missing from the first-init JSON output.
2. **No dependency validation**: The plugin loader has no mechanism to validate whether a plugin's dependencies are available before attempting to load it.
3. **Error output format**: The `Failed to load plugin` message goes to stderr via `logger.error()`, which is correct, but it is still disruptive for automated/agent workflows that capture stderr.

## Suggested Implementation Approach

Several possible fixes (not mutually exclusive):

**Option A - Remove chalk dependency from stats plugin**: Rewrite the stats plugin to not use chalk, or use ANSI escape codes directly. This is the simplest fix but reduces the plugin's visual quality.

**Option B - Bundle/inline chalk in the plugin**: Include chalk's functionality inline in the plugin file so it has no external dependencies. This makes the plugin self-contained.

**Option C - Graceful fallback in the plugin**: Wrap the chalk import in a try/catch and fall back to a no-op colorizer when chalk is unavailable. This is resilient but still leaves a plugin with degraded output.

**Option D - Plugin loader validates dependencies**: Before loading a plugin, the loader could pre-check for resolvable imports. This is complex and might not be practical for ESM dynamic imports.

**Option E - Don't install stats plugin by default**: Make stats plugin installation opt-in rather than automatic during `wl init`. This avoids the problem entirely but reduces discoverability.

**Option F - Install chalk alongside the plugin**: Have `wl init` create a local `package.json` in the plugins directory or install chalk into the project. This adds complexity and may be undesirable.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Running `wl init` in a fresh project (no node_modules) completes without errors
- [ ] All `wl` commands (`list`, `tui`, `stats`, etc.) run without emitting `Failed to load plugin` errors to stderr in a fresh project
- [ ] The `wl stats` command either works correctly or is not available (not broken/silently missing)
- [ ] First-time `wl init --json` output includes consistent statsPlugin information
- [ ] Existing projects with chalk available continue to work as before (no regression)
- [ ] Plugin loader handles missing dependencies gracefully without stderr noise
data.title: Fresh install fails because stats plugin is present locally

If xclip is not installed this currently issues a toast that is an error, but it is indistinguishable to a success toast.

## Summary

Remove the hard `import chalk from 'chalk'` dependency from the stats plugin and replace it with built-in ANSI escape code colorization, eliminating the root cause of the fresh-install failure.

## User Story

As a user who runs `wl init` in a fresh project (no node_modules), I want the stats plugin to load and display colored output without requiring chalk, so that `wl stats` works out of the box.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Stats plugin uses ANSI escape codes for colorization with no external runtime imports
- [ ] `wl stats` produces colored output identical (or near-identical) to current chalk-based output
- [ ] `wl stats --json` continues to work as before
- [ ] Plugin loads and works in projects with no `node_modules`
- [ ] Plugin does NOT emit any stderr output when loaded in a project without chalk
- [ ] Plugin loads and works in projects where `chalk` is available (no regression)

## Minimal Implementation

- Create a local `ansi` helper object inside the plugin providing color functions: green, cyan, red, gray, blue, yellow, magenta, white, greenBright, redBright, yellowBright, blueBright, magentaBright, whiteBright, cyanBright
- Replace `import chalk from 'chalk'` with the local helper
- Update all `chalk.xxx()` calls to use the local helper
- Test in a fresh project (no node_modules) and in an existing project

## Affected Files

- `examples/stats-plugin.mjs`

## Dependencies

None
data.title: Self-contained stats plugin (ANSI colors)

## Summary

Modify the plugin loader to downgrade load failures to a single-line warning instead of `logger.error()`, reducing noise for automated and agent workflows.

## User Story

As a developer using wl in automated pipelines, I want plugin load failures to produce a single-line warning (not a noisy error), so that my stderr output is clean and my automation is not disrupted.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] When a plugin fails to load (any reason), a single-line warning is emitted to stderr matching the pattern: `Warning: plugin <name> skipped: <reason>`
- [ ] Without `--verbose`, no stack trace or multi-line error is emitted to stderr
- [ ] With `--verbose`, the full error stack/details are shown via `logger.debug()`
- [ ] Other commands continue to function normally when a plugin fails to load
- [ ] The `wl plugins` command shows failed plugins with their error details
- [ ] The returned `PluginInfo` object still captures the error for programmatic use

## Minimal Implementation

- Add a `warn()` method to the `Logger` class in `src/logger.ts` that always writes to stderr (like `error()` but semantically distinct)
- In `loadPlugin()` (`src/plugin-loader.ts:163-166`), replace `logger.error()` with `logger.warn()` using the format: `Warning: plugin <name> skipped: <reason>`
- Add `logger.debug()` call with the full error message/stack for `--verbose` mode
- Ensure the returned `PluginInfo` still captures the error string
- Update tests in `tests/plugin-loader.test.ts`

## Affected Files

- `src/logger.ts`
- `src/plugin-loader.ts`
- `tests/plugin-loader.test.ts`

## Dependencies

None
data.title: Graceful plugin load failure handling

## Summary

Fix the first-time `wl init --json` code path to install the stats plugin consistently, matching the behavior of interactive and re-init paths.

## User Story

As an agent running `wl init --json` for the first time in a project, I want the stats plugin to be installed (or explicitly skipped) consistently across all init paths, so that the JSON output always includes a `statsPlugin` field and the plugin is available.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] First-time `wl init --json` installs the stats plugin (same as interactive init and re-init paths)
- [ ] `wl init --json` output MUST include a `statsPlugin` field in all code paths (first-init, re-init, interactive)
- [ ] First-init JSON output MUST NOT omit the `statsPlugin` field
- [ ] `wl init --stats-plugin-overwrite no` consistently skips plugin install across all paths
- [ ] No regressions in interactive init or re-init flows

## Minimal Implementation

- Add `ensureStatsPluginInstalled()` call to the first-init JSON code path (around `init.ts:1177+`)
- Include `statsPlugin` result in the JSON response object
- Add/update tests for first-init JSON path to verify `statsPlugin` field presence

## Affected Files

- `src/commands/init.ts` (first-init JSON path around line 1177+)
- `tests/cli/init.test.ts`

## Dependencies

- WL-0MLU6FTG61XXT0RY (Self-contained stats plugin) - stats plugin should be self-contained before we ensure it installs everywhere
data.title: Consistent stats plugin init paths

data.description:
## Summary

Add a "Handling Dependencies" section to `PLUGIN_GUIDE.md` documenting that plugins should be self-contained or degrade gracefully when dependencies are unavailable.

## User Story

As a plugin author, I want clear documentation on how to handle external dependencies in my plugin, so that my plugin works reliably across different project environments.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] PLUGIN_GUIDE.md includes a new "Handling Dependencies" section after "Plugin Best Practices"
- [ ] Section covers: self-contained plugins, ANSI fallback pattern, bundling with esbuild/rollup, and graceful import failure handling
- [ ] A code example showing the ANSI escape code pattern is included
- [ ] Existing troubleshooting "Module Resolution Errors" section references the new dependency guidance
- [ ] The FAQ entry about npm packages references the new section
- [ ] The stats plugin description notes it is self-contained (no external dependencies)

## Minimal Implementation

- Add "Handling Dependencies" section with subsections: Self-Contained Plugins, ANSI Color Fallback, Bundling Dependencies, Graceful Import Failure
- Include a concise code example showing the ANSI helper pattern from the updated stats plugin
- Add cross-reference from "Module Resolution Errors" troubleshooting section
- Update FAQ "Can I use npm packages in my plugin?" to reference the new section
- Update stats plugin description at bottom of guide

## Affected Files

- `PLUGIN_GUIDE.md`

## Dependencies

- WL-0MLU6FTG61XXT0RY (Self-contained stats plugin) - document the ANSI pattern after it exists in the stats plugin
data.title: Plugin Guide dependency best practices

## Summary

Add automated end-to-end tests that verify a fresh project (no node_modules) can run `wl` commands without plugin-related errors.

## User Story

As a maintainer, I want regression tests that catch the fresh-install plugin loading bug, so that it never recurs after the fix is deployed.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] At least one test creates a temp directory, runs `wl init`, and verifies stderr does NOT contain `Failed to load plugin` or `Cannot find package`
- [ ] At least one test verifies `wl stats` works in a fresh project (produces valid output or valid JSON with --json)
- [ ] Tests verify that `--verbose` shows additional plugin diagnostic info without errors
- [ ] Tests run in CI without modification to existing CI configuration
- [ ] Tests cover both first-init and re-init paths

## Minimal Implementation

- Add integration test file (or extend existing `tests/cli/init.test.ts`) with fresh-install scenarios
- Test 1: `git init` + `wl init --json` in temp dir, assert clean stderr
- Test 2: Run `wl stats --json` after init, assert valid JSON output with `success: true`
- Test 3: Run `wl list --json --verbose` after init, assert no `Failed to load plugin` in stderr
- Test 4: Run `wl init --json` twice (first-init + re-init), assert `statsPlugin` field in both responses

## Affected Files

- `tests/cli/init.test.ts` (or new `tests/cli/fresh-install.test.ts`)

## Dependencies

- WL-0MLU6FTG61XXT0RY (Self-contained stats plugin)
- WL-0MLU6G7Z71QOTVOB (Graceful plugin load failure handling)
- WL-0MLU6GKM40J12M4S (Consistent stats plugin init paths)
