## Summary

Widen the `db.search()` options type and implement SQL WHERE clauses (via JOIN with `workitems` table) in `searchFts()` and application-level filtering in `searchFallback()` for the six new filters.

## User Experience Change

Search results will be correctly filtered by priority, assignee, stage, issue type, deleted status, and needsProducerReview. Deleted items will be excluded by default (matching `wl list` behaviour) and included when `--deleted` is specified.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `db.search()` accepts `priority`, `assignee`, `stage`, `deleted`, `needsProducerReview`, and `issueType` in its options parameter
- `searchFts()` JOINs `worklog_fts` with `workitems` on `itemId = id` and applies WHERE clauses for each provided filter on the `workitems` table columns
- `searchFts()` excludes deleted items by default (`WHERE workitems.status != 'deleted'`); when `deleted: true` is passed, this exclusion is removed
- The existing `status` UNINDEXED column on the FTS table continues to be used for the `--status` filter (or migrated to the JOIN approach for consistency ‚Äî either is acceptable)
- `searchFallback()` applies equivalent application-level filtering for all six new fields
- Existing filter behaviour (status, parentId, tags, limit) is unchanged ‚Äî no regression
- When no new filters are provided, behaviour is identical to the current implementation
- No FTS schema migration is required

## Minimal Implementation

1. Extend the inline options type in `db.search()` (`src/database.ts` line 304) with: `priority?: string`, `assignee?: string`, `stage?: string`, `deleted?: boolean`, `needsProducerReview?: boolean`, `issueType?: string`
2. In `searchFts()` (`src/persistent-store.ts`):
   - Restructure the SQL query to JOIN `worklog_fts` with `workitems` on `worklog_fts.itemId = workitems.id`
   - Add conditional WHERE clauses for `workitems.priority`, `workitems.assignee`, `workitems.stage`, `workitems.issueType`, `workitems.needsProducerReview`
   - Add default `AND workitems.status != 'deleted'` clause, omitted when `deleted: true`
   - Existing `status` and `parentId` filters can continue using FTS columns or migrate to JOIN ‚Äî maintain backward compatibility
3. In `searchFallback()` (`src/persistent-store.ts`):
   - Add application-level `.filter()` calls for `priority`, `assignee`, `stage`, `issueType`, `needsProducerReview`
   - Add deleted item exclusion by default, removed when `deleted: true`
4. Pass through options from `db.search()` to both store methods

## Key Files

- `src/database.ts` ‚Äî `db.search()` method (line 302)
- `src/persistent-store.ts` ‚Äî `searchFts()` (line 830) and `searchFallback()` (line 942)
- `src/types.ts` ‚Äî `WorkItemQuery` interface for reference (line 108)

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Add search CLI flags and types (WL-0MLZVQF3P1OKBNZP) ‚Äî types must be defined first

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/database.ts`
- Updated `src/persistent-store.ts`
data.title: Implement search filter store logic

data.description:
Problem statement

Add automated tests (unit and integration) that verify the six newly-supported `wl search` filters (priority, assignee, stage, deleted, needsProducerReview, issue-type) work correctly on both the FTS (FTS5) path and the application-level fallback path. Tests must exercise individual filters and representative combinations, validate `--deleted` semantics and boolean parsing for `--needs-producer-review`, and include at least one CLI end-to-end test.

Users

- Developers and contributors who rely on `wl search` to find and triage work items.
- Automation and CI that depend on `--json` output and filtered queries.
- Project managers and producers who query by priority, stage, assignee, or review flags.

Example user stories

- As a developer, I want `wl search "bug" --priority high --assignee alice --json` to return only high-priority items assigned to Alice so I can triage quickly.
- As an automation consumer, I want search to respect `--stage in_progress` so CI scripts can find in-progress migration work.
- As a producer, I want `wl search "" --deleted` to include deleted items when explicitly requested and exclude them by default.

Success criteria

- Each of the six filters has at least one dedicated unit/integration test that exercises the FTS path.
- Each of the six filters has at least one dedicated unit/integration test that exercises the fallback path; fallback tests must run when FTS5 is unavailable in CI.
- At least two combination tests: `--priority + --assignee` and `--stage + --issue-type` covering both FTS and fallback paths.
- `--deleted` default exclusion and explicit inclusion are validated; `--needs-producer-review` boolean parsing is tested for `true/false/yes/no`.
- At least one CLI integration test verifies a representative flag in end-to-end human and `--json` output.

Constraints

- Do not modify production search logic unless tests surface clear regressions; scope is tests-only per intake.
- FTS-specific tests must be runnable (skipped or safe) in CI environments without SQLite FTS5 available.
- Use existing test helpers and patterns; avoid adding new test helper libraries unless strictly necessary.

Existing state

- Search feature and CLI exist: FTS5-backed search and an application-level fallback are implemented (`src/persistent-store.ts`, `src/database.ts`, CLI in `dist/commands/search.js`).
- Work items and planning already decompose this work: parent feature WL-0MLYN2DPW0CN62LM (Add missing filter flags), implementation WL-0MLZVQWYE1H6Y0H8 (Implement search filter store logic), and this test task WL-0MLZVRB3501I5NSU are present.
- Current tests include FTS-related tests but do not comprehensively cover the six new filters across both paths.

Desired change

- Add tests to `tests/fts-search.test.ts` (extend) to cover each filter on the FTS path.
- Add `tests/search-fallback.test.ts` to cover the fallback path equivalently and ensure these tests run in CI without FTS5.
- Add a CLI integration test (e.g., in `tests/cli/issue-status.test.ts`) that verifies at least one new flag in human and `--json` modes.
- Keep test scope focused on verification; do not perform production code changes unless a narrow, test-blocking bug is discovered and triaged.

Related work

- WL-0MLYN2DPW0CN62LM ‚Äî Add missing filter flags to `wl search` (feature providing the flags under test).
- WL-0MLZVQWYE1H6Y0H8 ‚Äî Implement search filter store logic (DB/query layer changes required for filters to work; this task is a dependency).
- WL-0MKXTCQZM1O8YCNH ‚Äî Core FTS Index (FTS schema & indexing; provides searchable index used by FTS tests).
- WL-0MKXTCTGZ0FCCLL7 ‚Äî CLI: search command (MVP) (CLI entrypoint used by integration tests).
- WL-0MKXTCXVL1KCO8PV ‚Äî App-level Fallback Search (fallback implementation; tests must exercise this when FTS5 is unavailable).
- CLI.md ‚Äî documentation with `worklog search` usage and `--rebuild-index` notes (repo doc to reference for CLI test expectations).

Implementation notes

- Place unit/focused FTS tests by extending `tests/fts-search.test.ts` following existing patterns.
- Add fallback tests in `tests/search-fallback.test.ts` so CI can skip or run fallback-only suites when FTS5 is missing.
- Reuse existing test fixtures/helpers; keep tests self-contained and deterministic.
- For CLI integration test, follow patterns in `tests/cli/issue-status.test.ts` and assert human and `--json` outputs.

Deliverables

- Modified `tests/fts-search.test.ts` with per-filter FTS tests.
- New `tests/search-fallback.test.ts` validating equivalent behavior via fallback path.
- Updated or new CLI integration test under `tests/cli/` verifying at least one new flag end-to-end.

Questions / open decisions

1. Confirmed scope is tests-only (no production changes) ‚Äî if tests reveal blocking bugs, should those be fixed in this work item or created as child work items? (recommended: create child bug work items)
2. CI configuration: tests must run without FTS5; preferred approach is to write fallback tests that run unconditionally and FTS tests that detect FTS5 and skip when unavailable.

data.title: Bug: wl next returns blocked items

data.description:
Summary:
The `wl next` command can recommend work items that are dependency-blocked. The current intake report contained garbled text from an earlier accidental update; this item should be a clean, idempotent bug intake that documents reproduction, expected behaviour, acceptance criteria, related work, and a plan.

User story:
As a producer, I want `wl next` to recommend only actionable work items so I can start work immediately without being blocked by unresolved dependencies.

Scope / definition:
- "Blocked" (per triage decision): dependencies-only. An item is considered blocked when it has at least one active dependency edge to another work item that is not actionable (completed/deleted/in_review/done are non-actionable per current `isDependencyActive` semantics). Do NOT treat the `blocked` status alone as the definition for this intake.
- This intake focuses on CLI `wl next` and the underlying selection function(s) (`findNextWorkItem` / `findNextWorkItemFromItems`). TUI changes are out-of-scope for this intake but may be a follow-up if required.

Observed behaviour:
`wl next` may return items that have active dependency blockers, causing producers to be shown work they cannot act on.

Expected behaviour:
By default `wl next` should exclude items that have active dependency blockers. A new `--include-blocked` flag should allow users to include dependency-blocked items when explicitly requested. Interactive/tui flows should get the same default unless a separate follow-up states otherwise.

Reproduction (next step):
- Per the operator's preference, attempt to reproduce from the repository worklog and tests. If reproduction is not found, create a minimal `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` fixture demonstrating the issue.
- Commands to use when reproducing: `wl next`, `wl list --status blocked`, `wl dep list <item-id>`.

Acceptance criteria (measurable):
1) A reproducible test case exists showing `wl next` returning a dependency-blocked item.
2) Selection logic is updated so dependency-blocked items are excluded from `wl next` by default.
3) Unit + integration tests verify `findNextWorkItem` and `wl next` do not return dependency-blocked items by default and that `--include-blocked` restores previous behaviour.
4) CLI help/docs updated to document the default and the new `--include-blocked` flag.
5) Implementation has a clear, linkable PR and corresponding work item comments referencing commit(s).

Suggested implementation approach:
- Add `includeBlocked` boolean flag to the `wl next` CLI and thread it through to `findNextWorkItem` / `findNextWorkItems`.
- In `findNextWorkItemFromItems`, apply a filter to remove items where `hasActiveBlockers(item.id)` is true unless `includeBlocked` is set.
- Add tests in `tests/database.test.ts` and `tests/cli/next.test.ts` (or equivalent) covering both default and `--include-blocked` behaviours.

Related work:
- WL-0MKW3FT5N0KW23X3, WL-0MKW48NQ913SQ212 (selection refactor & logging)
- WL-0MLDIFLCR1REKNGA (deleted-items returned)
- WL-0MLPSNIEL161NV6C (blocker detection heuristics)
- WL-0ML2TS8I409ALBU6 (exclude in-review items)
- WL-0MKXTSX9214QUFJF, WL-0MKXTSXPA1XVGQ9R (sort_index and ordering)

Notes:
- TF- prefixed IDs in earlier reports should be mapped to WL IDs where possible and recorded in the description.
- This work item should be idempotent: re-running the intake should not create duplicates. Use this WL id as the canonical intake for the bug.

## Summary
Add an `includeBlocked` parameter to `findNextWorkItemFromItems`, `findNextWorkItem`, and `findNextWorkItems` that defaults to `false`. When `false`, filter out items with `hasActiveBlockers(id) === true` from the general candidate pool early in the pipeline (alongside the existing deleted/in_review filters at the top of `findNextWorkItemFromItems`).

## User Experience Change
`wl next` will no longer recommend work items that have unresolved formal dependency edges. Producers only see actionable work.

## Minimal Implementation
1. Add `includeBlocked: boolean = false` parameter to `findNextWorkItemFromItems` (after `includeInReview`)
2. Add filter after the existing deleted/in_review filters: `if (!includeBlocked) { filteredItems = filteredItems.filter(item => !this.hasActiveBlockers(item.id)); }`
3. Add debug log line: `this.debug(debugPrefix + ' after dep-blocker filter=' + filteredItems.length)`
4. Thread the `includeBlocked` parameter through `findNextWorkItem` and `findNextWorkItems` public methods

## Files
- `src/database.ts` (lines ~839-1150)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `findNextWorkItemFromItems` accepts an `includeBlocked` boolean parameter defaulting to `false`
- When `includeBlocked=false`, items where `hasActiveBlockers()` returns true are excluded from the filtered candidate list
- When a critical item has active dependency blockers AND `includeBlocked=false`, the critical-items path (lines 889-930) still identifies and recommends blocker items
- When `includeBlocked=true`, no dependency-blocker filtering is applied (previous behaviour restored)
- An item with NO dependency edges is not affected by the filter

data.description:
**Headline**: Remove exclusive file-lock acquisition from read-only `wl` commands and switch the write path to atomic file replacement, eliminating lock contention that causes frequent "50 retries exhausted" errors during concurrent usage.

## Problem Statement

Read-only `wl` commands (`list`, `show`, `search`, `next`, `in-progress`, `recent`, `status`) acquire the same exclusive file lock as write commands, causing frequent lock acquisition failures ("50 retries exhausted") when an AI agent and a human (or multiple agents) run commands concurrently. Read-only commands should not need to acquire the file lock at all.

## Users

- **AI agents** running `wl` commands in parallel (e.g., multiple tool calls issuing `wl list`, `wl show`, `wl next` simultaneously alongside write operations like `wl update`, `wl create`).
  - *As an AI agent, I want to run `wl list` and `wl show` without blocking on or being blocked by concurrent writes, so that my tool calls do not fail with lock errors.*
- **Developers** using `wl` from the CLI while an agent session is also running `wl` commands.
  - *As a developer, I want to run `wl next` from my terminal without getting a lock error because an agent is simultaneously running `wl update`.*

## Success Criteria

1. Read-only commands (`list`, `show`, `search`, `next`, `in-progress`, `recent`, `status`, `dep list`, `doctor`) never acquire the exclusive file lock.
2. Read-only commands return results from the SQLite cache when a write is in progress, silently falling back to the last-imported state without warning or error.
3. Write operations use atomic file replacement (e.g., write to a temp file + rename) for the JSONL data file, so that concurrent readers cannot encounter a partially-written file.
4. All existing file-lock, database, and command tests continue to pass.
5. No new lock-related errors when running 5+ concurrent read-only commands alongside a write operation.

## Constraints

- **Scope**: This item covers removing lock acquisition from read paths only. The existing open item "Replace busy-wait sleepSync" (WL-0MLZJ7UJJ1BU2RHI) addresses CPU cost of lock retry loops and is out of scope here.
- **Consistency model**: Stale reads are acceptable. Read commands may return data from the last successful JSONL import into SQLite; they do not need to reflect in-flight writes.
- **Write atomicity**: The write path must be updated to use atomic file replacement (write-to-temp + rename) so that readers cannot see partial JSONL data. This replaces the current in-place write that relied on the lock for safety.
- **Backward compatibility**: The lock file format and `wl unlock` command must continue to work. Write-to-write locking must be preserved.
- **Platform support**: Must work on Linux, macOS, and WSL2.

## Risks & Assumptions

### Risks
- **Torn reads during atomic rename**: On some filesystems or NFS mounts, `rename()` may not be fully atomic with respect to concurrent `readFile()`. Mitigation: test on Linux (ext4/btrfs), macOS (APFS), and WSL2; add a graceful fallback (catch JSON parse errors and use cached SQLite data).
- **Race between mtime check and import**: A reader may stat the file, see a new mtime, and start reading just as a writer begins a new atomic rename. Mitigation: if the JSONL parse fails, fall back to the existing SQLite cache rather than crashing.
- **Scope creep**: The atomic-write change may surface opportunities to refactor other parts of the lock mechanism. Mitigation: record additional improvements as separate work items linked to this one rather than expanding scope.
- **Regression in write correctness**: Changing the write path (in-place to temp+rename) could introduce subtle bugs in export logic. Mitigation: existing test suite for file-lock and database must pass; add a specific test for concurrent read-during-write.

### Assumptions
- `fs.renameSync()` is atomic on the target platforms (Linux ext4/btrfs/tmpfs, macOS APFS/HFS+, WSL2) when source and destination are on the same filesystem.
- The temporary file for atomic writes will be created in the same directory as the target JSONL file (ensuring same-filesystem rename).
- Stale reads (returning data from the last successful SQLite import) are acceptable for all read-only commands; no command requires up-to-the-instant freshness.

## Existing State

The file-lock mechanism (`src/file-lock.ts`) uses a single exclusive advisory lock file (`worklog-data.jsonl.lock`) for all access to the JSONL data file. The `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` method in `database.ts:81` acquires this lock, and it is called:

1. **In the `WorklogDatabase` constructor** (line 54) -- every CLI command triggers this on startup.
2. **In read-only methods**: `getCommentsForWorkItem()` (line 1591), `listDependencyEdgesFrom()` (line 1339), `listDependencyEdgesTo()` (line 1347).
3. **In write methods**: `update()`, `delete()`, `addDependencyEdge()`, `removeDependencyEdge()` -- these additionally call `exportToJsonl()` which acquires the lock again (reentrant).

The `exportToJsonl()` method (line 141) writes the JSONL file **in-place** under the exclusive lock. This means removing the read lock without changing the write path would expose readers to partially-written files.

The lock uses a busy-wait retry loop (50 retries, 100ms delay via `sleepSync` spin-loop, 10s timeout). When contention is high (agent + human + multiple commands), readers frequently exhaust retries.

## Desired Change

1. **Remove lock from `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()`**: This method should stat the JSONL file and import it without acquiring the exclusive lock. If the JSONL file is being written to at that moment, the reader should use its existing SQLite cache (stale but consistent).
2. **Atomic JSONL writes**: Change `exportToJsonl()` to write to a temporary file and then atomically rename it to the target path. This ensures readers either see the old complete file or the new complete file, never a partial write. The exclusive lock is still acquired for write-to-write serialization.
3. **Remove lock from constructor path**: The `WorklogDatabase` constructor should call a lockless version of `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()`.
4. **Remove lock from read-only methods**: `getCommentsForWorkItem()`, `listDependencyEdgesFrom()`, `listDependencyEdgesTo()` should not trigger lock acquisition.

## Related Work

- **Process-level mutex for import/export/sync (WL-0MLG0DSFT09AKPTK)** - completed; introduced the file-lock mechanism.
- **Create file-lock.ts module with withFileLock helper (WL-0MLYPERY81Y84CNQ)** - completed; core lock implementation.
- **Integrate file lock into WorklogDatabase (WL-0MLYPF1YJ15FR8HY)** - completed; added lock to DB operations including reads.
- **Investigate file lock acquisition failure (WL-0MLZ0M1X81PGJLRJ)** - completed; previous investigation into the same class of error.
- **Replace busy-wait sleepSync (WL-0MLZJ7UJJ1BU2RHI)** - open; related but out of scope; addresses CPU cost of lock retries.
- **Bug: wl next returns blocked items (WL-0MLZWO96O1RS086V)** - completed; revealed per-item lock acquisition in hot loops via `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()`.
- **Key files**: `src/file-lock.ts`, `src/database.ts` (lines 54, 81, 141, 1339, 1347, 1591).

## Related work (automated report)

The following items and files were identified as directly related to the goals and context of this work item.

### Work Items

- **Process-level mutex for import/export/sync (WL-0MLG0DSFT09AKPTK)** [completed] -- This is the parent feature that introduced the file-lock mechanism. It established the `withFileLock` pattern used in `exportToJsonl()` and `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()`. The current item proposes removing the lock from the latter method, which was part of this original design.

- **Integrate file lock into WorklogDatabase (WL-0MLYPF1YJ15FR8HY)** [completed] -- This item added `withFileLock` to both `exportToJsonl()` and `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` in `database.ts`. The read-lock addition in `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` is the direct cause of the contention this item seeks to fix.

- **Investigate file lock acquisition failure (WL-0MLZ0M1X81PGJLRJ)** [completed, critical] -- Previous investigation into the same class of "50 retries exhausted" error. That investigation focused on stale locks from crashed processes and resulted in age-based expiry and `wl unlock`. This item addresses a different root cause: unnecessary lock acquisition on reads.

- **Replace busy-wait sleepSync (WL-0MLZJ7UJJ1BU2RHI)** [open, low priority] -- Proposes replacing the CPU-burning `sleepSync` spin-loop with `Atomics.wait` or similar. While out of scope for this item, reducing read-side lock acquisition will also reduce how often the sleepSync loop is entered, making the two items complementary.

- **Bug: wl next returns blocked items (WL-0MLZWO96O1RS086V)** [completed] -- Revealed that `wl next` was hanging due to per-item calls to `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` (each acquiring the file lock) inside `hasActiveBlockers()`. The fix optimized the hot loop, but the underlying problem of read operations acquiring exclusive locks persists.

### Repository Files

- **`src/file-lock.ts`** -- The file-lock implementation. Contains `withFileLock()`, `acquireFileLock()`, `releaseFileLock()`, and the `sleepSync` busy-wait. Changes to write-side atomicity may require updates here.
- **`src/database.ts`** -- The `WorklogDatabase` class. Lines 54 (constructor), 81 (`refreshFromJsonlIfNewer` with lock), 141 (`exportToJsonl` with lock), 1339/1347 (dependency methods calling refresh), 1591 (`getCommentsForWorkItem` calling refresh). All read-side lock removals happen in this file.
- **`tests/file-lock.test.ts`** -- Existing tests for the file-lock module. Must continue to pass after changes.
- **`tests/database.test.ts`** -- Existing tests for the database module. Must continue to pass after changes.
data.title: Remove file lock from read-only operations to reduce contention

data.description:
Problem statement

`wl next` sometimes recommends lower-priority work instead of a higher-priority item that is unblocked and would unblock other high-priority work. This causes producers to work on less-impactful tasks and increases overall lead time.

Users

- Producers and engineers who run `wl next` to get an actionable next task.
- Release coordinators and triage engineers who rely on `wl next` to prioritise unblockers.

Example user stories
- As a producer, I want `wl next` to recommend the highest-priority item that is actionable (unblocked) and that resolves downstream high-priority work so I can make the most impact.
- As an engineer, I want deterministic selection rules so `wl next` behaves predictably across runs and datasets.

Success criteria

- The selection algorithm ranks candidates with the precedence: priority ‚Üí blocks high-priority ‚Üí unblocked ‚Üí existing heuristics (assignee, recency, etc.).
- Unit tests cover `findNextWorkItemFromItems` and related helpers for the new ordering, including tie-breakers.
- An integration test using the ToneForge `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` fixture asserts that the expected recommendation (TF-0MLXF7XBI0J0H3P8) is returned for the described dataset.
- CLI help and `CLI.md` document the updated ranking behaviour and note backwards-compatible flags/behaviour (no change to `--include-blocked`).
- All existing tests remain passing; no regression in `--include-blocked` or critical-path surfacing logic.

Constraints

- Preserve existing `--include-blocked` semantics and the critical-path logic that surfaces blockers when appropriate.
- Keep the change contained to the selection/ranking logic and tests; avoid UI/TUI changes in this intake.
- No schema or data-model changes.

Existing state

- The repository already supports an `includeBlocked` option and threaded CLI flag (`src/commands/next.ts`, `src/cli-types.ts`, `src/database.ts`).
- Current selection logic is implemented in `src/database.ts` (`findNextWorkItemFromItems` / `findNextWorkItem` / `findNextWorkItems`).
- Tests referencing includeBlocked and `findNextWorkItem` exist in `tests/database.test.ts`.
- There are existing related work items that implemented dependency-blocker filtering and wiring (see Related work below).

Desired change

- Implement the ranking precedence: sort by priority first, then prefer items that block other high-priority items, then prefer unblocked items, then fall back to existing heuristics (assignee match, recency, etc.).
- Add unit tests for the selection ordering and tie-breakers.
- Add an integration test that runs against the ToneForge dataset and asserts the expected item is recommended.
- Update `CLI.md` and add an in-code comment near `findNextWorkItemFromItems` documenting the new ranking precedence.

Related work

Potentially related docs
- `src/database.ts` ‚Äî selection logic and `findNextWorkItemFromItems` implementation
- `src/commands/next.ts` ‚Äî CLI wiring and `includeBlocked` flag handling
- `tests/database.test.ts` ‚Äî existing tests for `findNextWorkItem` and includeBlocked cases

Potentially related work items
- Wire --include-blocked CLI flag (WL-0MM04H3N11BK85P9) ‚Äî wired the CLI flag and normalizeActionArgs
- Add dependency-blocker filter (WL-0MM04GRDP11MCFX4) ‚Äî added `includeBlocked` parameter and filtering behaviour
- Add dependency-blocker filter tests (WL-0MM04HDI618Y7DT0) ‚Äî tests that verify includeBlocked filtering
- Parent intake: Default next behaviour & includeBlocked (WL-0MLZWO96O1RS086V) ‚Äî planning and decomposition of next-related work

Notes / implementation hints

- The change is likely localized to `src/database.ts` near `findNextWorkItemFromItems` (search for `includeBlocked` and the critical-items block around lines 850-930). Add an intermediate scoring or comparator step where candidates are scored by the new precedence rather than only filtered.
- Keep `includeBlocked` behaviour unchanged: the new ranking only affects ordering among candidates that would already be considered.
- Add tests mirroring existing patterns in `tests/database.test.ts` and add a fixture-based integration test that loads the ToneForge `.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` file to reproduce the reported dataset.

Risks & assumptions

- Risk: scope creep ‚Äî selection tuning could expand into broader ranking refactors. Mitigation: record unrelated or optional algorithmic improvements as separate work items and keep this change narrowly focused to the precedence change and tests.
- Risk: regressions in critical-path/blocker surfacing logic. Mitigation: preserve `--include-blocked` semantics and add unit tests covering critical-path behaviour.
- Assumption: ToneForge dataset reproduces the reported behaviour and is available at `/home/rogardle/projects/ToneForge/.worklog/worklog-data.jsonl` for the integration test.

Next steps

- Please review this intake draft and either approve or provide edits/clarifications. When approved I will run the five conservative intake reviews and produce a final draft to update the work item description.

One-line headline summary

Prefer unblocked, high-priority unblockers when `wl next` selects the next work item, with tests and docs to prevent regressions.
data.title: Worklog: next() prefers lower-priority blockers over higher-priority blocking items

data.description:
## Summary

Remove the `withFileLock()` wrapper from `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` so all read-only commands become lockless.

## Context

Parent: Remove file lock from read-only operations to reduce contention (WL-0MM085T7Y16UWSVD)

The `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` method in `database.ts:81` currently acquires the exclusive file lock via `withFileLock()`. This lock is called from 4 sites:
1. Constructor (line 54) -- every CLI command triggers this on startup
2. `getCommentsForWorkItem()` (line 1591)
3. `listDependencyEdgesFrom()` (line 1339)
4. `listDependencyEdgesTo()` (line 1347)

Since `exportToJsonl()` in `src/jsonl.ts` already uses atomic write (temp-file + `renameSync`), readers cannot encounter a partially-written file. The lock on the read path is therefore unnecessary and causes contention.

## User Story

As an AI agent or developer, I want read-only `wl` commands to execute without acquiring the exclusive file lock, so that concurrent reads do not fail with 'retries exhausted' errors.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` no longer calls `withFileLock()`
- The method still correctly stats the file, checks mtime, and imports when newer
- All 4 call sites (constructor, `getCommentsForWorkItem`, `listDependencyEdgesFrom`, `listDependencyEdgesTo`) use the lockless path
- `exportToJsonl()` in `database.ts:141` retains its `withFileLock` wrapper unchanged
- All existing `database.test.ts` tests pass
- Read-only commands must not fail with 'retries exhausted' when a write lock is held by another process

## Minimal Implementation

- Remove the `withFileLock(this.lockPath, () => { ... })` wrapper from `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` in `database.ts:81`, keeping inner logic intact
- Verify `exportToJsonl()` retains its `withFileLock` wrapper
- Run existing test suite

## Key Files

- `src/database.ts` (lines 74-123)
- `src/file-lock.ts` (unchanged)
- `tests/database.test.ts`

## Dependencies

None (first feature to implement)
data.title: Remove lock from refreshFromJsonlIfNewer

data.description:
## Summary

Add try-catch around the JSONL import in the lockless `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` to fall back to cached SQLite data on parse errors, with debug-level logging.

## Context

Parent: Remove file lock from read-only operations to reduce contention (WL-0MM085T7Y16UWSVD)

After removing the file lock from `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` (WL-0MM09W1K81PB9P0C), readers may encounter transient conditions such as:
- A partial read during an atomic rename race (filesystem-dependent)
- The JSONL file being deleted between stat and read
- Corrupted data from an interrupted write on non-POSIX filesystems

In all these cases, the reader should gracefully fall back to the existing SQLite cache rather than crashing.

## User Story

As an AI agent or developer, I want read-only commands to return cached data rather than crashing when the JSONL file is temporarily unavailable or corrupted, so that my workflow is never interrupted by transient filesystem conditions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- If `importFromJsonl()` throws during a lockless read, the error is caught and the method returns without crashing
- The existing SQLite cache remains intact and serves the read
- A debug log line is emitted to stderr when `WL_DEBUG` is set (e.g., `[wl:db] JSONL parse failed, using cached data: <error message>`)
- No output when `WL_DEBUG` is not set
- A unit test verifies: given a corrupted JSONL file, `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` does not throw and the database returns previously-cached data
- If the JSONL file is deleted between stat and read, the method must not throw

## Minimal Implementation

- Wrap `importFromJsonl()` call and subsequent store operations in `refreshFromJsonlIfNewer()` in a try-catch
- In the catch block, emit a debug log (using the `debugLog` pattern from `file-lock.ts` or the existing `this.debug()` method, gated by `WL_DEBUG`) and return
- Add unit test with deliberately malformed JSONL file
- Add unit test for file-deleted-between-stat-and-read race

## Key Files

- `src/database.ts` (lines 74-123, specifically around the `importFromJsonl` call at line 107)
- `tests/database.test.ts`

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Remove lock from refreshFromJsonlIfNewer (WL-0MM09W1K81PB9P0C)
data.title: Graceful fallback on JSONL parse errors

## Summary

Add a vitest test that verifies 5+ concurrent read-only operations do not error when running alongside a write operation, validating zero lock-related errors under concurrency.

## Context

Parent: Remove file lock from read-only operations to reduce contention (WL-0MM085T7Y16UWSVD)

The success criteria for the parent work item require: 'No new lock-related errors when running 5+ concurrent read-only commands alongside a write operation.' This feature delivers the automated test that validates this requirement.

## User Story

As a developer, I want an automated concurrency test in the vitest suite that proves lockless reads work correctly alongside writes, so that regressions are caught in CI.

## Acceptance Criteria

- A vitest test forks N child processes (N >= 5) performing reads while one performs writes on a shared JSONL file
- No child process exits with a lock acquisition error
- All read processes return valid (possibly stale) data
- The test passes reliably in CI (no flakiness from timing)
- No child process hangs or deadlocks (test completes within 30 second timeout)
- The test runs as part of the standard `vitest` suite

## Minimal Implementation

- Create `tests/lockless-reads.test.ts`
- Use `child_process.fork()` or `child_process.execSync` to spawn concurrent processes that instantiate `WorklogDatabase` with a shared JSONL file
- One writer process performs create + export operations while readers run list/show operations
- Assert: no process throws, all readers return arrays (possibly empty on first read, populated after import)
- Set a 30-second timeout on the test to catch deadlocks

## Key Files

- New: `tests/lockless-reads.test.ts`
- Reference: `src/database.ts`, `src/file-lock.ts`

## Dependencies

- Feature 1: Remove lock from refreshFromJsonlIfNewer (WL-0MM09W1K81PB9P0C)
- Feature 2: Graceful fallback on JSONL parse errors (WL-0MM09WH9M0A076CY)

data.description:
Problem statement

`wl search <work-item-id>` does not reliably return the work item when queried by ID. Agents and humans frequently provide full or partial IDs (sometimes without the project prefix) and expect a fast, deterministic match; current behaviour either fails to return the exact item or ranks text references ahead of an exact ID match.

Users

- Producers, maintainers, and developers who need to look up a known work item quickly by ID.
- Automated agents and scripts that receive or log work-item IDs and use `wl search` to fetch items programmatically.

Example user stories
- As a producer, when I run `wl search WL-0MM0AN2IT0OOC2TW` I want the matching item returned first so I can inspect or act on it immediately.
- As an agent, when I receive `0MM0AN2IT0OOC2TW` (no `WL-` prefix), I want `wl search 0MM0AN2IT0OOC2TW` to resolve using the repo's configured prefix and return the matching item.

Success criteria

- Exact full-ID queries return the matching work item as the top result (fast exact-match path) and also include ranked FTS results below it when relevant.
- Prefix-less IDs are resolved using the repository's configured project prefix (e.g., `WL`) and behave the same as prefixed full IDs.
- Partial-ID substring matches are supported when the query token length is >= 6 characters; exact matches are ranked higher than partials.
- Tests: unit + integration tests cover both the FTS path and the application-level fallback path; at least one CLI end-to-end test asserts exact-ID and partial-ID behaviours. Tests run in CI with and without FTS available.
- No regression in existing search behaviour (title/description/comment/snippet matching, flags, and `--json` output remain consistent).

Constraints

- Maintain backwards compatibility for existing `wl search` flags and JSON output.
- Do not change FTS index schema or ranking rules beyond adding an efficient exact-ID short-circuit; prefer implementing ID-match logic in the CLI/controller layer or DB wrapper to avoid reindex work.
- Prefix resolution must use the project's configured prefix when an unprefixed token appears to be an ID (avoid accidental rewrites of normal search terms).
- Performance: exact-ID path should be cheap (O(1)/indexed lookup) and not materially degrade search latency.

Existing state

- The project has an FTS5-backed `wl search` command (WL-0MKRPG61W1NKGY78) and the CLI supports many flags; CLI flags parity work (WL-0MLYN2DPW0CN62LM) added filter flags but store-level filter application is handled in WL-0MLZVQWYE1H6Y0H8.
- Current `wl search` returns ranked FTS results and supports `--json`. Exact ID queries are not handled as a prioritized special-case; searches for IDs may return no matches or return references but not the canonical item first.

Desired change

- Implement an exact-ID short-circuit in the `wl search` flow that:
  1) normalizes the query token (trim, uppercase, accept or add missing `WL-` prefix using repo prefix if unprefixed),
  2) attempts a fast exact lookup for that ID (DB primary-key or indexed field), and
  3) if found, return that item at the top of results and then append the normal FTS-ranked results (excluding duplicates).
- Support prefix-less ID resolution: if a single token looks like an ID (alphanumeric of length >= 6 and matches configured ID pattern), resolve it using the repo's prefix and try exact lookup.
  - Resolution rule: always assume the repository's configured prefix when a single token appears to be an ID (recommended behaviour).
- Implement partial-ID substring behaviour for queries >= 6 chars: search for items whose `id` contains the substring and include them in ranked results below exact matches.
- Add unit tests for fast exact-ID lookup, partial-ID behaviour, and prefix-less lookup; add integration tests that exercise the FTS and fallback paths and a CLI e2e test that asserts the exact-ID behaviour in human and `--json` output.

Related work

- WL-0MKRPG61W1NKGY78 ‚Äî Full-text search (FTS5) feature: FTS index and `wl search` command (ranking, snippets, rebuild/backfill). Relevant for how FTS results are appended.
- WL-0MLYN2DPW0CN62LM ‚Äî Add missing filter flags to `wl search`: CLI flags and types (priority, assignee, stage, deleted, needs-producer-review, issue-type). Ensures flag parity and example usage.
- WL-0MLZVQWYE1H6Y0H8 ‚Äî Implement search filter store logic: DB/query-layer application of filters (WHERE clauses) used by `wl search` backends.
- WL-0MLZVRB3501I5NSU ‚Äî Add search filter test coverage: tests that exercise filters on FTS and fallback paths; will be extended for ID-match cases per success criteria.
- CLI.md ‚Äî Examples and help text for `wl search` (update guidance if output ordering changes or examples need clarifying).
- AGENTS.md ‚Äî Agent usage guidance (`wl search <keywords> --json`) ‚Äî important to ensure agent-oriented JSON remains stable.

Notes / open questions (for reviewer)

- Confirm the repository prefix resolution rule for unprefixed IDs (I assume the project's configured prefix should be applied; if multiple prefixes are used across projects additional rules may be needed).
- Confirm the minimum substring length for partial-ID matches (draft uses 6 characters to reduce noise). If you want a different threshold say so.
data.title: wl search does not find work items by ID

## Summary

Add a scoring boost in `computeScore()` so that candidates which unblock high-priority downstream items rank higher among equal-priority peers.

## User Experience Change

When running `wl next`, among equal-priority candidates the algorithm will now prefer items that unblock high-priority or critical downstream work. This reduces lead time by ensuring unblockers are worked on first.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `computeScore()` must add a boost (e.g., +500) when the item is a dependency blocker of at least one `blocked` item with `high` or `critical` priority
- The boost must be proportional: blocking a `critical` item scores higher than blocking a `high` item
- The boost must not override the primary priority ranking (priority weight 1000 remains dominant)
- `--include-blocked` semantics must be unchanged (only controls candidate pool, not scoring)
- Existing `selectBySortIndex` / `selectByScore` call sites must require no changes

## Minimal Implementation

- In `computeScore()` (`src/database.ts`), call `getDependencyEdgesTo(item.id)` (already exists at `src/database.ts:1352`) to find items that depend on this item
- For each active blocker relationship where the blocked item has `high` or `critical` priority, add a proportional boost
- Add an in-code comment documenting the ranking precedence: priority -> blocks-high-priority -> unblocked -> existing heuristics

## Key Files

- `src/database.ts` ‚Äî `computeScore()` around line 745, `getDependencyEdgesTo()` at line 1352
- `src/persistent-store.ts` ‚Äî `getDependencyEdgesTo()` at line 664

## Dependencies

None (foundation for all other features under WL-0MM08MZPJ0ZQ38EK)

## Deliverables

- Modified `src/database.ts` with updated `computeScore()` and in-code documentation

data.description: When we make a change to a record using Update in the tui that change is not automatically reflected in other wl commands. For example, if we update the status of an item to in_progress and then immidiately hit n (for next) we might be given that same item. This suggests that the update is not being written through to the DB. It should be. However, this might create conflicts if the DB has been updated in a sync. We need to investigate this and establish whether it is a risk that needs mitigating, or not.

data.description:
# Intake Brief: Add exponential back-off to file lock retry

> Replace the fixed-interval retry loop and CPU-burning `sleepSync` in `acquireFileLock()` with exponential backoff (1.5x, jitter, 30s timeout) and `Atomics.wait`, reducing contention overhead and CPU waste under concurrent agent workloads.

**Work Item:** WL-0MM0BT1FA0X23LTN | **Type:** task | **Priority:** high

## Problem Statement

The `acquireFileLock()` retry loop in `src/file-lock.ts` uses a fixed 100ms delay between every retry attempt. Under contention from multiple concurrent agents or processes, this fixed-interval polling creates unnecessary CPU load, lock-file churn, and thundering-herd effects. Additionally, the `sleepSync` helper uses a CPU-burning busy-wait spin-loop, compounding the waste. The current default timeout of 10s is too short for high-contention scenarios with parallel agents.

## Users

- **Developers and agents** running multiple `wl` commands concurrently (e.g. parallel agents working on the same repository).
  - *As a user running multiple `wl` commands concurrently, I want the lock retry to use exponential back-off with jitter so that contention is resolved more efficiently and the system degrades gracefully under load.*
  - *As a user, I want the retry sleep to not burn CPU cycles so that my machine remains responsive during lock contention.*

## Success Criteria

1. Retry delay in `acquireFileLock()` increases exponentially from `retryDelay` (default 100ms) using a 1.5x multiplier, capped at `maxRetryDelay` (default 2000ms).
2. A random jitter of up to 25% of the current delay is added to each sleep to avoid thundering-herd effects.
3. The `retries` option is removed from `FileLockOptions`; the `timeout` (default bumped from 10s to 30s) is the sole limiter for retry duration.
4. `sleepSync` is replaced with `Atomics.wait` on a `SharedArrayBuffer`, eliminating CPU-burning busy-wait.
5. `FileLockOptions` accepts an optional `maxRetryDelay` field; omitting it preserves the 2000ms default.
6. The timeout deadline is still respected ‚Äî backoff delays are clamped to remaining time before deadline.
7. All existing file-lock tests pass (updated as needed to reflect the removal of `retries`).
8. New unit tests verify backoff progression, jitter bounds, and the `Atomics.wait`-based sleep.
9. The solution works on Linux, macOS, and WSL2.

## Constraints

- **Synchronous only:** The lock acquisition and sleep must remain synchronous (no async/Promise-based sleep). The entire codebase uses synchronous I/O for lock operations.
- **Backward compatibility:** Callers not passing the removed `retries` field must continue to work without changes. The `retries` field is removed from the `FileLockOptions` interface (any callers still passing it will get a TypeScript compilation error, which is acceptable as a deliberate breaking change).
- **Platform support:** `Atomics.wait` must work on Linux, macOS, and WSL2. Node.js supports this natively.
- **No async refactoring:** This change should not require converting any callers of `acquireFileLock` or `withFileLock` to async patterns.

## Existing State

- `acquireFileLock()` (`src/file-lock.ts:203-313`) implements a synchronous retry loop with:
  - Fixed delay of `retryDelay` (default 100ms) between attempts
  - Maximum of `retries` (default 50) attempts
  - Overall `timeout` (default 10s) deadline
  - Stale lock cleanup (dead PID, age-based expiry, corrupted files)
  - Diagnostic logging
- `sleepSync()` (`src/file-lock.ts:114-119`) is a busy-wait spin-loop: `while (Date.now() < end) {}`
- `FileLockOptions` (`src/file-lock.ts:21-32`) has 5 fields: `retries`, `retryDelay`, `timeout`, `staleLockCleanup`, `maxLockAge`
- 55 existing tests in `tests/file-lock.test.ts` covering acquisition, stale locks, error messages, reentrancy, concurrency, and diagnostic logging
- Consumers: `src/database.ts`, `src/commands/sync.ts`, `src/commands/export.ts`, `src/commands/import.ts`, `src/commands/unlock.ts`

## Desired Change

1. **Replace `sleepSync`** with an `Atomics.wait`-based implementation:
   ```typescript
   function sleepSync(ms: number): void {
     const buf = new SharedArrayBuffer(4);
     Atomics.wait(new Int32Array(buf), 0, 0, ms);
   }
   ```
2. **Implement exponential backoff** in the retry loop:
   - Start at `retryDelay` (default 100ms)
   - Multiply by 1.5x on each failed attempt
   - Cap at `maxRetryDelay` (default 2000ms)
   - Add random jitter: `delay + Math.random() * delay * 0.25`
   - Clamp to remaining time before deadline
3. **Remove `retries` from `FileLockOptions`** ‚Äî the retry loop runs until `timeout` is reached.
4. **Bump default `timeout`** from 10s to 30s.
5. **Add `maxRetryDelay`** to `FileLockOptions` (optional, default 2000ms).
6. **Update tests:**
   - Remove/update tests that rely on `retries` option
   - Add tests for backoff progression (verify delays increase with 1.5x multiplier)
   - Add tests for jitter bounds (within 0-25% of base delay)
   - Add tests verifying `Atomics.wait`-based sleep does not busy-wait
   - Update concurrency tests for new timeout default
7. **Close WL-0MLZJ7UJJ1BU2RHI** (Replace busy-wait sleepSync) as absorbed into this work item.

## Risks & Assumptions

- **Breaking change (retries removal):** Removing `retries` from `FileLockOptions` will cause TypeScript compilation errors for any callers passing it. Mitigation: grep for all usages of `retries` in the codebase and update them as part of this work item. The only known consumers are internal (`database.ts`, `sync.ts`, `export.ts`, `import.ts`).
- **Atomics.wait on main thread:** `Atomics.wait` throws on the main thread in browser environments, but this is a Node.js CLI tool so this is not a concern. Assumption: all consumers run in Node.js.
- **Test timing sensitivity:** Backoff with jitter makes retry timing non-deterministic. Tests that assert specific retry counts or timing may become flaky. Mitigation: test backoff progression by mocking or instrumenting `sleepSync` rather than measuring wall-clock time.
- **30s default timeout:** The increased timeout means commands under contention may block for up to 30s before failing. Assumption: this is acceptable for the concurrent-agent use case and preferable to premature failure.
- **Scope creep:** This item absorbs WL-0MLZJ7UJJ1BU2RHI (sleepSync replacement) but should not expand further (e.g., async lock refactoring, distributed locking). Mitigation: record opportunities for additional improvements as separate work items linked to this one rather than expanding scope.

## Suggested Next Step

This work item is well-scoped for direct implementation without a separate PRD. Proceed to planning: break into sub-tasks (sleepSync replacement, backoff implementation, retries removal + timeout bump, test updates) and begin implementation from WL-0MM0BT1FA0X23LTN.

## Related Work

- **Replace busy-wait sleepSync** (WL-0MLZJ7UJJ1BU2RHI) ‚Äî open, low priority. Will be absorbed into this work item and closed.
- **Create file-lock.ts module** (WL-0MLYPERY81Y84CNQ) ‚Äî completed. Original module creation.
- **Investigate file lock acquisition failure** (WL-0MLZ0M1X81PGJLRJ) ‚Äî completed, critical. Root-cause investigation that surfaced the need for backoff.
- **Remove file lock from read-only operations** (WL-0MM085T7Y16UWSVD) ‚Äî completed. Reduced contention by removing locks from reads.
- **Corrupted Lock File Recovery** (WL-0MLZJ5P7B16JIV0W) ‚Äî completed. Lock file resilience.
- **Improved Lock Error Messages** (WL-0MLZJ6J500NLB8FI) ‚Äî completed. Error diagnostics.
- **Lock Diagnostic Logging** (WL-0MLZJ7HFO1BWSF5P) ‚Äî completed. Debug logging.

## Related work (automated report)

### Related work items

- **Process-level mutex for import/export/sync** (WL-0MLG0DSFT09AKPTK) ‚Äî completed, high priority. The parent epic that created \`src/file-lock.ts\` with the original fixed-interval retry loop and \`sleepSync\` implementation that this work item replaces. Understanding the original design intent and constraints is essential context.
- **Write tests for file-lock module and concurrent access** (WL-0MLYPFP4C0G9U463) ‚Äî completed, high priority. Created the 38 existing file-lock tests (commit cba5345) that must be updated when \`retries\` is removed and backoff behavior changes. Many tests pass explicit \`retries\` values that will need migration to timeout-only patterns.
- **Age-Based Lock Expiry** (WL-0MLZJ64X215T0FKP) ‚Äî completed, high priority. Added \`maxLockAge\` to \`FileLockOptions\` and age-based stale lock detection in the same retry loop that this work item modifies. The backoff changes must preserve the age-expiry check ordering and not regress the 7 age-based tests.
- **wl unlock CLI Command** (WL-0MLZJ70Q21JYANTG) ‚Äî completed, high priority. Exported \`readLockInfo\` and \`isProcessAlive\` from \`file-lock.ts\`. These exports and the unlock command's lock metadata display must remain compatible after the \`FileLockOptions\` interface changes.
- **Integrate file lock into sync, import, and export commands** (WL-0MLYPFD7W0SFGTZY) ‚Äî completed, medium priority. These command-level consumers call \`withFileLock()\` which delegates to \`acquireFileLock()\`. The changed default timeout (10s to 30s) and removed \`retries\` option will affect their lock acquisition behavior under contention.

### Related files

- **\`src/file-lock.ts\`** ‚Äî Primary implementation file. Contains \`sleepSync\` (line 114), \`FileLockOptions\` interface (line 21), and \`acquireFileLock\` retry loop (line 203). All changes land here.
- **\`tests/file-lock.test.ts\`** ‚Äî 55 existing tests with 38 calls to \`acquireFileLock\`, many passing explicit \`retries\` values that must be updated or removed.
- **\`src/database.ts\`** ‚Äî Imports \`withFileLock\` (line 12) and calls it from \`exportToJsonl\` (line 156). Consumer affected by timeout default change.
- **\`src/commands/sync.ts\`** ‚Äî Imports \`withFileLock\` (line 15) and wraps \`performSync\` (line 287). Consumer affected by timeout default change.
- **\`src/commands/export.ts\`** ‚Äî Imports \`withFileLock\` (line 8) and wraps export operation (line 22). Consumer affected by timeout default change.
- **\`src/commands/import.ts\`** ‚Äî Imports \`withFileLock\` (line 8) and wraps import operation (line 22). Consumer affected by timeout default change.
- **\`src/commands/unlock.ts\`** ‚Äî Consumes exported \`readLockInfo\` and \`isProcessAlive\` from \`file-lock.ts\`. Must remain compatible after interface changes.
- **\`tests/lockless-reads.test.ts\`** ‚Äî Concurrency test that exercises lock behavior with concurrent readers/writers. May need timeout adjustments after the default changes.

data.description:
Summary:
There is an inconsistency in `wl list` where using the human-readable flag `--stage in_review` returns no results but the same filter passed with `--json` returns items. Both invocations should produce the same set of results.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Run: `wl list --stage in_review`
2. Observe no items are printed in the normal output
3. Run: `wl list --stage in_review --json`
4. Observe JSON output contains one or more work items in stage `in_review`

Expected behavior:
- `wl list --stage in_review` and `wl list --stage in_review --json` should return the same set of work items (just formatted differently). The human-readable output should include any items shown by the `--json` output.

Observed behavior:
- `--json` returns work items while the plain output appears empty for the same stage filter.

Suggested investigation areas / possible causes:
- CLI output formatting code path applies an additional filter or silently fails to render items when not in `--json` mode.
- Stage parsing or canonicalization differs between the two code paths (e.g. `in_review` vs `in-review` or case-sensitivity mismatch).
- The tabular rendering code may drop items when certain fields are missing or when there is unexpected data.

Suggested fix:
- Ensure stage filter logic is shared between JSON and non-JSON code paths or centralize filtering logic.
- Add tests to cover `wl list --stage <stage>` rendering with and without `--json` to prevent regression.

Acceptance criteria:
- Reproduce the issue locally and add a failing test that demonstrates the discrepancy.
- Implement a fix so that `wl list --stage in_review` displays the same items as `wl list --stage in_review --json`.
- Add unit/integration tests verifying parity between JSON and human-readable output for stage filters.

Additional notes:
- If this is environment-specific (e.g., terminal width or color settings), include reproduction environment details in the issue comments.
data.title: wl list --stage in_review returns nothing while --json shows content

## Summary

Replace the CPU-burning busy-wait spin-loop in `sleepSync()` (`src/file-lock.ts:114-119`) with a non-blocking `Atomics.wait` implementation.

## User Story

As a user running multiple `wl` commands concurrently, I want the retry sleep to not burn CPU cycles so that my machine remains responsive during lock contention.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `sleepSync()` must use `Atomics.wait(new Int32Array(new SharedArrayBuffer(4)), 0, 0, ms)` instead of busy-wait
- No CPU spin must be observed during sleep (measurable via process CPU time before/after)
- `sleepSync(0)` and `sleepSync(-1)` must not throw or hang
- All 55 existing file-lock tests must pass without modification
- Implementation must work on Linux, macOS, and WSL2 (Node.js native support)

## Minimal Implementation

1. Replace the body of `sleepSync()` at `src/file-lock.ts:114-119` with:
   ```typescript
   function sleepSync(ms: number): void {
     const buf = new SharedArrayBuffer(4);
     Atomics.wait(new Int32Array(buf), 0, 0, ms);
   }
   ```
2. Add unit tests verifying sleep does not busy-wait (compare CPU time before/after a 200ms sleep)
3. Add edge-case tests for `sleepSync(0)` and `sleepSync(-1)`
4. Verify existing test suite passes

## Dependencies

None (this is the foundation task).

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/file-lock.ts`
- New test cases in `tests/file-lock.test.ts`

## Related Files

- `src/file-lock.ts:114-119` (sleepSync implementation)
- `tests/file-lock.test.ts` (existing test suite)
data.title: Replace sleepSync with Atomics.wait

data.description:
## Summary

Replace the fixed-delay retry logic in `acquireFileLock()` with exponential backoff (1.5x multiplier, 25% jitter, capped at `maxRetryDelay`).

## User Story

As a user running multiple `wl` commands concurrently, I want the lock retry to use exponential back-off with jitter so that contention is resolved more efficiently and the system degrades gracefully under load.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Retry delay must start at `retryDelay` (default 100ms) and multiply by 1.5x each attempt
- Delay must be capped at `maxRetryDelay` (default 2000ms, new `FileLockOptions` field)
- Backoff must not exceed `maxRetryDelay` even after many iterations
- Random jitter must be >= 0 and <= 25% of base delay (formula: `delay + Math.random() * delay * 0.25`)
- Jitter must never be negative and must never cause delay to exceed the cap plus 25% of the cap
- Sleep duration must be clamped to remaining time before deadline
- Diagnostic logs must include the current delay value per attempt
- New unit tests must verify backoff progression (delays increase with 1.5x multiplier) by instrumenting/mocking `sleepSync` to capture delay values
- New unit tests must verify jitter bounds (within 0-25% of base delay)

## Minimal Implementation

1. Add `maxRetryDelay` field to `FileLockOptions` interface (optional, default 2000ms)
2. Add `DEFAULT_MAX_RETRY_DELAY_MS = 2000` constant
3. In `acquireFileLock()`, track `currentDelay` variable initialized to `retryDelay`
4. After each failed attempt: `currentDelay = Math.min(currentDelay * 1.5, maxRetryDelay)`
5. Add jitter: `sleepDelay = currentDelay + Math.random() * currentDelay * 0.25`
6. Clamp to remaining time: `sleepDelay = Math.min(sleepDelay, deadline - Date.now())`
7. Update diagnostic log to include delay value
8. Add tests that mock/instrument `sleepSync` to capture delay progression
9. Add tests for jitter bounds

## Dependencies

- Replace sleepSync with Atomics.wait (WL-0MM0WORIS1UCKM55) must be completed first.

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/file-lock.ts` (new interface field, backoff logic)
- New test cases in `tests/file-lock.test.ts`

## Related Files

- `src/file-lock.ts:21-32` (FileLockOptions interface)
- `src/file-lock.ts:203-313` (acquireFileLock retry loop)
- `tests/file-lock.test.ts` (test suite)
data.title: Implement exponential backoff with jitter

data.description:
## Summary

Remove the `retries` field from `FileLockOptions`, change the retry loop to run until `timeout` is reached (default bumped from 10s to 30s), and update all consumers, error messages, and test call sites.

## User Story

As a user running multiple `wl` commands concurrently, I want the lock retry to be governed solely by a timeout (not a fixed retry count) so that the system adapts to varying contention levels without premature failure.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `retries` field must be removed from `FileLockOptions` interface
- Passing `retries` in options must cause a TypeScript compile error (verified by absence of the field in the interface)
- `DEFAULT_RETRIES` constant must be removed
- Retry loop must run `while (Date.now() < deadline)` instead of `for (attempt <= retries)`
- Default `timeout` must change from 10,000ms to 30,000ms
- Error message on exhaustion must say "timeout" not "retries exhausted"
- Error message on timeout must include the timeout duration in milliseconds
- All 38 test call sites that pass `retries` must be updated to use `timeout` only
- Worker script in concurrency tests must be updated (remove `retries: 5000`, rely on 30s default)
- `lockless-reads.test.ts` assertion must be updated (no longer references "retries exhausted")
- `src/database.ts` comment referencing "retries exhausted" (line 79) must be updated
- Build must succeed with no TypeScript errors

## Minimal Implementation

1. Remove `retries` from `FileLockOptions` interface (`src/file-lock.ts:22-23`)
2. Remove `DEFAULT_RETRIES` constant (`src/file-lock.ts:44`)
3. Remove `retries` destructuring from `acquireFileLock()` (`src/file-lock.ts:204`)
4. Rewrite retry loop: replace `for (let attempt = 0; attempt <= retries; attempt++)` with `while (Date.now() < deadline)`
5. Change `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_MS` from `10_000` to `30_000` (`src/file-lock.ts:46`)
6. Update error messages: replace "retries exhausted" with timeout-based message (`src/file-lock.ts:308-312`)
7. Update all 38 test call sites in `tests/file-lock.test.ts` to remove `retries` parameter
8. Update worker script (`tests/file-lock.test.ts:884`): remove `retries: 5000`, use `{ timeout: 30000 }` or rely on default
9. Update `tests/lockless-reads.test.ts:103` assertion
10. Update `src/database.ts:79` comment
11. Update the "retries-exhausted error" test (`tests/file-lock.test.ts:595`) to test timeout error instead
12. Run full build and test suite to verify

## Dependencies

- Implement exponential backoff with jitter (WL-0MM0WP7AO0ZUP8AV) must be completed first.

## Deliverables

- Updated `src/file-lock.ts` (interface change, loop restructure, error messages)
- Updated `tests/file-lock.test.ts` (38+ call site updates, worker script update, error test update)
- Updated `tests/lockless-reads.test.ts` (assertion update)
- Updated `src/database.ts` (comment update)

## Related Files

- `src/file-lock.ts:21-32` (FileLockOptions interface)
- `src/file-lock.ts:44` (DEFAULT_RETRIES constant)
- `src/file-lock.ts:203-313` (acquireFileLock retry loop)
- `tests/file-lock.test.ts` (38 call sites with retries parameter)
- `tests/lockless-reads.test.ts:103` (retries exhausted assertion)
- `src/database.ts:79` (comment)

data.description:
## Summary

The test `should not boost for completed or deleted downstream items` in `tests/database.test.ts` (line ~1128) is non-deterministic and fails intermittently in CI.

## Root Cause

The test creates two work items (`itemA` and `itemB`) in rapid succession without any delay between them. The test relies on `itemA` being older than `itemB` to win the age-based tie-breaker in `findNextWorkItem`. However, when both items are created within the same millisecond (common in slower CI environments), the `createdAt` timestamps are identical, and the final tie-breaker falls through to `id.localeCompare()` which depends on random ID characters, making the result non-deterministic.

The same issue exists in the second sub-case within the test where `olderB2` and `newerA2` are created without delay.

## Comparison with Similar Tests

Other tests in the same describe block (e.g., `should fall through to existing heuristics when blocks-high-priority scores are equal` at line 1074, and `should NOT boost an item that only blocks low/medium priority items` at line 1091) correctly use `async` and `await delay()` between creates when they depend on age ordering.

## Fix

1. Make the test `async`
2. Add `const delay = () => new Promise(resolve => setTimeout(resolve, 10))`
3. Add `await delay()` between the creates that need deterministic age ordering

## CI Evidence

- Failed in CI run: https://github.com/rgardler-msft/Worklog/actions/runs/22373435326/job/64757949193
- Passes locally (machine is fast enough that sequential creates get different millisecond timestamps)
- Passes on main branch locally but is subject to the same race condition

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Test uses `async` and `await delay()` between work item creates that depend on age ordering
- [ ] Test passes consistently in CI (no more flaky failures)
- [ ] All other tests continue to pass

# Intake Brief: Revamp documentation for quick user onboarding

> **Headline:** Overhaul Worklog's 612-line README into a concise getting-started guide and review all documentation for accuracy, enabling new users to onboard quickly.

**Work Item:** WL-0MM1AUM8I0F949VA
**Type:** Epic | **Priority:** Critical | **Assignee:** Map

## Problem Statement

Worklog's README is 612 lines and mixes getting-started instructions with architectural details, API references, and data format specifications. New human users cannot quickly determine how to install and start using Worklog. The broader documentation set (~25 markdown files) has grown organically and needs review for accuracy against the current implementation.

## Users

**Primary audience:** New human users discovering Worklog for the first time.

**User Stories:**

- As a new Worklog user, I want the README to give me only the essentials I need to install and start using Worklog, with clear links to detailed documentation, so that I can be productive quickly (aspirational goal: within 5 minutes).
- As a returning user, I want a documentation index in the README so I can quickly find the reference material I need.
- As a potential contributor, I want accurate documentation that reflects the current implementation so I can understand the codebase without reading source code.

## Success Criteria

1. **README.md is under 150 lines** and contains only: project description (1-2 sentences), installation, quick init, first work item walkthrough, and a documentation index linking to all other docs.
2. **Every non-AGENTS.md markdown file has been reviewed** for accuracy against the current implementation. Inaccuracies are corrected and noted in a comment on this work item.
3. **README contains a documentation index** with links and one-line descriptions for all relevant *.md files, including a link to AGENTS.md (which is not itself modified).
4. **3-5 tutorial proposals** are documented (title, target audience, topic outline) in a dedicated doc file (e.g., `docs/tutorials-proposals.md` or similar).
5. **No documentation files are deleted without record**; obsolete files (e.g., `issues/*.md`) are evaluated and either archived, removed with a note, or updated.
6. **All documentation changes are committed** and associated with this work item.

## Constraints

- **AGENTS.md is excluded from modification** but should be linked in the README documentation index.
- **Content relocated from the README** should go to existing documentation files where a natural home exists. New files may be created when no suitable home exists (e.g., for API reference or data format specifications).
- **The issues/ directory** (containing `0001-add-tag-matching-to-search.md`, `0002-add-limit-to-list.md`, `0003-investigate-flaky-tests.md`) must be evaluated for relevance before any action is taken.
- **No documentation should be deleted** without being noted in a comment on the work item.
- **The "5-minute onboarding" goal is aspirational**, not a literal measured acceptance criterion.

## Existing State

The project currently has:
- **README.md** (612 lines): 30+ sections covering installation, configuration, usage, API, data format, plugins, development, and git workflow.
- **13 top-level .md files** ranging from 6 lines (RELEASE_NOTES.md) to 711 lines (CLI.md).
- **Nested docs** under `docs/` (migrations, TUI, PRDs, benchmarks, validation), `examples/`, `tests/`, and `issues/`.
- **RELEASE_NOTES.md** is nearly empty (6 lines) and may need attention.
- No existing work items related to documentation were found in the worklog.

## Desired Change & Work Breakdown

This epic should be broken into the following child tasks, implemented roughly in this order:

1. **README revamp + content relocation** - Strip README.md to under 150 lines of essential getting-started content and a documentation index. Move detailed content (architecture, data format, API endpoints, configuration) to existing or new documentation files. These two concerns are tightly coupled and should be handled together.
2. **Documentation review** - Review each non-AGENTS.md markdown file for accuracy against the current implementation. May be further broken into sub-tasks per file or group. Correct inaccuracies and note changes in a comment on this work item.
3. **Issues directory evaluation** - Assess `issues/0001-*.md`, `issues/0002-*.md`, and `issues/0003-*.md` for continued relevance. Archive, remove with a note, or update.
4. **Tutorial proposals** - Draft 3-5 tutorial proposals (title, target audience, topic outline) in a dedicated doc file such as `docs/tutorial-proposals.md`.

## Related Work

- No duplicate or related work items found in the worklog.
- No prior documentation overhaul work items exist.

### Related work (automated report)

**Related work items:** None found. No existing open or closed work items in the worklog relate to documentation overhaul, README revamp, onboarding, tutorials, or quickstart improvements.

**Related repository documentation (all in scope for this epic):**

- `README.md` (612 lines) -- Primary project readme; the main target for this revamp. Contains installation, configuration, usage, API, data format, plugins, development, and git workflow sections.
- `QUICKSTART.md` (148 lines) -- Existing quick start guide. Overlaps with README getting-started content; should be reviewed for consistency and deduplication.
- `CLI.md` (711 lines) -- CLI command reference. Natural destination for CLI-related content relocated from the README.
- `EXAMPLES.md` (249 lines) -- Usage examples. May absorb example content currently in the README.
- `PLUGIN_GUIDE.md` (648 lines) -- Plugin development guide. README's plugin section content should reference this file.
- `TUI.md` (152 lines) -- Terminal UI documentation. Referenced from README.
- `GIT_WORKFLOW.md` (440 lines) -- Git workflow documentation. README's git workflow section should link here.
- `DATA_SYNCING.md` (142 lines) -- Data syncing guide. Related to git workflow content.
- `MULTI_PROJECT_GUIDE.md` (160 lines) -- Multi-project setup. Currently referenced in README.
- `IMPLEMENTATION_SUMMARY.md` (226 lines) -- Architecture/implementation details. Candidate destination for README's architectural content.
- `LOCAL_LLM.md` (307 lines) -- Local LLM integration guide.
- `RELEASE_NOTES.md` (6 lines) -- Nearly empty; needs evaluation during review.
- `MIGRATING_FROM_BEADS.md` (98 lines) -- Migration guide from legacy system.
- `docs/opencode-tui.md` -- OpenCode TUI integration documentation.
- `docs/migrations.md`, `docs/migrations/sort_index.md` -- Migration documentation.
- `docs/tui-ci.md` -- TUI CI testing documentation.
- `docs/prd/sort_order_PRD.md` -- Sort order PRD.
- `docs/benchmarks/sort_index_migration.md` -- Sort index migration benchmarks.
- `docs/validation/status-stage-inventory.md` -- Status/stage validation inventory.
- `examples/README.md` -- Examples directory readme.
- `tests/README.md`, `tests/cli/mock-bin/README.md` -- Test documentation.
- `issues/0001-add-tag-matching-to-search.md`, `issues/0002-add-limit-to-list.md`, `issues/0003-investigate-flaky-tests.md` -- Legacy issue files; need relevance evaluation.

## Risks and Assumptions

- **Risk: Scope creep.** Reviewing ~25 documentation files could expand into rewriting them. *Mitigation:* Record opportunities for deeper rewrites as separate work items linked to this epic rather than expanding its scope.
- **Risk: Accuracy verification requires deep codebase knowledge.** Verifying documentation against the current implementation may surface discrepancies that are unclear. *Mitigation:* Flag uncertain items for producer review rather than guessing.
- **Risk: Content relocation may break existing links.** Moving content out of the README could break bookmarks or external references. *Mitigation:* Check for cross-references before relocating content.
- **Risk: Near-empty RELEASE_NOTES.md.** At 6 lines, this file may confuse users expecting release history. *Mitigation:* Decide during the documentation review whether to populate, mark as placeholder, or remove with a note.
- **Assumption:** The current set of markdown files represents the full documentation surface. No documentation exists outside the repository.
- **Assumption:** AGENTS.md is maintained separately and should not be modified as part of this work.

## Suggested Next Step

Break this epic (WL-0MM1AUM8I0F949VA) into child work items as outlined in the Work Breakdown above, then plan and implement each in order: README revamp + content relocation first, then documentation review, issues evaluation, and tutorial proposals.

data.description:
## Summary

`wl next` uses a hierarchical depth-first traversal sorted by `sortIndex` to select the next work item. The traversal descends into completed items' subtrees, which causes orphaned open children buried under completed ancestors to be surfaced before higher-priority, shallower open items elsewhere in the tree.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Have a completed root-level epic with a low `sortIndex` (e.g. "CLI" epic, WL-0MKXJEVY01VKXR4C, sortIndex=300)
2. Under that epic, have a chain of completed items with one open leaf child (e.g. WL-0ML4TFYB019591VP, "Docs follow-up for dependency edges", priority=low, sortIndex=6400)
3. Have a root-level open non-epic item with a higher `sortIndex` than the completed epic but lower than the leaf (e.g. WL-0ML5YRMB11GQV4HR, "Slash Command Palette", priority=medium, sortIndex=2700)
4. Run `wl next`

## Expected Behaviour

`wl next` should recommend WL-0ML5YRMB11GQV4HR (or another active root-level item) since the completed subtree's work is done and the orphaned child is a low-priority leftover.

## Actual Behaviour

`wl next` recommends WL-0ML4TFYB019591VP (the low-priority orphaned child) because the DFS enters the completed "CLI" epic subtree first (sortIndex=300) and finds the open leaf before considering root-level items with higher sortIndex values.

## Root Cause

`selectBySortIndex` delegates to `orderBySortIndex` which calls `getAllWorkItemsOrderedByHierarchySortIndex()` in `persistent-store.ts` (lines 450-487). This performs a depth-first traversal sorting siblings by `sortIndex`. It does not check whether ancestor items are completed before descending, so a completed parent with a low `sortIndex` pulls its open descendants to the front of the ordering.

Relevant code:
- `src/database.ts`: `selectBySortIndex` (lines 971-979), `findNextWorkItemFromItems` (lines 996-1298)
- `src/persistent-store.ts`: `getAllWorkItemsOrderedByHierarchySortIndex()` (lines 450-487)

## Suggested Fix

Skip completed subtrees entirely during the hierarchical DFS traversal. Open children under completed parents should be treated as orphans and surfaced separately (e.g. treated as root-level items or placed at the end of the candidate list).

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `wl next` does not descend into subtrees where the parent item has status `completed` or `deleted`.
2. Open items whose parent is completed/deleted are surfaced as if they were root-level items (orphan promotion).
3. Orphaned items do not inherit traversal priority from their completed ancestors' `sortIndex`.
4. Existing tests pass; new tests cover the orphan scenario.
5. The fix is in the traversal/ordering logic, not in post-hoc filtering.
data.title: wl next descends into completed subtrees, surfacing low-priority orphaned children over higher-priority root items

data.description:
## Summary

`wl next` filters out all items with `issueType === 'epic'` from the candidate pool (line 1013 of `database.ts`). This means that epics with no children can never be surfaced by `wl next`, regardless of their priority. A critical epic with no children is completely invisible to the scheduling algorithm.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a critical epic with no children: `wl create -t "Critical epic" --priority critical --issue-type epic`
2. Run `wl next`

## Expected Behaviour

The critical epic should appear in the `wl next` recommendation, either directly or with a note that it needs to be broken down into children.

## Actual Behaviour

The epic is silently excluded. `wl next` recommends a lower-priority non-epic item instead. There is no warning that a critical epic exists but cannot be surfaced.

## Root Cause

In `src/database.ts`, `findNextWorkItemFromItems` (line 1013) unconditionally filters out epics:

```typescript
issueType !== 'epic'
```

The rationale is that epics should be worked on via their children, but this assumption breaks when an epic has no children yet.

## Suggested Fix

Include epics in the candidate list. Epics are a valid work item type and should be surfaced by `wl next` like any other item. The original exclusion was likely intended to prevent epics from being recommended when their children should be worked on instead, but this is better handled by the existing descent logic (which already prefers children over parents) rather than by blanket exclusion.

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `wl next` includes epics in the candidate pool regardless of whether they have children.
2. Epics with children continue to behave correctly: the algorithm descends into children as it does today.
3. Childless epics are surfaced according to normal priority/sortIndex rules.
4. Existing tests pass; new tests cover the childless epic scenario.
5. The epic type filter on line 1013 of `database.ts` is removed or replaced with logic that only skips epics when they have open children.
data.title: wl next excludes epics from candidate list, making childless critical epics invisible
